My tongue and my sword are both sharp, and my poetic lance reaches where the sword cannot. If I have little wealth, I am generous with it, and if my branch is bent by hardship, it is still praised. Neither wealth makes me forget my modesty and honor, nor do the blows of fate dull my file. My family is larger than other men's dependents, yet I endure on cold, pure water. I give whatever I find, and I say to the one who lights my fire on a windy night: Light it up! I am one who says 'Welcome' and 'Be at home' to the grieving visitor when he comes unannounced. When generosity calls me, I answer it, and I strike the gleaming, blazing white of lightning. I am sweet, though bitterness may touch me, and I am one who abandons what I have not been accustomed to. I drive my mounts onward through pain of foot, and I forsake the comfortable, well-made bed. I work the strong she-camel until I bring her back so exhausted that when her saddle is removed, she cannot be hobbled. I task her to travel through the entire night, heading to the door of Ibn Salma morning and evening. I found him an ocean overflowing with bounty, a generous man who grows in goodness the more it is mentioned. So do not be hasty, O Qays, and pause, for your utmost fate is to be met with every Indian sword — keen blades and spears in the hands of mighty men. Whenever you see them, O son of al-Khutaym, you will be struck dumb. Lions whose cubs defend their lair, thrusting with Khatti spears at every gathering. The Aws have tasted battle and been driven out, while you were hiding behind the women's curtains in every pursuit. So fondle the soft, fair-eyed beauties at your doors, and line your pretty eyes with kohl. A base mother weaned you away from glory, and a fire-steel that gives no spark whenever it is struck.
context: A masterful fakhr qasida combining self-praise, praise of a patron (Ibn Salma), and fierce satire against the Aws tribe and a man called Qays ibn al-Khutaym. Hassan portrays himself as the ideal Arab: generous despite poverty, brave in battle, eloquent in verse, and hospitable to all visitors. The poem ends with devastating insults against his Awsite rivals, mocking their cowardice and ignoble mothers. This reflects the pre-Islamic rivalry between the Khazraj and Aws tribes of Medina.

titleAr: ومن عاش منا عاش في عنجهية
titleEn: Whoever Among Us Lives, Lives in Proud Defiance

verses.sadr: ومن عاش منا عاش في عنجهية

translationEn: Whoever among us lives, lives in proud defiance, enduring the hardship of a wretched livelihood.
context: A single verse of tribal boasting, likely a fragment from a longer poem. Hassan declares that his people live with unbreakable pride even in the harshest conditions, a common theme in pre-Islamic and early Islamic poetry where endurance of hardship is a mark of nobility.

titleAr: لو كنت من هاشم ، أو من بني أسد ،
titleEn: If You Were from Hashim or Banu Asad

verses.sadr: لو كنت من هاشم ، أو من بني أسد ،
verses.ajuz: أو عبد شمس ، أو أصحاب اللوا الصيد

verses.sadr: أو من بني نوفل ، أو رهط مطلب
أو في الذؤابة من قوم ذوي حسب ،
verses.ajuz: لم تصبح اليوم نكسا ثاني الجيد

verses.sadr: أو من بني زهرة الأخيار قد علموا ،
verses.ajuz: أو من بني جمح البيض المناجيد

verses.sadr: أو في لذؤابة من تيم ، رضيت بهم ،
verses.ajuz: أو من بني خلف الخضر الجلاعيد

verses.sadr: يا آل تيم ألا ينهى سفيهكم ،

verses.ajuz: وطلحة بن عبيد الله ذو الجود

translationEn: If you were from Hashim, or from Banu Asad, or Abd Shams, or the lion-hearted bearers of the standard — or from Banu Nawfal, or the clan of Muttalib — by God, you would not have dared to threaten me. Or if you belonged to the elite of a people of noble lineage, you would not have become today a wretch with a bowed neck. Or from Banu Zuhra, the finest — they know — or from Banu Jumah, the fair-faced and illustrious, or from the cream of Taym, whom I would accept, or from Banu Khalaf, the verdant and mighty. O clan of Taym, will you not restrain your fool before the hurling of words like boulders? Were it not for the Messenger — for I shall never disobey him until my grave conceals me — and the Companion of the Cave, whom I shall always honor, and Talha ibn Ubaydullah, the generous one — you have been struck with a scandalous disgrace that leaves even the sound among the people as if dead. But I shall redirect my efforts and divert my words away from you with firm speech, not mere threats, toward al-Zibari, for baseness has sworn him allegiance, or toward the vilest of the sons of Abud.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the forelock, the elite or cream of a people
niche in a grave, burial place

context: A poem of satirical rebuke in which Hassan ibn Thabit enumerates the noble clans of Quraysh, implying that his target belongs to none of them. He restrains himself out of obedience to the Prophet Muhammad, respect for Abu Bakr (the Companion of the Cave), and Talha ibn Ubaydullah, redirecting his satire toward Ibn al-Zibari and the enemies of Islam.

titleEn: Has the Eye Not Known Its Sleeplessness?

verses.ajuz: ن : عاد له الشر من عادها

translationEn: Has the eye not known its sleeplessness, and the flowing of tears, and their exhaustion? Remembering the disheveled one after slumber, and the place where tent-ropes and pegs once lay — when the rumbling spring clouds pass over her courtyard, they pour their rain upon it. She rose to show you her flowing-haired beauty, bending under the weight that burdened her. Her face like the face of a spring gazelle traversing hillocks and slopes. The night drove him toward the thorny trees, fearing overcast skies and their biting cold. If I should die, do not marry one who forsakes his clan, one of their enviers, who considers praising their honor an insult out of folly, and who hates those who lead them. And if they reproach him once, their provisions are consumed in his night-plotting. One like me could be endured, yet I burden myself with what weighs me down. I shall give the clan what they sought from me and prove their threats false. I shall bear the burden if a debt afflicts them, and strike with the sword whoever plots against them. Yathrib knows that we are lions within it, shaking off their cloaks for battle, thrusting spears into the chests of warriors until we break their shafts — when they are roused and the forbearing become youthful in passion, and the people gather their hosts. The chaste women say to the righteous: evil returns upon whoever opposes her. We made bounty a shield against hardship, and in times of strain, we were their pillars.
context: A poem combining the nasib (amatory prelude) with tribal boasting (fakhr). Hassan recalls a beloved and a ruined campsite, then shifts to declaring his loyalty to his clan and their martial prowess in Yathrib (Medina). The poem showcases the Ansar's pride in their role as defenders, a recurring theme in Hassan's pre-Islamic and Islamic poetry.

titleEn: If You Reform, You Are Still an Abidi
translationEn: If you reform, you are still an Abidi, and the reform of the Abidi leads only to corruption. If you turn corrupt, then I have found you — as I well know — far from all rectitude. You find him just as he has always been, with his blunders and the foolishness of his heart. Error is plain in him, never beyond his reach, yet he is helpless before the paths of right guidance. Besides all this, a wretch stood up to insult me, like a pig wallowing in ashes. I bear witness that your mother was a harlot, and that your father is among the worst of mankind. I shall never cease satirizing the Abidi, as long as time endures and the caller calls. These verses have traveled and endure — the transmitters recite them in every valley. Cursed be Abid and his kinsmen, for their final destination is the worst of returns.
context: A fierce satirical poem directed at a man from the Abid clan. Hassan declares that neither reform nor corruption can change his target's fundamentally base nature. The poem exemplifies the hija tradition in which the poet's verses become a lasting weapon, transmitted by reciters across the land as an enduring mark of shame.

titleEn: Mongrels When Their Lineage Is Traced
translationEn: Mongrels when their lineage is traced — slaves, ruffians, with fire-sticks that yield no spark.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: mongrels, people of mixed or dishonorable descent

context: A single-verse satirical fragment that delivers a devastating tribal insult. Hassan packs three layers of abuse into one line: impure lineage (mongrels), servile status (slaves and ruffians), and incompetence (fire-sticks that fail to produce flame, metaphorically indicating cowardice and inability).

titleEn: We Are Not Drinkers Beneath a Cloak's Shade
translationEn: We are not drinkers beneath the shade of a cloak, counting out a he-goat and a lancet for the tavern. Rather, we are noble drinkers who, when they become merry, lavish the pure milk and the layered hump-fat. You would think them dead in the stillness of Halima, but if you come to them, you will praise their hospitality the next day. If you visit them, you find around their dwellings crushed musk and saffron scattered about. You see upon the folds of their carpets, spread out, sandals and fine goblets, and striped garments — and a dew-drenched youth hurrying along, his cheek pressed against brocade whose borders have been finely cut.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hump-fat of a camel, a delicacy

context: A poem of tribal pride (fakhr) in which Hassan contrasts the miserly drinkers who count their pennies at a tavern with his own people, who are generous hosts of legendary hospitality. The vivid descriptions of musk, saffron, fine carpets, and brocade paint a picture of aristocratic Ansari life in Yathrib.

titleEn: By God, I Do Not Know — Though I Ask

verses.ajuz: مهانة ، ذات الخيف ، ألأم ، أم سعد

verses.sadr: وكان أبو سرح عقيما فلم يكن
verses.ajuz: له ولد حتى دعيت له بعد

translationEn: By God, I do not know — though I inquire: is Muhana, she of the tent at al-Khayf, more base, or is it Saad? A mongrel slave, bright red of complexion, with taut sinews at the nape and tightly curled hair. Abu Sarh was barren and had no offspring until you were claimed as his son after the fact.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: mongrel, of mixed or base descent
sinew, tendon at the back of the neck

context: A short satirical poem attacking an opponent's lineage by questioning his parentage and physical features. Hassan suggests the target was not a legitimate son but was adopted or falsely attributed to Abu Sarh. The rhetorical question comparing two figures in baseness is a classic hija device.

titleEn: The Merciful Has Cursed a Host Led Astray

verses.ajuz: يبين فيه اللؤم من كان يهتدي

translationEn: The Merciful has cursed a host led by the pretender of Banu Shuja to war against Muhammad — an ill-omened, accursed wretch who was despised from of old, in whom baseness is plain to anyone with guidance. He led them into error until they crumbled, for his command was one of misguidance, not right direction. Then my Lord sent down His armies for the Prophet and supported him with victory in every battle. And verily the reward of God for every believer in His oneness is gardens of Paradise in which they shall dwell forever.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Merciful, one of God's names
Paradise, the highest garden in heaven
monotheist, one who professes God's oneness

context: An Islamic poem praising the Prophet Muhammad and condemning those who waged war against him. Hassan attributes the enemy's defeat to divine intervention, with God sending down His armies in support of the Prophet. The poem ends with a declaration of the reward awaiting believers in Paradise, reflecting Hassan's role as the poet-defender of early Islam.

titleEn: The Vile Son of Nabigha Claimed
translationEn: The vile son of Nabigha claimed that we would not place our honor beneath Muhammad's cause. Our wealth and our lives are shields for him — whoever does good shall be rewarded and praised. Truthful young men, like lions, fierce in battle — whoever meets them on the day of combat shall flee. The people of Ibn Nabigha are base and humiliated, unable to face even the whistle of the thunderer. Your father built them a house of shortcoming in disbelief and baseness — what a wretched house of origin!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to do, to perform (a good deed)
fierce in battle, kindlers of war

context: A poem defending the Ansar's dedication to the Prophet Muhammad against the taunts of Ibn Nabigha, who mocked their willingness to sacrifice for Islam. Hassan affirms that their wealth and lives are shields for Muhammad, praises the Ansar as fearless warriors, and returns the insult by condemning the enemy's lineage as one of disbelief and baseness.

titleEn: I Asked All of Quraysh — Their Worst Are Banu Abid
translationEn: I asked all of Quraysh, and their worst are Banu Abid — disfigured of face is the Abidi. When they sit in the middle of the assembly, they call out to each other like the croaking frogs of spring swarming about. Sayfi was never one to honor a pledge — the back of a fox, confounded at some of the watering-places.
context: A satirical attack on Banu Abid, whom Hassan declares the worst clan of Quraysh. The comparison of their speech in assembly to croaking frogs is a classic degrading image. The final verse attacks a man named Sayfi as untrustworthy, likened to the back of a fox that cannot find its way to water — a metaphor for cowardice and incompetence.

titleEn: If You Seek the Mightiest Chief

verses.sadr: ليس يرى من ضرب كبش بدا

translationEn: If you seek the mightiest chief among men, then go to Saad — Saad ibn Zayd. Take him as your army, for he is no coward who trembles and shakes. He sees no shame in striking the leading ram.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ram; metaphor for a leader in battle

context: A short rajaz poem praising Saad ibn Zayd, an Ansari chief known for his bravery. The rajaz meter, with its energetic rhythm, was often used for war chants and brief tributes. Hassan recommends Saad as a warrior who is both fearless and decisive, using the image of striking the leading ram to signify his willingness to confront enemy leaders head-on.

titleEn: I Am the Son of Khalda and al-Agharr
translationEn: I am the son of Khalda, and al-Agharr, and the two Maliks, and their helper — and the nobles of your people, if you send to the people of Yathrib one who inquires. I strove through the mansions of the outer quarters and the inner ones, exerting myself. You shall come to know, and you — when your knowledge is certain — shall praise them. They are the feeders when the years of drought settle and become still. The tops of the cooking vessels in the bowls of the poplar, frozen solid with fat.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Yathrib, the pre-Islamic name for Medina

context: A poem of tribal self-praise (fakhr) in which Hassan enumerates his ancestors and the noble lineages of his Khazraj clan. The poem celebrates the generosity of his people who feed others even in times of drought, a paramount virtue in Arabian society. The image of frozen fat on cooking pots indicates abundant food prepared even in the harshest conditions.

titleAr: فمن يك منهم ذا خلاق ، فإنه
titleEn: Whoever Among Them Has Good Character

verses.sadr: فمن يك منهم ذا خلاق ، فإنه

translationEn: Whoever among them possesses good character, his character shall restrain him from the injustice that has been confirmed against him.
context: A single-verse fragment functioning as a moral aphorism within a longer satirical context. Hassan suggests that only those among his opponents who possess genuine moral fiber will be restrained from committing further injustice. The implication is that most of them lack such character entirely.

titleAr: لعمرك ما تنفك عن طلب الخنا
titleEn: By Your Life, They Never Cease Seeking Disgrace

verses.sadr: لعمرك ما تنفك عن طلب الخنا
verses.ajuz: بنو زهرة الأنذال ما عاش واحد
إذا حضرت يوما من الدهر ماجد

translationEn: By your life, Banu Zuhra, those low-born wretches, never cease seeking disgrace as long as one of them lives. Their endeavors are base, their ancestors fall short of goodness toward the foreign neighbor — they muster only against him. Not one among them, when noble deeds and high honor present themselves on any day of time, proves to be glorious.
context: A satirical attack on Banu Zuhra, a clan of Quraysh whom Hassan denounces as perpetually ignoble. The three verses build a systematic indictment: they seek disgrace, their efforts are base and hostile toward strangers, and when the occasion calls for honor, not a single one of them rises to it.

titleAr: لقد كان قيس في اللئام مرددا ،
titleEn: Qays Was Tossed About Among the Base

verses.sadr: لقد كان قيس في اللئام مرددا ،

translationEn: Qays was tossed about among the base — the residue of a chick, the mine of baseness, a toiler. A wretched birth from Sumayya — she is a wretched Umayya whose glory is the worst of inherited possessions. In open fornication, publicly, from the most foolish among them — she surpassed them all in every arena. She brought forth Qays, the basest of people in origin, whenever the base origins of men are mentioned.
context: A savage genealogical attack on a man named Qays, whose mother Sumayya is slandered as having conceived him in fornication. Hassan traces his baseness through both parents, making him 'the mine of baseness.' This type of genealogical satire was particularly devastating in Arab tribal society where lineage determined social standing.

titleAr: وما طلعت شمس النهار ولا بدت
titleEn: No Sun Has Risen Nor Appeared Upon You with Glory

verses.sadr: وما طلعت شمس النهار ولا بدت
verses.ajuz: عليك ، بمجد ، يا ابن مقطوعة اليد
تبنى عليك اللؤم في كل مشهد

verses.sadr: إذا الدهر عف في تقادم عهده ،
verses.ajuz: على عار قوم ، كان لؤمك في غد

translationEn: No sun of day has risen nor appeared upon you with any glory, O son of the woman whose hand was cut off. Your father was a foundling, the basest of men in station — baseness is built upon you in every gathering. When time, in the passage of its ages, erases the shame of other peoples, your baseness shall remain fresh as tomorrow.
context: A fierce satirical poem in which Hassan denies his target any claim to honor. The reference to 'the woman whose hand was cut off' is a specific genealogical insult. The final verse is particularly devastating: while time erases other peoples' shame, this man's baseness remains eternally fresh.

titleEn: Whose Is the Child at the Edge of the Valley?

verses.ajuz: في الترب ملقى ، غير ذي مهد

translationEn: Whose is the child at the edge of the valley floor, cast in the dust, without a cradle? A fair-skinned, gentle woman of Abd Shams bore him — smooth of cheek. She rushes toward the crying, wailing — O Hind, you are hard in your anger! When she wishes, she calls for a censer to be lit for her with Indian aloe-wood. The resemblance of the boy was overcome, and the blackness of kinky, jet-black hair appeared. She gestured to the base woman, whose habit was to crack the knuckle-bones with a tough molar.
context: A poem that combines narrative and satire, telling the story of an abandoned child in the valley of Mecca. Hassan uses the imagery of an infant cast in the dust without a cradle to question the legitimacy of an enemy's birth. The details about Hind and the women of Abd Shams root this poem in the rivalries of early Islam.

titleEn: Whose Are These Abandoned Infants?

verses.sadr: فيهم صبي له أم لها نسب ،
verses.ajuz: في ذروة من ذرى الأحساب ، أياد

verses.sadr: تقول وهنا ، وقد جد المخاض بها :
verses.ajuz: يا ليتني كنت أرعى الشول للغادي

translationEn: Whose are these abandoned infants, cast away, squirming through the night in the sandy plain of Ajyad? She labored through the night — her midwives were none but the wild beasts and the jinn of the valley. Among them is a child whose mother has a lineage at the peak of noble pedigrees, from Iyad. She says weakly, as labor grips her: 'Would that I were herding the she-camels for the traveler!' They left him, noble of face, covered in dust, though her uncle and father are the lords of the assembly.
context: A companion poem to the previous one, continuing the theme of abandoned infants in Mecca's valleys. Hassan paints a vivid and pathetic scene of a woman of noble lineage giving birth alone in the wilderness with only wild beasts and jinn as attendants. The irony that her family are lords of the assembly while she is abandoned heightens the satirical force.

titleAr: لقد علم الأقوام أن ابن هاشم
titleEn: The People Know That the Son of Hashim

verses.sadr: لقد علم الأقوام أن ابن هاشم
verses.ajuz: هو الغصن ذو الأفنان لا الواحد الوغد
فدونك فالصق مثل ما لصق القرد

verses.sadr: وإن سنام المجد من آل هاشم

verses.ajuz: ولكن هجين ليس يورى له زند

verses.sadr: وأنت زنيم نيط في آل هاشم ،
verses.ajuz: كما نيط خلف الراكب القدح الفرد

translationEn: The people know that the son of Hashim is the branch with many boughs, not the solitary wretch. You have no recognized origin among them, so go ahead and cling to them the way a monkey clings. The summit of glory among the family of Hashim belongs to the sons of the daughter of Makhzum, while your father is a slave. The mixed clans of Zuhra never produced a generous man from among you, nor did glory ever come near your old women. You are not like al-Abbas, nor like his maternal cousin — rather, you are a mongrel whose fire-stick yields no spark. You are an appendage attached to the family of Hashim, as a single arrow is hung behind the rider. A man whose mother was Sumayya, and the dark-skinned one — overcome when hardship reaches its peak.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: appendage, one attached to a tribe not his own
fire-stick, the stick rubbed to produce fire

context: A poem praising the lineage of Banu Hashim — the Prophet Muhammad's clan — while attacking an opponent who falsely claims connection to them. Hassan uses vivid metaphors: the opponent clings like a monkey, is hung on like a spare arrow, and his fire-stick yields no spark. The poem contrasts genuine Hashemite nobility with the pretender's servile origins.

titleEn: May God Have Mercy on Nafi ibn Budayl
translationEn: May God have mercy on Nafi ibn Budayl — the mercy sought by one who yearns for the reward of holy struggle. He was patient, truthful in speech — when the people spoke at length, he spoke the word of rectitude. I was, before meeting him, ignorant of his worth, but now his loss has struck my heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: holy struggle, striving in God's path

context: An elegy (ritha) for Nafi ibn Budayl, a companion who fell in battle. Hassan praises his patience, truthfulness, and wisdom, confessing that he only came to truly appreciate the man after his death. The poem reflects the deep bonds formed among the early Muslim community through shared struggle.

titleAr: غدا أهل حضني ذي المجاز بسحرة
titleEn: The People of the Slopes of Dhu al-Majaz Set Out at Dawn

verses.sadr: غدا أهل حضني ذي المجاز بسحرة
verses.ajuz: وجار ابن حرب بالمحصب ما يغدو
وأصبحت رخوا ما تخب وما تعدو

translationEn: The people of the slopes of Dhu al-Majaz set out at dawn, while the dependent of Ibn Harb at al-Muhassab does not stir. Hisham ibn al-Walid clothed you with his garments — wear them out and replace them with new ones afterward. He fulfilled his desire from him and departed in the morning, while you became slack, neither trotting nor galloping. If the elders who witnessed Badr had been present, the backs of horses would have been drenched with freshly drawn rose-red blood. The flatulent donkey's reins did not protect it, and Hind did not prevent the disgrace of her father.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to replace, to get a substitute
Badr, site of the first major Islamic battle

context: A satirical poem mocking an enemy associated with the Quraysh leadership. Hassan references the Battle of Badr, suggesting that had the elders of Quraysh who died there been alive, there would have been real fighting. The mention of Hind (bint Utba, wife of Abu Sufyan) connects this to the rivalry between the Muslims and the Meccan aristocracy.

titleAr: شق له من اسمه كي يجله ،
titleEn: He Derived for Him from His Name to Honor Him

verses.sadr: شق له من اسمه كي يجله ،

verses.ajuz: من الرسل والأوثان في الأرض تعبد
بذلك ما عمرت في الناس أشهد

verses.sadr: تعاليت رب الناس عن قول من دعا

translationEn: He derived for him from His own name to honor him: the Lord of the Throne is Mahmud (Praised), and this one is Muhammad (Praised One). A prophet who came to us after despair and an interval between messengers, while idols were worshipped upon the earth. He became a shining lamp and a guide, gleaming as a polished Indian sword gleams. He warned us of the Fire and gave glad tidings of Paradise, and taught us Islam — so God we praise. You are the God of truth, my Lord and Creator — to that I bear witness as long as I live among people. Exalted are You, Lord of mankind, above the words of those who call upon any god besides You — You are the Most High and Most Glorious. Yours is all creation and all bounty and all command — from You we seek guidance, and You alone we worship. For the reward of God for every believer in His oneness is gardens of Paradise in which he shall dwell forever.
context: One of Hassan's most celebrated Islamic poems, praising the Prophet Muhammad by connecting his name to God's attribute al-Mahmud (the Praised). The poem describes Muhammad as a light that came after a long interval without prophets, then transitions into a declaration of pure monotheism echoing Quranic language. The verse 'from You we seek guidance and You alone we worship' recalls Surat al-Fatiha.

titleEn: Tell the Destitute That Goodness Has Left Them

verses.sadr: من ذا الذي عنده رحلي ، وراحلتي ،
verses.ajuz: ورزق أهلي ، إذا لم يؤنسوا المطرا

verses.sadr: أم من نعاتب لا نخشى جنادعه ،
verses.ajuz: إذا اللسان عتا في القول ، أو عثرا

verses.sadr: لم يترك الله منا بعده أحدا ،
verses.ajuz: ولم يعش بعده أنثى ، ولا ذكرا
وكان أمرا من امر الله قد قدرا

translationEn: Tell the destitute that goodness has left them — it departed with the Prophet from them at dawn. Who now holds my saddle and my riding-camel, and the provision of my family when they see no rain? Or whom can we reproach without fearing his retort, when the tongue grows bold in speech or stumbles? He was the light, he was the radiance we followed — after God — and he was our hearing and our sight. Would that on the day they laid him in his grave-niche, concealed him, and cast the earth-clods over him, God had not left a single one of us after him, and no woman or man had lived beyond him. The necks of all Banu al-Najjar were bowed in humility, for it was a matter decreed by God.
context: One of Hassan's most moving elegies, composed upon the death of the Prophet Muhammad in 632 CE. The poem expresses the devastating sense of loss felt by the Muslim community. Hassan declares that the Prophet was their light, their hearing and sight after God, and wishes that no one had survived him. The bowing of Banu al-Najjar's necks — Hassan's own clan — reflects the collective grief of the Ansar.

titleEn: You Were the Pupil of My Eye
translationEn: You were the pupil of my eye, and now my eye has gone blind over you. Let whoever wishes die after you, for it was only your death that I feared.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: eye, the seeing part of the eye
to fear, to be on guard against

context: A brief but devastating elegy, likely for the Prophet Muhammad, though it has also been attributed to laments for other beloved figures. The metaphor of the departed being 'the pupil of my eye' — and the eye going blind upon their loss — is one of the most quoted couplets in Arabic elegy. The second verse's stark declaration that all other deaths are now insignificant captures the totality of grief.

titleEn: Beware — I Have Grown Old and Trials Overcame Me
translationEn: Beware of me — I have grown old and calamities overcame me in my concern for you as old age whitened my hair. You made me a target for the base — every one of them shoots his baseness at me, whether reaching the mark or falling short. Until their gums bleed and a black stain grows upon them, its roots like the bitter colocynth plant. Did you serve them my honor, mocking me heedlessly? May your mother be bereaved — give them to devour something other than my honor! A target at which the archers take turns, as though they were shooting at a boulder on the slope of al-Mashar.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to give as slaughter, to give over
al-Mashar, a sacred site near Muzdalifa

context: A poem of reproach and self-defense from Hassan's old age. He addresses someone who exposed him to the attacks of base men, making his honor a target for all. Despite his advancing years, Hassan's poetic venom remains potent, and he demands that his honor be spared while threatening his attackers with satirical retaliation.

titleEn: Al-Nadira, the Lady of the Curtained Chamber

verses.ajuz: ما رد طرف العين ذو شفر
من غير ما نسب ولا صهر

verses.sadr: لو كنت لا تهوين لم تردي ،
verses.ajuz: أو كنت ، ما تلوين في وكر

translationEn: Al-Nadira, the lady of the curtained chamber, traveled to you by night, though she was not wont to travel. I stopped in the desert to ask her: how did you find the way to the traveler's camp? The white camels had loosened their halters from the weakness they showed, and their flaws had overtaken their beauty from the emaciation that afflicted them. We used to, when the midday heat settled upon us, steal through it on swift she-camels with crooked necks — winding, bleating, carrying us aloft, excused from the hard driving and the shouting. Facing every blazing noon, they breathe in rings of brass. And her resting-place at every halt was like the roosting-spot of the dark, dusty sandgrouse. A chameleon rose upon a branch and confronted us, or was about to swish its tail. And bearing the long day when its grasshoppers chirred from the heat of noon. And the dark night I travel through with the company across the barren, desolate waste, where the echo mourns its brother as the bereaved mourns the companion of the grave. Its darkness forms a barrier until it becomes oppressive to the night-traveler. I have shown the riders their families and guided them through dusty, trackless wastes. I offered my riding-mount and was generous with it to them in hardship and in ease. Misfortunes do not crush me, nor does my chest grow narrow with any need. My least word exhausts whoever tries to match me — by your life, I am no idle talker. I honor whoever honors me, and my claw falls upon the one who hides enmity. I do not steal what other poets have uttered — rather their poetry does not match mine. My lineage forbids that to me, and a speech like the cutting-edges of rock. And my brother from among the perceptive jinn, who weaves words in the finest ink. O Nadira, what is between you and me is rupture, and the estrangement you have caused. Be generous, for generosity is a virtue, and requite the sword with some of what it cuts. I swore I would never forget you, as long as the eyelid returns the eye's glance. I swore I would never forget your conversation, so long as the dissolute one remembers the pleasure of wine. You are more beautiful, when you emerged for us on the day of departure in the courtyard of the palace, than a pearl for which kings pay dearly — one nurtured by the swirling depths of the sea. With rounded calves, resembling two papyrus reeds in a deep, overflowing pool. She grows as her noble stock grows, in the abode of the people of glory and pride. Longing overtakes me and I remember her, without any tie of kinship or marriage — like the memory of a thirsty man who has no water on the summit of a steep, rugged peak. She would sit beside me, but shyness and the constraint of modesty prevented me. If you did not love, you would not have come; or if you were, you would not be sheltering in a nest. I would surely have come to him seeking him — so preserve your modesty, and accept my excuse. Tell al-Nadira, if you encounter her: the generous one is not the companion of scarcity. My people are Banu al-Najjar — their support is gracious, and they are ever present to aid me. Death stands before me — I am not oppressed — and the people of noble deeds from Banu Amr, a deep-rooted stronghold whose fortresses of honor have been ours since ancient times.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: echo (believed to be a bird in pre-Islamic lore)

context: One of Hassan's longest and most ambitious poems, combining the traditional pre-Islamic qasida structure with personal lyric and tribal pride. It opens with the mysterious nocturnal visit of al-Nadira, transitions through a desert journey passage (rahil) with vivid descriptions of camels and landscape, moves into poetic self-assertion and boasts of originality, and concludes with a love passage and a declaration of tribal strength. The mention of a jinn companion who aids his poetry reflects the pre-Islamic belief that great poets were inspired by spirits.

titleEn: A Harsh Night in Yathrib Revisited Me

verses.ajuz: وهم ، إذا ما نوم الناس ، مسهر
وكم من كريم يبتلى ، ثم يصبر

verses.sadr: أغر كلون البدر من آل هاشم ،
وكنا نرى في جعفر من محمد
فما زال في الإسلام من آل هاشم
بهم تكشف اللأواء في كل مأزق
verses.ajuz: عماس ، إذا ما ضاق بالقوم مصدر
عقيل ، وماء العود من حيث يعصر

translationEn: A harsh night in Yathrib revisited me — a sorrow that keeps me sleepless when others sleep. The memory of a beloved stirred a flowing tear, and the causes of weeping are remembrance. What a trial — the loss of a beloved is an affliction, yet many a noble man is tested and then endures. I saw the best of the believers go one after another to their fates, while I was left among those held back. May God not distance the slain who fell in succession at Mu'tah — among them Ja'far of the Two Wings, and Zayd, and Abdullah — when they fell together while the causes of death hovered about them. On the morning they went forth leading the believers, led to death by one of auspicious nature, radiant — brilliant as the color of the full moon, from the family of Hashim, refusing when asked to submit to injustice, bold. He fought with the lance until he died unpropped by any pillow, in a battle-ground where spears shattered. His reward became that of the martyrs — gardens and dense, green orchards. We used to see in Ja'far something of Muhammad — loyalty, and firm command when he commanded. There has never ceased to be among the family of Hashim pillars of honor unassailable and cause for pride. They are the mountain of Islam, and the people around them are rubble beside a peak that awes and overwhelms. Through them affliction is lifted in every dire strait when the way out narrows for the people. They are God's allies, upon whom He sent down His judgment, and among them is this purified Book. Noble lords among them are Ja'far and his maternal brother Ali, and among them is Ahmad, the Chosen One, and Hamza, and al-Abbas, and among them Aqil — the essence of the wood is from where it is pressed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to revisit, to come upon at night
the one with two wings (title of Ja'far)
Mu'tah, site of a battle in 629 CE

context: A major elegy commemorating the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE), where three Muslim commanders fell in succession: Zayd ibn Haritha, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib (the Prophet's cousin, known as 'the one with two wings'), and Abdullah ibn Rawaha. Hassan mourns them while praising the entire Hashemite family — including Ali, Hamza, and al-Abbas — as the pillars of Islam. The poem is both a personal lament and a political statement about the centrality of the Prophet's family.

titleEn: I Was Told That Abu Mundhir
translationEn: I was told that Abu Mundhir rivals you for the title of al-Harith the Younger. The back of your neck is more handsome than his face, and your mother is better than al-Mundhir himself. Your left hand in its difficulty is like his right hand upon the impoverished. How vast is the distance between you in generosity, in valor, in goodness, and in appearance!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the back of the neck, the nape

context: A poem praising al-Harith the Younger by mocking Abu Mundhir's pretension to rival him. Hassan employs a series of devastating comparisons: the back of al-Harith's neck surpasses Abu Mundhir's face, and al-Harith's left hand gives more generously than Abu Mundhir's right. The poem is both panegyric and satire, praising one by diminishing the other.

titleEn: O Eye, Pour Forth Your Scanty Tears

verses.sadr: قد أتانا من قتلهم ما كفانا ،

translationEn: O eye, pour forth your scanty tears, and remember in times of ease the people of the graves. Remember Mu'tah and what befell there, on the day they turned away in the onslaught of the raid — when they departed and left behind Zayd, who was the finest refuge for the destitute and the captive. The beloved of the best of all creation altogether — the master of mankind, whose love resides in the hearts. That is Ahmad, beside whom there is none other — in him is both my grief and my joy. Then weep for the Khazraji with your tears — a chief who was there, no rare visitor. Enough news of their killing has reached us, so we spend the night in sorrow, not in joy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Mu'tah, site of a major battle

context: Another elegy for the fallen at the Battle of Mu'tah, focusing on Zayd ibn Haritha, the Prophet's adopted son and the first of the three commanders to fall. Hassan calls on the eye to weep and remember, linking the martyrs to the Prophet's love. The mention of a Khazraji chief connects the poem to Hassan's own Ansari community. The final verse's stark admission of overwhelming grief reflects the magnitude of the losses.

titleAr: أوفت بنو عمرو بن عوف نذرها ،
titleEn: Banu Amr ibn Awf Fulfilled Their Vow

verses.sadr: أوفت بنو عمرو بن عوف نذرها ،
إن لم تروا مددا له وكتيبة
فعدمت ما ولد ابن عمرو منذر
أبلغ بني بكر ، إذا ما جئتهم ،
من خير خندف كلها ، بعد الذي
حاشا بني عمرو بن عوف إنهم

translationEn: Banu Amr ibn Awf fulfilled their vow, while Banu al-Najjar were stained with treachery. They failed on the day of zealous duty — they are not, in that regard, among the righteous. They forgot Muhammad's testament concerning his son-in-law and exchanged honor for an abode of ruin. Did you leave him alone in a place of loss, visited by the rabble of the cities? Heartsick, calling upon his absent supporters — woe to you, O company of the Ansar! Would that you had honored your covenants then, and ransomed him with your hearing and sight! His neighbors, the groaners around his houses, betrayed him — by the Lord of the House of curtains! If you do not send him reinforcements and a battalion leading the vanguard of a mighty host — then I renounce whatever al-Mundhir son of Amr has fathered, until they make their forces halt at Sirar. By God, they will never keep faith after their leader, even if they swore by the saddle-cloth of a donkey. Convey to Banu Bakr, when you reach them, blame — what wretched places for marriage alliances! They betrayed a man white as the crescent moon, blameless, whose sword-blades were tempered with a flint of fire. From the best of all Khindif, after him whom God aided to triumph over the disbelievers. You obeyed the enemy concerning him, though you could have been, had you wished, in a place apart and safe. Let the rumor-mongers not suppose that the blood of the people of the house will go unavenged. Excepted are Banu Amr ibn Awf — their resting-places are written among the righteous.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Khindif, a major Arab tribal confederation

context: A politically charged poem addressing the crisis of succession after the Prophet's death. Hassan reproaches the Ansar for failing to uphold the Prophet's testament regarding his son-in-law (Ali ibn Abi Talib), praising Banu Amr ibn Awf for their loyalty while condemning Banu al-Najjar — his own clan — for their betrayal. This remarkable poem shows Hassan turning his satirical powers against his own people for what he sees as a failure of duty, making it one of the most significant political poems of early Islam.

titleAr: وأفلت يوم الروع أوس بن خالد ،
titleEn: Aws ibn Khalid Escaped on the Day of Terror

verses.sadr: وأفلت يوم الروع أوس بن خالد ،

translationEn: Aws ibn Khalid escaped on the day of terror, spewing blood like a nosebleed, his chest dyed crimson.
context: A single-verse satirical fragment mocking Aws ibn Khalid for fleeing from battle. The vivid image of him spewing blood with his chest dyed crimson suggests he was wounded but fled rather than standing and fighting. In Arabian martial culture, fleeing from battle was the ultimate disgrace, and Hassan immortalizes this shame in a single devastating line.

titleEn: She Asks about a Noble Champion

verses.sadr: دعاه إله الخلق ذو العرش دعوة
فوالله لا أنساك ما هبت الصبا ،
على أسد الله الذي كان مدرها ،
ألا ليت شلوي ، يوم ذاك ، وأعظمي

verses.ajuz: جزى الله خيرا من أخ ونصير

translationEn: She asks about a noble, pure-blooded champion — bold in battle, a raider at dawn, courageous. A trusted brother who stirs with generosity and kindness, far-reaching in resolve, patient in calamities. I said to her: martyrdom is a repose and the pleasure of a forgiving Lord. For your father, the virtuous Hamza — know this — was the minister of the Messenger of God, the best of ministers. The Creator of all, Lord of the Throne, called him to a Garden in which He is pleased, and to joy. That is what we had hoped and wished for Hamza on the Day of Gathering — the finest destination. By God, I shall never forget you as long as the east wind blows, and I shall weep for you in my presence and on my journeys — for the Lion of God who was their champion, warding off from Islam every disbeliever. Would that my remains and my bones on that day had been scattered to hyenas and vultures. I say, as the herald of death proclaimed his passing: may God reward him well — a brother and a supporter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: raider, one who attacks at dawn

context: An elegy for Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle, who was martyred at the Battle of Uhud (625 CE). Hassan addresses a woman inquiring about Hamza's fate, consoles her with the promise of paradise, and vows never to forget the 'Lion of God' who defended Islam. This is one of the most celebrated elegies from the early Islamic period.

titleAr: ألا ليت شعري هل اتى أهل مكة
titleEn: Has the News Reached the People of Mecca

verses.sadr: ألا ليت شعري هل اتى أهل مكة
وكم قد قتلنا من كريم مرزإ ،
verses.ajuz: له حسب في قومه نابه الذكر

translationEn: Would that I knew whether the news has reached the people of Mecca — our annihilation of the disbelievers in the hour of hardship. We slew the chieftains of the people at their very camps, and they returned with nothing but a back-breaking blow. We killed Abu Jahl and Utba before him, and Shayba who fell upon his hands and chest. How many a noble, grievously mourned man we slew, one of high standing and illustrious fame among his people. We left them to the prowling beasts that visit them, and they burn in a fire whose depths blaze fiercely — on account of their disbelief in God while the religion stands firm, and they gained no advantage in their blood-feud against us. By your life, the horsemen of Malik and their partisans did not falter on the day we met at Badr.
context: A boastful poem celebrating the Muslim victory at the Battle of Badr (624 CE), the first major military triumph of Islam. Hassan names the slain Qurayshi leaders — Abu Jahl, Utba, and Shayba — and challenges Mecca to acknowledge the defeat. The poem served as propaganda directed at the Quraysh to demoralize them after their devastating loss.

titleEn: Weep for the Slain of Ma'una

verses.ajuz: من أبيض ماجد من سر عمرو

translationEn: Weep for the slain of Ma'una — let your tears pour forth freely, not sparingly. Weep for the horsemen of the Messenger on the morning they met their fates, and destiny met them by decree. Annihilation struck them through the treachery of a people whose covenant they broke with betrayal. Alas for Mundhir when he turned away and rushed to his death with patience. How many a shining, glorious man from the clan of Amr was struck down that morning.
context: An elegy for the companions martyred at Bi'r Ma'una (625 CE), when a delegation of around seventy Muslim missionaries was treacherously ambushed. Hassan mourns the betrayal and singles out al-Mundhir ibn Amr for his courageous acceptance of death. The incident deeply affected the early Muslim community.

titleEn: The Son of Wudd Lies Fallen

verses.ajuz: يا عمرو ، أو لجسيم أمر منكر

translationEn: The young warrior son of Wudd now lies fallen on the southern slope of Sal', his blood unavenged. You found our swords drawn and famous, and you found our steeds did not fall short. At Badr you met a band that struck you with blows unlike the strikes of the weary. Now you are no longer called upon for any great occasion, O Amr, nor for any grave and terrible affair.
context: A satirical poem mocking Amr ibn Abd Wudd, the famed Qurayshi champion who was slain by Ali ibn Abi Talib during the Battle of the Trench (627 CE). Hassan taunts the fallen warrior, reminding him that his swords and horses proved no match for the Muslims, and that he will never again be summoned for battle.

titleAr: لست إلى عمرو ، ولا المرء منذر ،
titleEn: I Am Not Beholden to Amr Nor to Mundhir

verses.sadr: لست إلى عمرو ، ولا المرء منذر ،
verses.ajuz: إذا ما مطايا القوم أصبحن ضمرا

verses.sadr: ولا تك كالشاة التي كان حتفها

verses.ajuz: ولم يخشه ، سهما من النبل مضمرا

translationEn: I owe nothing to Amr nor to Mundhir when the riding camels of the people grow lean from travel. Were it not for Abu Wahb, poems would have passed over the heights of al-Barqa', spent and exhausted. Whoever directs poems against us is like one who sends dates as merchandise to the people of Khaybar. Do not be like the drowsy man who dreams he is in the city of Chosroes or the city of Caesar. And do not be like the ewe whose death came from digging with her own forelegs, unsatisfied with her burrow. Nor be like the howling one who bared his chest, not fearing a concealed arrow among the shafts. Do you boast of wearing linen, when even the Nabataeans wear fine cloaks, though short?
context: A boastful poem with satirical elements, in which Hassan asserts his poetic independence and warns an adversary against challenging him. The famous proverb-like verse about sending dates to Khaybar (a date-producing region) means attempting something futile. The poem weaves together animal parables and cultural references to Persian and Roman courts.

titleEn: God Curse the Dwelling of Kawtha
translationEn: May God curse the dwelling in the belly of Kawtha and strike it with poverty and destitution. I do not mean the Kawtha of Iraq, but rather the worst of quarters — the quarter of Abd al-Dar. It has gathered baseness and folly together and contained all of that settled within it. And whenever Quraysh aspires to glory, it leaves that clan behind in their abode in humiliation.
context: A satirical poem targeting the Banu Abd al-Dar, a clan of the Quraysh. Hassan deliberately plays on the name Kawtha, clarifying he means not the place in Iraq but the neighborhood of this particular clan, which he accuses of embodying all of Quraysh's worst qualities. This kind of clan-targeted satire was a powerful weapon in tribal Arab society.

titleEn: I Asked Quraysh and They Did Not Lie
translationEn: I asked Quraysh and they did not lie — so ask Wahwah and Abu Amir: what is Hassan's standing among his people? The questioner is not like the one who already knows. If they spoke truly, they would tell you that we are the possessors of overwhelming nobility. We are firebrands in battle, turning back the edge of every arrogant transgressor. I inherited noble deeds and the giving of wealth, and glory from generation to generation — the bearing of blood-money, the freeing of captives, and honor rooted in illustrious lineage. With every sturdy lance of solid joints, and every gleaming, sharp white blade, and a coat of mail white as a river, ample, that folds with its length over the one who spreads it — with these we pluck out the souls of armored men when dawn lights up for the beholder. When people race toward their goals, you will find al-Zibari trailing at the rear. The tongue-tied man in the assembly cannot be compared to the seasoned, eloquent poet. How can a silenced mute oppose me, one who traces his lineage to an obscure, ruined stock?
context: A boastful poem (fakhriyya) in which Hassan defends his tribal honor and poetic prowess. He challenges the Quraysh to confirm his noble lineage among the Khazraj, listing inherited virtues — generosity, ransoming captives, and martial valor. The poem ends with a direct taunt at Ibn al-Zibari, his Qurayshi poetic rival, whom he dismisses as tongue-tied and obscure.

titleEn: Sorrows Have Increased and Tears Flow

verses.ajuz: هيفاء ، لا دنس فيها ولا خور

verses.sadr: دع عنك شعثاء ، إذ كانت مودتها
وأت الرسول فقل يا خير مؤتمن

verses.ajuz: أمام قوم هم آووا ، وهم نصروا

verses.sadr: والناس ألب علينا ، ثم ليس لنا ،
وكم رددنا ببدر ، دون ما طلبوا ،
ونحن جندك يوم النعف من أحد ،
فما ونينا ، وما خمنا ، وما خبروا
verses.ajuz: منا عثارا ، وجل القوم قد عثروا

translationEn: Sorrows have increased, and the water of my eye pours down — streaming when a tear overwhelms it like pearls. Longing for Sha'tha', when Sha'tha' was plump and slender-waisted, free of any taint or weakness. Leave Sha'tha' behind, for her affection was scant, and the worst bond is one that is meager. Come instead to the Messenger and say: O best of trustees for the believers when mankind is judged. Why is Sulaym summoned, being distant, ahead of a people who gave shelter and who gave victory? God named them the Ansar for their support of the religion of guidance while the blaze of war raged on. They fought in the path of God and bore calamities without faltering or growing weary. All people were united against us, and we had nothing but swords and the tips of spears as armor. The proximity of war does not make our assembly cower; when its fire blazes, we are its kindling. How many times at Badr did we repel what the hypocrites sought, and upon us victory was sent down. We were your soldiers on the ridge of Uhud, when the confederates of Mudar rallied in arrogance. We neither weakened nor faltered, nor did they ever find a stumble in us, though most of the people had stumbled.
context: A praise poem addressed to the Prophet Muhammad, advocating for the rights of the Ansar (the Medinan supporters of Islam). Hassan opens with a conventional love prelude about Sha'tha' before pivoting to protest the precedence given to the tribe of Sulaym over the Ansar, who were first to shelter the Prophet. He recounts their sacrifices at Badr and Uhud as proof of their primacy.

titleAr: على حين أن قالتا لأيمن أمه :
titleEn: When His Mother Said to Ayman

verses.sadr: على حين أن قالتا لأيمن أمه :
فلولا الذي قد كان من شأن مهره ،

verses.ajuz: وما كان منه عنده غير أيسر

translationEn: At the time when his mother said to Ayman: you were a coward and did not join the horsemen of Khaybar — but Ayman was not a coward; rather, his colt was harmed by drinking fermented bran-mash. Were it not for what befell his colt, he would have fought there as a horseman, not left-handed in valor. But the condition of his colt held him back, and what happened was beyond his easy control.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Khaybar — a Jewish stronghold near Medina

context: A short defense poem for Ayman, whose mother had accused him of cowardice for missing the Battle of Khaybar (628 CE). Hassan explains that Ayman's horse had fallen ill from drinking fermented mash, preventing him from riding to battle — thus exonerating him from the charge of cowardice. Such poetic defenses were important for maintaining tribal honor.

titleEn: Quraysh Was an Egg That Cracked Open
translationEn: Quraysh was an egg that cracked open, and the pure yolk belongs to Abd al-Dar. My Lord's decree singled them out with the honor of veiling the House of God with its curtains. They are the people of noble deeds and eminence, of the assembly-hall, and the keepers of the mighty caravan. They led Quraysh in every gathering and with valor when the long spears sway.
context: A praise poem for the Banu Abd al-Dar, the Qurayshi clan entrusted with the custodianship (hijaba) of the Kaaba, including the privilege of draping it with curtains. Hassan employs the metaphor of an egg cracking to reveal its pure yolk — the best of Quraysh. This poem likely dates from before or during the early Islamic period when tribal alliances were being cultivated.

titleAr: إني لأعجب من قول غررت به ،
titleEn: I Marvel at a Speech That Deceived You

verses.sadr: إني لأعجب من قول غررت به ،
لو تسمع العصم ، من صم الجبال ، به ،

verses.ajuz: تبغ السراب ، فلا عين ولا أثر

verses.sadr: لا ينبت العشب عن برق وراعدة
verses.ajuz: غراء ، ليس لها سيل ولا مطر

translationEn: I marvel at a speech that deceived you — sweet, drawing both ear and eye toward it. Were the mountain goats on the deaf peaks to hear it, even those sure-footed ones would slide down from the towering heights. Like wine and honey flowing on its surface, yet its depths have neither taste nor substance. Like a mirage resembling a pool, but if you seek that mirage, there is neither spring nor trace. Grass does not sprout from lightning and a dazzling thundercloud that brings neither torrent nor rain.
context: A satirical poem exposing the emptiness behind an adversary's eloquent but hollow speech. Hassan constructs a series of vivid similes — honey without substance, a mirage without water, thunder without rain — to dismantle the pretensions of a rival poet or orator. The poem is a masterclass in the Arabic poetic tradition of using natural imagery for satirical purposes.

titleEn: Sulaym Has Raged in Ignorant Folly

verses.ajuz: كلابا لها في الدار ، عال هريرها

translationEn: Sulaym has raged in ignorant folly, and their loose minds have gone astray with many a stumble. Their endeavors are base, their talk is lies, and their wealth is scant when their hawks are mourned. They have the sense of women and the worst of customs, their generosity meager when their seas are sought. If you visit them as a guest, you will find around their tents dogs whose growling fills the dwelling.
context: A sharp satirical poem attacking the tribe of Sulaym, with whom the Ansar of Medina had ongoing rivalries. Hassan attacks them on every front — their foolishness, dishonesty, stinginess, and the inhospitality symbolized by barking dogs around their tents. In Arab culture, dogs guarding a tent (rather than being kept at a distance) signified poverty and unwillingness to receive guests.

titleEn: Amra Has Resolved to Sever, So Set Forth at Dawn

verses.ajuz: ليس هذا منك يا عمر بسر

verses.sadr: ثم كانا خير من نال الندى ،
صبر للموت ، إن حل بنا ، م

translationEn: Amra has resolved to sever the bond, so set forth at dawn — only the distressed heart seeks soothing. Let not your love be a love shown openly; this is no secret from you, O Amr. They asked Hassan: who are his maternal uncles? Only the ignorant asks about such things. I said: my maternal uncles are the Banu Ka'b — when heroes expose their unguarded backs, they stand firm. Many a maternal uncle of mine, had you seen him, was open-handed on the cold, harsh day, at this very gate, where every free-born man of handsome countenance dwells. He kindles the fire when it is extinguished and fills the pot with the humps of slaughtered camels. Who can be deceived by fate or feel safe from it after the line of Amr and Hujr — two kings who ruled from the Snow Mountain to the flanks of Ayla, over slave and free? They were the best who attained generosity, surpassing all people in justice and righteousness — two horsemen of the cavalry, even when the veiled woman clutched the edges of her curtain. They came upon the Persians in their very homeland, and those Persians finally yielded after clinging fast. Then the two cried out: O people of Ghassan, be steadfast! For this is a day of brave, patient warriors. Make your right hands the refuge of your swords, with chosen steel, uncracked. With blows that make the jinn take notice, and thrusts like the mouths of wells. Those who have fought us know well that we have long brought benefit and harm. We are patient before death when it comes upon us — sincere in valor, noble champions. Honor and wealth have made their abode among us, and from that we hold supremacy over all people. My root is from them, and whoever boasts of such lineage, people will recognize the boaster's boast. We are the people of honor and glory together, neither craven nor crooked nor perverse. So ask about us and about our deeds — every people has knowledge of the report.
context: A major fakhr (boasting) poem tracing Hassan's Ghassanid maternal lineage. After a brief nasib (love prelude) about Amra, he celebrates his maternal uncles from the Banu Ka'b and the Ghassanid kings Amr and Hujr, who ruled from Mount Hermon to Aqaba. The poem recounts their campaigns against Persia and portrays the Ghassanids as the paragon of Arab nobility, valor, and generosity — a heritage Hassan claims as his own.

titleAr: قد أصبح القلب عنها كاد يصرفه
titleEn: The Heart Has Nearly Turned Away from Her

verses.sadr: قد أصبح القلب عنها كاد يصرفه
يا زيد ، يا سيد النجار ، إن لما
verses.ajuz: أحدث قومك في عثمان لي خبرا

verses.sadr: وإن لي حاجة ، يا زيد ، أذكرها ،
verses.ajuz: لم أقض منها إلى ما قومنا وطرا

verses.sadr: يا زيد ! هل لك فيهم قبل موبقة
يا زيد ! أهد لهم رأيا يعاش به ؛
verses.ajuz: يا زيد زيد بني النجار ، مقتصرا

verses.sadr: يا زيد ! أخرج بني النجار إذ عميت ،

translationEn: The heart has nearly turned away from her, distracted by speech other than poetry. O Zayd, O chief of al-Najjar — what your people have wrought against Uthman has given me grave concern. I have a need, O Zayd, that I must mention; I have not fulfilled my desire since our people acted thus. I see in them a course that will destroy them, and young men who have lost their sight regarding them. O Zayd, will you intervene before a catastrophe that kindles fire among their courtyards? O Zayd, guide them with counsel they can live by — O Zayd, Zayd of the Banu al-Najjar, with restraint. O Zayd, lead the Banu al-Najjar out from this blindness, and abandon the factions of Ghassan to their consequences.
context: A political poem addressed to Zayd, chief of the Banu al-Najjar (a Khazraj clan), urging him to intervene during the political crisis surrounding the Caliph Uthman ibn Affan's rule. Hassan, who supported Uthman, warns of impending destruction if the tribal leaders do not restrain their factions. This poem is a rare window into the political tensions that preceded the First Fitna (civil war).

titleAr: رميت بها أهل المضيق ، فلم تكد
titleEn: I Aimed at the People of the Narrow Pass

verses.sadr: رميت بها أهل المضيق ، فلم تكد

verses.ajuz: فقلت لهم من صادر مع صادر

verses.sadr: ذكرت بها التعريس لما بدا لنا
verses.ajuz: خيام بها من بين باد وحاضر

verses.sadr: إذا فضلة من بطن زق ونطفة

verses.ajuz: يدي رونق من ماء زمزم فاتر

translationEn: I launched her toward the people of the narrow pass, and she barely escaped from among donkeys and camels. She passed by the Ansar amid their saddles, and I said to them: one departing joins another. I circumambulated the Ancient House and took the road of Kada' along well-worn, traveled paths. I recalled the night halt when tents appeared to us, some of nomads and some of settled folk. And Dhu Dawran loomed before us — you would think from the drought that its slopes were the bared necks of unveiled women. She halted and set down a small foot on the slope, so I could see what provisions the noble traveler carried — only the remnant from a waterskin's belly, a drop of water, and a small bowl atop a lean, crooked she-camel. I stood with a cup of wine and poured it out, my hands glistening with the lukewarm water of Zamzam. When we descended into the belly of Marr, the Khuza'a branched off from us into encampments in the wadis.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Kada' — a mountain pass entering Mecca
Zamzam — the sacred well in Mecca

context: A descriptive poem (wasf) narrating a journey to Mecca. Hassan describes the approach through narrow passes crowded with animals, passing the Ansar's camps, circumambulating the Kaaba, and the austere provisions of travel. The poem is rich in topographical detail — the pass of Kada', the mountain Dhu Dawran, the valley of Marr — and captures the pilgrim's experience of the Meccan landscape.

titleEn: Show Me Fortunes Like the Fortunes That Rose

verses.ajuz: بمكة من أولاد عمرو بن عامر

verses.sadr: وكم عقدوا لله ، ثم وفوا به ،
verses.ajuz: بما ضاق عنه كل باد وحاضر

translationEn: Show me fortunes like those that rose in Mecca from the sons of Amr ibn Amir. They raised the pillar of the religion until its foundations were firmly established with keen, cutting blades. How many a covenant did they make with God and then fulfill it — achieving what every nomad and settled man found too vast to accomplish.
context: A concise praise poem celebrating the Ansar as descendants of Amr ibn Amir, the legendary ancestor of the Yemeni Arab tribes. Hassan credits them with establishing the foundations of Islam through both covenant and sword, challenging anyone to produce a people of comparable fortune and achievement.

titleAr: ما البكر إلا كالفصيل وقد ترى
titleEn: The Young Camel Is Like the Weaned Calf

verses.sadr: ما البكر إلا كالفصيل وقد ترى

translationEn: The young camel is no different from the weaned calf, and you may see that being a weaned calf is no disgrace upon it. By Him to whose House the pilgrims make pilgrimage, we riders of Mecca are the company of the Ansar. We split your skulls with every Indian blade, like the butcher striking the first portions of the slaughtered shares — until you call him by the name of the stallion of Hunayda, who guards his mares, mature and bellowing.
context: A boastful poem combining proverbial wisdom with martial threat. Hassan opens with the folk saying that a young camel is like a weaned calf — appearances can be deceiving — before declaring the prowess of the Ansar. The butchery metaphor graphically depicts the violence of battle, and the poem closes with the image of a powerful stallion as the embodiment of Ansar strength.

titleAr: وأمانة المري ، حيث لقيته ، يا حار من يغدر بذمة جاره
titleEn: The Trustworthiness of the Murri

verses.sadr: وأمانة المري ، حيث لقيته ، يا حار من يغدر بذمة جاره

translationEn: As for the trustworthiness of the Murri — wherever you meet him — O Harith, whoever among you betrays his neighbor's covenant, know that Muhammad never betrayed. If you betray, then treachery is your inborn trait, and treachery grows in the roots of the sakhbar bush. The trustworthiness of the Murri, wherever you meet him, is like glass — once cracked, it cannot be mended.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Murri (man from the tribe of Murra)

context: A satirical poem attacking the tribe of Murra for their treachery, contrasting their broken covenants with the Prophet Muhammad's faithfulness. The proverb that treachery grows in the roots of the sakhbar bush suggests it is innate to them. The simile of cracked glass — impossible to repair — became proverbial in Arabic literature for irreversible betrayal.

titleAr: ما ولدتكم قروم من بني أسد ،
titleEn: No Noble Stallions of Banu Asad Sired You

verses.sadr: ما ولدتكم قروم من بني أسد ،
verses.ajuz: ولا هصيص ، ولا تيم ، ولا عمر

verses.sadr: ولا عدي بن كعب ، إن صيغتها
وأنت عبد لقين ، لا فؤاد له ،
verses.ajuz: من آل شجع ، هناك اللؤم والخور

translationEn: No noble stallions of the Banu Asad sired you, nor Husays, nor Taym, nor Umar, nor Adi ibn Ka'b — whose forging is like Indian steel, neither worn out nor faded. You are but a slave of a base man with no heart, from the clan of Shuja' — there lies baseness and weakness. Your birth among the Shuja' is plainly evident, as plainly as the direction from which the moon rises.
context: A genealogical satire in which Hassan denies his target any noble lineage among the respected Qurayshi clans — Banu Asad, Taym, Umar, and Adi ibn Ka'b. He instead assigns the target to the obscure Shuja' clan, equating their origin with baseness and cowardice. The comparison of fine Qurayshi lineage to Indian steel emphasizes the contrast with the target's ignoble birth.

titleEn: Uyayna Thought When He Raided Her

verses.ajuz: م ، لم يكشفوا عن ملط حصيرا

translationEn: Uyayna thought, when he raided her, that he would demolish her palaces. You promised your host what would never be, saying: we shall seize great spoils. But you found Medina, when you came to her, and you found the roar of lions within her. They turned back swiftly like the pace of ostriches, without having uncovered a single wall from its mat. Our commander over us is the Messenger of the Sovereign — how beloved a commander he is to us! A Messenger whose revelation we believe — he was a shining lamp.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Uyayna ibn Hisn — a Ghatafan chief

context: A satirical poem mocking Uyayna ibn Hisn al-Fazari, the Ghatafan chieftain who raided the outskirts of Medina but fled when he encountered Muslim resistance. Hassan contrasts Uyayna's boastful expectations with his humiliating retreat, comparing the fleeing raiders to ostriches. The poem closes with a declaration of faith in the Prophet as the community's true leader and a 'shining lamp' — echoing Quranic imagery (33:46).

titleAr: حار بن كعب ألا الأحلام تزجركم
titleEn: O Harith ibn Ka'b, Do Your Minds Not Restrain You

verses.sadr: حار بن كعب ألا الأحلام تزجركم
لا بأس بالقوم من طول ومن عظم ،
لا ينفع الطول من نوك الرجال ، ولا

translationEn: O Harith ibn Ka'b, do your minds not restrain you from us? You who are from the hollowed-out Jawf, those who strut. There is nothing wrong with these people in height and bulk — the bodies of mules and the minds of sparrows. Stop your swaggering and walk with an even gait, for true men are those of sinew and remembrance. You are like hollow logs, bored through at their bases, in which the winds of storms howl. No lancing, no charging horsemen — just your belching around the ovens. Height is of no use when men are fools, and God does not guide the path of a barren people. I shall keep my honor away from the worst of you, for the Najashi is a thing not worth mentioning. He found his father and his grandfather confined, set apart from the heights of glory and goodness.
context: One of Hassan's most famous satirical poems, targeting the tribe of Harith ibn Ka'b. The iconic verse 'bodies of mules and minds of sparrows' became one of the most quoted lines in Arabic poetry for describing people who are physically imposing but intellectually vacuous. The image of hollow logs howling with wind reinforces the theme of emptiness behind appearances. Hassan also targets al-Najashi, a rival poet.

titleEn: By Your Life, in the Valley between Ma'rif

verses.ajuz: وبين الجثى ، لا يجشم السير ، حاضر

verses.sadr: إذا قيل يوما إظعنوا قد أتيتم ،
تقول وتذري الدمع عن حر وجهها :

verses.ajuz: له من ذرى الجولان بقل وزاهر
سوى أنها قد بل منها المشافر
من الغاب ذو طمرين ، فالبز آطر
لدى نحرها من جمة الماء عاذر

translationEn: By your life, in the valley between Ma'rif and Nata'a there are dwellings and settled camps. By my life, a clan between the house of Muzahim and the hillocks — settled, not burdened with travel. A lawful clan whose herds are not driven in haste, having abundant provisions from behind the distant pastures. When told one day 'Move on, you have been overtaken,' they stood fast, and no camels were driven to them. More deserving of her are young men and riding camels whose lean, crooked forms cut through the night. She speaks and sheds tears from her noble face: perhaps, my soul, you will depart before your own soul departs. The patriarch of Ghassan opened for her a valley that has from the peaks of the Golan herbs and blossoms. She pastured in Ghassan on the slopes of Mahbal to Harith of the Golan, where the heights are visible. I brought her close for the saddle, and she was like a male ostrich in the Samawa, startled and fleeing. I led her to water, but she barely drank — only her lips were moistened. I drove her from the water of Thahmal at dawn — from the thicket came a man in rags, his garments sparse. She spent the night, and the water settled beneath her throat, a surplus at her breast from the abundance of water. She traveled through the night, then halted at Yathrib, among both nomads and settled folk.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Yathrib — the pre-Islamic name of Medina

context: A descriptive journey poem (rahil) tracing a route from the Khaybar region through the Golan Heights to Yathrib (Medina). Hassan describes settled clans, pastures, and the stages of a night journey on a lean she-camel. The topographical references — Nata'a, the Golan, al-Samawa, Thahmal — provide a geographic map of the poet's world. The poem blends the traditional journey motif with Ghassanid territorial pride.

titleEn: His Lightning Struck Busra

verses.sadr: أفنى بذي بعل حتى باد ساكنها ،

verses.ajuz: من وخز جن بأرض الروم مذكور

translationEn: His bolts struck Busra, and from his spear there rose smoke of fire like whirlwinds. He annihilated the people of Dhu Ba'l until its inhabitants vanished, and every palace of rough timber was left desolate. The people were hurried from their needs by a calamity — a piercing affliction by jinn in the land of the Romans, well remembered.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Busra — a city in southern Syria

context: A short praise poem celebrating a military campaign against Busra (Bosra) in the Byzantine Levant. The imagery of fire and whirlwinds evokes the devastation wrought by Arab raiders on Roman frontier towns. The reference to 'jinn piercing' in the land of the Romans may allude to plague or supernatural dread associated with these raids.

titleEn: Salama Is a Doll in a Door's Panel

verses.sadr: ولا ينفك ما عاش ابن روح

translationEn: Salama is a doll in a door's panel — woe to you, do you not honor as you give refuge? As long as Ibn Rawh lives, a Judhami man, he will never cease to be treacherous in his covenant.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: woe to you, may you be bereft

context: A brief, biting poem combining a love image with political satire. Salama is compared to a decorative doll painted on a door panel — beautiful but inert and useless. The second verse attacks Ibn Rawh of the Judham tribe as constitutionally treacherous. The juxtaposition of the ornamental beloved with tribal betrayal is characteristic of Hassan's compressed satirical style.

titleAr: يا ابني رفاعة ، ما بالي وبالكما ،
titleEn: O Two Sons of Rifa'a, What Is Between Me and You

verses.sadr: يا ابني رفاعة ، ما بالي وبالكما ،
قد خاب قوم نيار من سراتهم
لولا ابن هيشة ، إن المرء ذو رحم ،

translationEn: O two sons of Rifa'a, what is between me and you? Do you hold back, though my fire has not touched you? He would not stop until a dog hurls insults at me — I struck him on his mouth with stones. The three of them share half a garment among themselves — a loincloth and a cloak, neither of them clean. Wretched are the people whose chief, Niyyar, is a man from a starving household kindled by a fire-poker. Were it not for Ibn Haysha — for a man has ties of kinship — I would have sunk my claws into them at al-Bazwa'.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you two hold back, restrain yourselves

context: A satirical poem attacking the sons of Rifa'a and their ally Niyyar, mocking their poverty — three men sharing a single set of unclean garments — and their aggression. Hassan restrains himself only because of kinship ties through Ibn Haysha. The image of striking a barking dog on the mouth with stones conveys his contempt for those who provoke him without cause.

titleAr: أبلغ معاوية بن حرب مألكا ، أبلغ معاوية بن حرب مألكا ،
titleEn: Convey a Message to Mu'awiya ibn Harb

verses.sadr: أبلغ معاوية بن حرب مألكا ، أبلغ معاوية بن حرب مألكا ،

translationEn: Convey a message to Mu'awiya ibn Harb — and for every affair that is sought, there is a reckoning. Never accept a humiliation offered to you while the Ansar have not yet been harmed — not until tribe destroys tribe in retaliation, and dwelling destroys dwelling, and a battalion comes through the passes of the Hijaz, and Sarar overflows with armored men.
context: A political warning poem addressed to Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan (here called ibn Harb after his grandfather), urging him not to accept any humiliating terms from enemies while the Ansar remain undefeated. Hassan promises that the Ansar will fight to the last, sending battalions through the mountain passes of the Hijaz. This poem reflects the tense political dynamics of the early caliphate period.

titleEn: A People Whose Hatred Is Crooked

verses.sadr: يجيش بما فيه لنا الصدر مثل ما
verses.ajuz: تجيش بما فيها من اللهب القدر

verses.sadr: وإن سمعوا سوءا بدا في وجوههم ،
verses.ajuz: لما سمعوا ، مما يقال لنا البشر
فجورا بظهر الغيب أو ملحم قحر
فأثنت بما فينا ، إذا حمدت بدر
إذا لم يكن غير السيوف لنا ستر
من الروع ، كاب حسن ألوانها ، الزهر

translationEn: A people whose hatred is crooked — as though within their bellies, from what they conceal against us, there are live coals. Their chests seethe against us, just as a pot seethes with the flames within it. Their cheeks turn away when they face me in any gathering, as though they are afflicted with a twisted neck. Their heads incline away from me when good is mentioned of us in their presence, though they are not deaf. But when they hear something evil, joy appears on their faces at what is said against us. A treacherous wretch from Jud ceaselessly insults me — wickedly behind my back, or a stubborn brawler. Yet if Badr were asked about our fine valor and spoke of what is in us, Badr would praise us — guarding our honor with our very lives when nothing but swords served as our shield, and the women bared their arms and revealed from fright their bright faces, faded in complexion.
context: A poem combining satire of envious enemies with boasting about the Ansar's valor at Badr. Hassan vividly depicts the hypocrites who seethe with concealed hatred, turning away when good is mentioned of the Muslims and rejoicing at bad news. He then contrasts their treachery with the Ansar's proven courage, where women bared their arms and faces paled from fear as the men fought with swords as their only defense.

titleEn: Qurayza Has Met What Grieves Them

verses.ajuz: سوى ما قد أصاب بني النضير

translationEn: Qurayza has met what grieves them, and they found no helper against humiliation. A calamity struck them that was within them, beyond what had already befallen the Banu al-Nadir. On the morning the Messenger of God came sweeping down upon them like the luminous moon, with led horses galloping, carrying horsemen upon them like falcons. We left them having gained nothing — their blood upon them like perfume. They lay slain, with birds circling over them; thus is repaid the boaster with his false claims. So let the like of this follow as counsel to Quraysh, from the Most Merciful, if you accept my warning.
context: A poem about the siege and punishment of the Jewish tribe of Banu Qurayza (627 CE), who were accused of treachery during the Battle of the Trench. Hassan describes the Prophet descending upon them like the moon, with cavalrymen like falcons. The poem serves as both a record of the event and a warning to the Quraysh, urging them to accept Islam before meeting a similar fate. The reference to the Banu al-Nadir recalls their earlier expulsion from Medina.

titleEn: Quraysh Plastered the Basins of Glory

verses.sadr: والله ما في قريش كلها نفر
أما ابن نابغة العبد الهجين ، فقد
ما بال أمك زاغت عند ذي شرف

verses.ajuz: عند الحجون ، فما ملا وما فترا

verses.sadr: يا آل سهم ، فإني قد نصحت لكم ،
verses.ajuz: لا أبعثن على الأحياء من قبرا

verses.sadr: ألا ترون بأني قد ظلمت ، إذا
كم من كريم يعض الكلب مئزره
قولي لكم ، آل شجع ، سم مطرقة

verses.ajuz: لما تركت لكم أنثى ولا ذكرا

translationEn: Quraysh plastered the basins of glory, but the clan of Sahm rushed in and their basin was left empty. They brought their herds to drink, and the brimming basins of glory — their pool misled the drinkers and collapsed. By God, in all of Quraysh there is no clan with more old men who are cowardly, foul-mouthed, and ignorant — repulsive, bald, petty brokers with wolfish cunning, like monkeys chewing date-pits in the middle of the assembly. They are babbling, ill-omened, deprived — their guest when he departs is given the moon as provision. As for Ibn Nabigha, that base-born slave, I have unleashed upon him a tongue sharp and keen. What ailed your mother that she strayed to Judhayma at a man of rank, when she had effaced the traces? She lingered three days, with Milhan embracing her at al-Hujun, neither tiring nor slowing. O clan of Sahm, I have given you sincere counsel — I shall not raise from the graves against the living. Do you not see that I have been wronged, if al-Zibari were the equal of Thabit's sandal? How many a noble man's waistcloth does the dog bite, then flees when you throw a stone at him. My words to you, O clan of Shuja', are the venom of a solid hammer that knocks the filth from its teeth. Were it not for the Prophet and the obligation of truth even in anger, I would not have left you a single female or male.
context: A devastating satirical poem targeting the Banu Sahm clan of Quraysh and Hassan's rival poet Ibn al-Zibari. Hassan attacks their lineage, character, and hospitality with a barrage of animal imagery — monkeys, dogs, wolves. The poem escalates to a scandalous accusation about the target's mother before concluding that only the Prophet's command for truthfulness restrains him from total annihilation. This is among the most aggressive examples of hija (invective) in the Diwan.

titleEn: A Base People Whose Goodness God Has Curtailed

verses.sadr: كأن ريحهم في الناس ، إذ خرجوا ،
verses.ajuz: ريح الحشاش إذا ما بلها المطر

verses.sadr: قد ابرز الله قولا ، فوق قولهم ،

translationEn: A base people whose goodness God has curtailed, scattering like dung falls behind the rider. Their scent among people, when they emerged, was like the smell of dry grass when rain soaks it. God has brought forth a word above their words, just as the moon rises high above the stars.
context: A concise satirical poem in three verses, employing earthy, visceral imagery to degrade the targets. Their goodness scatters like camel dung on the road, and their presence reeks like wet grass. The final verse pivots to declare the supremacy of God's word over their empty speech — the moon outshining the stars. This closing theological affirmation elevates the poem beyond mere personal invective into a statement of divine vindication.

titleEn: I Do Not Revile the Hammas Tribe

verses.ajuz: لا هم كرام ولا عرضي لهم خطر

verses.sadr: كأن ريحهم ، في الناس إذ برزوا ،
verses.ajuz: ريح الكلاب إذا ما بلها المطر

verses.sadr: أولاد حام ، فلن تلقى لهم شبها
إن سابقوا سبقوا ، أو نافروا نفروا ،
verses.ajuz: أو كاثروا أحدا من غيرهم كثروا

verses.sadr: شبه الإماء ، فلا دين ولا حسب ،
verses.ajuz: لو قامروا الزنج ، عن أحسابهم ، قمروا
