translationEn: As for the Hammas, I do not revile them — they are neither noble nor does my honor risk anything from them. They are base people whom God has diminished in number, like dung that falls around a hollow. Their odor when they appear among people is like the smell of dogs when the rain wets them. They are sons of Ham, and you will find no likeness for them except goats with fur on their shoulders. If they race they win, or if they boast they flee, or if they outnumber anyone besides themselves they are many. Like slave-women — no religion and no lineage — if they gambled with Zanj over their ancestries they would lose. You find the Hammas man whose sanctity does not protect you — like the Nabataeans: when you enslave them they endure.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Hammas tribe, a group being satirized
the hollow/anus — used for crude comparison
they boasted of lineage in competition
the Nabataeans, an Arabized people of non-Arab origin
East African peoples, used for comparison

context: A satire (hija) against the Hammas tribe, notable for its harsh racial and social mockery. Hassan employs vivid animal imagery and ethnic comparisons typical of pre-Islamic and early Islamic satirical poetry, attacking the tribe's lineage, smell, and lack of honor. Such invective poetry served a social function in Arab tribal culture by discrediting enemies.

titleEn: She Pointed and Her Habit Was Baseness
translationEn: She pointed — the vile woman — and her habit was baseness when she pointed along with disbelief. She was brought out dancing to Uhud, striding among the people on a slow camel — a sluggish camel with no vigor in it, moved by neither reproach nor driving. Your staff and your backside are your shield, beating the weak-boned, bare-knuckled. Her haunches and her seat were wounded from the relentless forcing. Her companion stayed nursing her with water and with lote-tree leaves. You came rushing as a visitor with your father, your son on the day of Badr — and your uncle stripped of his armor, your brother groveling in the dust. You forgot the obscenity you committed, O Hind — woe to you, the shame of all ages. You returned humiliated, with no blood-price for what you gained, no vengeance. The slave-women claim she bore a small child who was born of fornication.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the vile/base woman — a term of contempt
dancing, moving rhythmically in a provocative manner
the tendon at the back of the knee

context: A fierce satirical poem directed at Hind bint Utba, the Qurayshi woman famous for mutilating Hamza's body after the Battle of Uhud (625 CE) and chewing his liver in rage. Hassan attacks her morality, the defeat of her family at Badr, and her conduct. This represents the political-religious hija that Hassan composed on behalf of the Prophet against Quraysh's leaders.

titleEn: Your Vile Father Was a Disgrace
translationEn: Your vile father was a disgrace, diminished — your father the billy-goat was like a barren ewe. He was a humiliated wretch, a cursed outcast, then they named him, after being despised, 'the mighty.' The sons of Nawfal are people of generosity and largesse — they sheltered you from poverty and provided for the old woman.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the vile, the contemptible, base person
a billy-goat — used as an insult
a barren, dry ewe that gives little milk
an outcast, one driven away from his tribe

context: A short satirical poem attacking an unnamed adversary's father, contrasting his lowly origins with the generosity of Banu Nawfal who sheltered him. Hassan uses animal imagery (billy-goat, barren ewe) as a standard device in Arabic hija poetry to demean lineage and masculinity. The poem exemplifies the tribal honor-shame dynamic central to early Islamic poetry.

titleAr: لو كان ، في الدار ، قوم ذو محافظة ،
titleEn: Had There Been Guardians in the Abode

verses.sadr: لو كان ، في الدار ، قوم ذو محافظة ،

verses.ajuz: من المعاشر ، ممن قد نفت عدس

translationEn: Had there been in the dwelling people of protection — defenders of truth, determined, with Anas as their maternal uncle — when you settled, O Khubaib, in a spacious abode, they would not have shackled you with chains and guards. They would not have driven you to al-Tan'im with a rabble from the clans, those whom 'Adas has rejected. Be patient, O Khubaib — for killing is an honor, and the soul returns to the Gardens of bliss.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: defender of truth and kinship bonds
a rabble, a mixed base group of people
a place outside Mecca where Khubaib was crucified
a tribe or person referenced in the context

context: An elegy for Khubaib ibn Adi al-Ansari, a companion of the Prophet who was captured by Quraysh and crucified at al-Tan'im near Mecca around 625 CE. Hassan laments that had his own Ansar kinspeople been present, they would have prevented his capture. The poem ends with consolation — martyrdom leads to Paradise — a characteristically Hassanic blend of tribal elegy and Islamic faith.

titleAr: يا آل بكر ألا تنهون جاهلكم ،
titleEn: O Banu Bakr Restrain Your Fool

verses.sadr: يا آل بكر ألا تنهون جاهلكم ،
verses.ajuz: عبد ابن رحضة عنزا بين أتياس

verses.sadr: يا ابن التي سلحت في بيت جارتها ،
مثل القرود ، إذا ما جئت ناديهم ،

translationEn: O clan of Bakr, will you not restrain your ignorant one — a slave of Ibn Ruhda, a she-goat among billy-goats? O son of the woman who defecated in her neighbor's house, and a foul stench spread from it that disgusted the people. As if her nails were split from stone — there is not one of them that is not swollen and hardened. Like monkeys when you come to their gathering-place, you find every lowly wretch with a hardened back.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: your ignorant/foolish one, used as insult
billy-goats, plural of تيس — used as insult

context: A satire against Banu Bakr, attacking a specific individual and his mother with coarse, vivid insults typical of early Arabic hija. Hassan addresses the tribe collectively, shaming them for not controlling a fool in their midst. The poem uses graphic bodily imagery and animal comparisons that characterize the most aggressive stratum of classical Arabic satirical poetry.

titleEn: Whose Abandoned House Stands at Buwat

verses.sadr: دارها ، إذ تقول : ما لابن عمرو
verses.ajuz: لج ، من بعد قربه ، في شطاط

translationEn: Whose house has become deserted at Buwat — nothing remaining but blackened stones, motionless like black birds? That is the house of the beloved, now empty, after she had settled it in vitality. It was her house, when she said: why has Ibn Amr become distant, estranged after his nearness? Tell her I am the best guardian of what she carried without excess. How many a pleasure I witnessed, O Umm Amr, among white fair women in striped garments — with companions, white-faced and noble, awakened after the bells' tinkling. Wine red as the blood of a slaughtered animal, matured from the vintage of the Nabataeans. A young man who lavishes wealth on it hosted it, and I drank with Salih ibn Ilat. Around me slave-musicians played all day, tawny-skinned, coquettish, and restrained. They circled with the cup among noble drinkers who had prepared fine carpets for Salih. After a time he said: 'Scatter among yourselves — not for show, but away from the mingling.' How many a wide tract I traversed, with the jinn playing around me, my sharp sword at my armpit — above the place where a rider descends, lofty, like a wolf of the thicket, darting. While we were roasting camel hump, a vigorous voice startled us. We were brought a swimming fleet horse, not broken by manger and tether — untamed, a beast of the wilderness, full-humped, with good milch camels and broad-spreading pasture-camels in winter. They called out and bridled him, saying to a boy skilled at breaking horses: 'Calm him — draw from the west, and you will find him a good draw-horse with few stumbles.' The boy went forward, controlling a colt full of the bucket's water, resistting the whips. The women turned away when they saw a compact-backed figure like a rope's back — above him a feeder of wild beasts, gentle, knowing the direction of armpits. Tamed to the hunt, casting a glance in the open and the wide sandy plains. Then he pursued a swift onager and a wild she-onager and a wild bull, controlling it with a lasso. Then we departed, and my friend fears no treachery from my tongue through familiarity.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a place name — location of a ruined encampment
blackened hearth stones, remains of a campfire
camel hump meat, considered a delicacy
a fleet, long horse that gallops powerfully

context: A long qasida (36 verses in the original, 24 reconstructed here) opening with the classical nasib (amatory prelude) lamenting a deserted campsite at Buwat, then moving through scenes of convivial drinking, music, and a hunt. This poem showcases Hassan's pre-Islamic and early Islamic sensibility, combining the standard qasida structure with vivid desert and hunt imagery. Buwat is a location associated with early Islamic expeditions.

titleAr: بني أسد ، ما بال آل خويلد
titleEn: O Banu Asad Why Does Khuwaylid's Clan

verses.sadr: بني أسد ، ما بال آل خويلد
verses.ajuz: يحنون شوقا كل يوم إلى القبط
تخالف كعبا في لحى لهم ثط

verses.sadr: ترى ذاك في الشبان والمرد منهم ،
verses.ajuz: مبينا ، وفي الأطفال منهم وفي الشمط

translationEn: O Banu Asad, what is wrong with the clan of Khuwaylid — they long every day with nostalgia for the Copts? When Qahqa'a is mentioned they yearn for her memory, and for the saltwort paired with speckled fish. Their eyes are like glass, and their physiognomy differs from Ka'b's in sparse, thin beards. You see this plainly in their youth and the beardless among them, and in their children and in the grey-haired. By the life of Abu al-Awwam, Khuwaylid — the morning they adopted him was to bind him in the condition of a slave. And if you drag a crime against me, I will send you back as a slave in humiliation and abasement.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Copts, Egyptians — reference to non-Arab origin
saltwort, a desert shrub eaten by camels
sparse-bearded, thin-bearded — considered a mark of non-Arab origin
condition of slavery, terms of bondage

context: A satire against the clan of Khuwaylid from Banu Asad, attacking them as of Coptic (Egyptian) origin, reflected in their glass-like eyes and sparse beards — physical markers used in early Arabic poetry to impugn non-Arab lineage. Hassan accuses them of enslavement status, a devastating charge in tribal Arab society. The poem reflects the ethnic and social hierarchies of early Islamic Arabia.

titleEn: Spurious Words from Umayya Reached Me
translationEn: Spurious words from Umayya reached me — he is not one who preserves loyalty in absence. I shall spread, if I survive, words for you that will circulate in the gatherings of Ukaz. Rhymes like smooth stones when they flow, from the hard, unyielding, massive ones — they will visit you wherever you winter in any land, and crush you in your dwelling with their hardness. I have built against you strong verses, like the frame of a load bound with wooden pegs — covering him with shame, wrapping him in disgrace, blazing and flaming like fire's heat. Like the roar of a lion defending its lair, with firmly rooted ribs and powerful. You lower your gaze when I meet you face to face, and cast sidelong glances when I turn away.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: spurious, contemptible words — words without substance
the great pre-Islamic fair and poetry competition market
smooth, rounded stones; also peace — pun used for verse imagery
wooden pegs used to bind a loaded camel's pack
a lion — used for fierce comparison
sidelong glances, the corner of the eye

context: Hassan's famous poetic reply to Umayya ibn Khalaf al-Khuza'i who had satirized him. The poem is a classic example of the poetic exchange (munafara) between rival poets. Hassan threatens verses that will spread through the entire Arabian fair-circuit of Ukaz, using rock, fire, and lion imagery to describe the devastating power of his satirical poetry. The poem showcases Hassan's confidence in his craft.

titleAr: منعنا رسول الله ، إذ حل وسطنا ،
titleEn: We Protected God's Messenger Among Us

verses.sadr: منعنا رسول الله ، إذ حل وسطنا ،
verses.ajuz: على انف راض من معد وراغم

translationEn: We protected the Messenger of God when he settled among us — to the satisfaction of Ma'add's willing and the vexation of the unwilling. We protected him when he settled in the midst of our houses with our swords from every aggressor and oppressor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we protected, we defended — key verb of Ansar identity
a major Arab tribal grouping, ancestor of northern Arabs
one who is humiliated against his will, vexed

context: A short but powerful fakhr/madih poem expressing the Ansar's pride in having sheltered and protected the Prophet Muhammad upon his migration to Medina (622 CE). The phrase 'we protected the Messenger of God' is central to Ansar identity, and Hassan here distills that pride into two compact verses. The contrast between 'the satisfied' (willing tribes) and 'the vexed' (hostile ones) reflects the political landscape of the early Muslim community.

titleEn: The Nobles of Fihr and Their Brothers

verses.sadr: يرضى بها كل من كانت سريرته

verses.ajuz: أو حاولوا النفع في أشياعهم نفعوا

verses.sadr: لا يرقع الناس ما أوهت أكفهم
verses.ajuz: عند الدفاع ، ولا يوهون ما رقعوا

verses.sadr: إن كان في الناس سباقون بعدهم ،

verses.ajuz: في فضل أحلامهم عن ذاك متسع

verses.sadr: كم من صديق لهم نالوا كرامته ،

verses.ajuz: فما ونى نصرهم عنه وما نزعوا

verses.sadr: إن قال سيروا أجدوا السير جهدهم ،
verses.ajuz: أو قال عوجوا علينا ساعة ، ربعوا

verses.sadr: ما زال سيرهم حتى استقاد لهم
verses.ajuz: أهل الصليب ، ومن كانت له البيع

verses.sadr: خذ منهم ما أتى عفوا ، إذا غضبوا ،
verses.ajuz: ولا يكن همك الأمر الذي منعوا

verses.sadr: لا فخر إن هم أصابوا من عدوهم ،
verses.ajuz: وإن أصيبوا فلا خور ولا جزع

verses.sadr: إذا نصبنا لقوم لا ندب لهم ،

verses.ajuz: إن جد بالناس جد القول أو شمعوا

translationEn: The nobles of Fihr and their brothers have established a way of life for people to follow — one with which every person whose inner self is the fear of God and the path they have laid down is satisfied. A people who, when they fight, harm their enemies, and when they seek to benefit their followers, they benefit them. That is a natural disposition of theirs, not newly invented — for know that the worst of character traits are innovations. People cannot mend what their hands have torn in defense, nor do they tear what they have mended. If there are those who outrace others after them, every race falls short of even the least of their lead. They do not withhold their excellence from an ally, nor does greed ever afflict them in any ambition. They do not act in ignorance, and even if you try to provoke their ignorance, the vastness of their forbearance has room for that. Chaste — their chastity is mentioned in revelation — they do not covet, and greed does not destroy them. How many a friend they have honored, and how many a striving enemy they have subdued. They gave the Prophet of guidance and piety their obedience — their support of him never slackened nor withdrew. When he said 'march,' they marched with all their effort; or when he said 'turn to us for a while,' they halted. Their march continued until the people of the Cross and those with churches submitted to them. Take from them what comes freely when they are angered, and do not let your concern be what they withheld. For in war against them — so abandon enmity — lies an evil waded through with colocynth and euphorbia. We rise when war's claws reach us, while the rabble cringe from its talons. No boasting if they strike their enemy, and if they are struck — no weakness and no wailing. As if in battle, with death lurking, they are lions of Bisha, with swollen shanks. When we set out after people who have no match for us, like a newborn camel creeping toward the wild beast. How noble a people whose followers are the Messenger of God when desires and factions diverge! I dedicate my praise to a people whose praise is seconded by what a skillful weaver's tongue loves. They are the best of all the living — when the truth of things is tested by word or example.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the nobles, the leading figures of a tribe
Fihr ibn Malik, ancestor of Quraysh and many Arab tribes
innovations, newly invented practices — frowned upon
colocynth — a bitter plant symbolizing hardship
the lowly, the fringes, rabble people
a valley in Arabia famous for its lions
twisted, swollen at the joints — describing lions' powerful ankles

context: A long madih poem praising the Companions of the Prophet — the Muhajirun (emigrants from Mecca, descendants of Fihr/Quraysh) and Ansar — for their unwavering obedience, chastity, generosity, forbearance, and military prowess. Hassan praises their Quranic virtue and their role in the early Islamic conquests. This is one of his major polished qasidas and represents the classical form of Islamic panegyric poetry.

titleEn: Lightning Kept Me Awake at Sla and Fari
translationEn: I was kept awake by the flashing of the gleaming lightning, while we were intoxicated between Sla' and Fari'. I lay awake watching it until I knew its location in the flanks of Sla' and the broad, flowing valleys. The striped rocky terrain of al-Azaf rolled its thunder, like the moaning of young camels toward the sound of their companions.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the gleaming, the shining — referring to lightning bolts
broad, raised valleys where water flows
striped rocky terrain associated with jinn music

context: A brief but evocative poem opening with the classical motif of the narrator kept awake by lightning near Medina. The mountains of Sla' and Fari' are Medinan landmarks. The thunder is compared to the mourning of young camels — a beloved Arabic image combining nature and longing. The poem reflects Hassan's mastery of atmospheric imagery within the nasib tradition.

titleAr: ألا يا لقوم هل لما حم دافع ؟
titleEn: Is There Any Repelling of What Fate Decrees

verses.sadr: ألا يا لقوم هل لما حم دافع ؟
verses.ajuz: وهل ما مضى من صالح العيش راجع
مطيع له في كل أمر وسامع
إذا لم يكن إلا النبيين شافع
وإن قضاء الله لا بد واقع

translationEn: Alas, O people — is there any repelling of what fate has decreed? And can the good of past life ever return? I remembered a time gone by and my bowels heaved, and tears poured from me. A longing and grief recalled for me beloved ones — and fallen martyrs among them: Nafi' and Rafi'. And Sa'd — they have passed on to the Gardens, while their dwellings are desolate and the earth is bare of them. They kept faith on the day of Badr with the Messenger, while above them hung the shadows of death and the gleaming swords. He called, and they answered him in truth — every one of them obedient and attentive to him in every matter. They did not change until they were gathered together — and nothing cuts short the appointed times except battles. For they hope for his intercession when no one but the Prophets can intercede. That, O best of servants, is our trial, and our witness in the cause of God — while death is a quencher. Ours is the foremost precedence toward you, and those who came after us follow our predecessors in obedience to God. We know that sovereignty belongs to God alone, and that God's decree must inevitably come to pass.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what was fated, what was decreed by destiny
scenes of battle, places of wrestling down
intercession on the Day of Judgment
our trial, our ordeal, our test
the foremost precedence, first foot forward
a quencher, something that satisfies thirst — death satisfies

context: An elegy for the Ansari martyrs of Badr (624 CE), specifically naming Nafi' and Rafi' and Sa'd among the fallen. Hassan reflects on time's passing, the desolation of their former homes, and their faithfulness to the Prophet. The poem ends on an Islamic note of submission to divine decree and hope for the Prophet's intercession — a hallmark of Hassan's blend of tribal elegy with Islamic theology.

titleEn: Lamis Has Departed with Severed Bonds

verses.ajuz: في الفجر ، فيض غروب ذات أتراع

verses.sadr: هلا سألت ، هداك الله ، ما حسبي ،
هل أغفر الذنب ذا الجرح العظيم ، ولو
أسعى على جل قوم كان سعيهم

verses.ajuz: من عائق مثل عين الديك شعشاع
نحو الصريخ ، إذا ما ثوب الداعي

translationEn: Lamis has departed with severed bonds between you — she settled at al-Ghamr, a longing departure with flowing banners. She has become a neighbor among Banu Nasr, grazing in the broad lowlands in honor and lush pasture. My eyes, as their caravan turned away at dawn, were like the overflow of full water-troughs. Why did you not ask — may God guide you — what my lineage is, O Umm al-Walid? The best speech is for one who heeds. Can I forgive the fault — this great wound — even if its torrents pass through me with pains? God knows what I strive for on their behalf and what hides in my chest and ribs. I strive for the best of a people whose endeavor was quiet and effortless in the midst of the tribe. I make no peace with those who showed hostility and I abandon them, and I am never absent for them in any day with reproaches. I have gone at morning to the tavern where wine greets me — a hindrance like the rooster's eye, shining bright. It comes to me in the morning, with my companion's elbow as support, and we spend our pleasures in amusement and music. When we wish we call for it, and he pours for us from a full-chested, full-bellied vessel. And I have seen myself at the head of the tribe, girt with a sharp sword the color of salt, cutting. Pushed off me by the sword's strap is a long coat of mail, loose and wide like the color of a standing pool in the lowland — among youths whose faces are like Indian swords, turning toward the cry when the caller calls.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she departed, she separated — opening motif of longing
a woman's name — a common beloved in Arabic nasib
hindrance — here used for wine that keeps one back from duties
a full-length coat of mail, reaching the ankles
a cry for help, distress call in battle
the caller's cry when summoning to battle

context: A qasida opening with the classic nasib lamenting the departure of the beloved Lamis, then transitioning through scenes of wine-drinking and ending with warrior imagery. This tripartite structure — amatory prelude, wine/companionship, warrior boast — is a hallmark of the classical qasida tradition. The poem reflects Hassan's range as a poet across the erotic, convivial, and martial registers.

titleEn: Do the Ruins of Umm al-Walid Stir You

verses.sadr: فلم يبق إلا موقد النار حوله
فدع ذكر دار بددت بين أهلها
وقل : إن يكن يوم بأحد يعده
وقد ضاربت فيه بنو الأوس كلهم ،

verses.ajuz: وما كان منهم ، في اللقاء ، جزوع

verses.sadr: وفوا إذ كفرتم ، يا سخين ، بربكم ،

verses.ajuz: فلا بد أن يردى بهن صريع

verses.sadr: كما غادرت في النقع عثمان ثاويا ،

verses.ajuz: على القوم مما قد يثرن نقوع
وإن كان أمر ، يا سخين ، فظيع

translationEn: Do the deserted ruins of Umm al-Walid stir your longing — barren, with none of their people gathered there? The summer winds have effaced them, and a pouring rain from Aquarius, with rumbling, weeping clouds. Nothing remains except the fire-place, around which are stationary stones like roosting doves. Leave the mention of a house whose people are scattered — a separation that divided the assembled, a cutting departure. Say: if the day of Uhud is counted by a fool as a victory, the truth will surely spread. Banu al-Aws all fought in it, and they had a high reputation there. Banu al-Najjar defended and fought — none of them was cowardly in the encounter. Before the Messenger of God they did not abandon him, and they have a supporter and intercessor from their Lord. They kept faith when you, O Sakhin, disbelieved in your Lord — and a disobedient slave and an obedient one are never equal. In their right hands white blades when battle rages — inevitably someone is laid low by them. Just as they left Uthman lying prostrate in the dust and Sa'd felled, with spears thrust in. They left under the battle-dust, propped up, the noble Ubayy — his shirt soaked with blood — in the hand of the Messenger of God, until it enveloped the people with what they had stirred up. Those are my people, lords from their branches — and from every people come lords and branches. By them does God give us honor when He honors us, even if a matter, O Sakhin, is dreadful. If you mention the slain and Hamza among them — a martyr who stayed loyal to God while obedient — then the Gardens of eternity are his abode, and the decree of Him who decides matters is swift. But your slain in the Fire — the best of their provision there is scalding water together and bitter zaqqum.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an insulting epithet for Quraysh, meaning 'the burnt/angry ones'
battle dust, the cloud of dust in combat
spears made of wicker bamboo — a type of lance
thrust in, plunged — of spears into fallen warriors
dark blood, blood soaking a garment
zaqqum, bitter hellish plant mentioned in the Quran

context: A long and significant poem contrasting the Battle of Uhud (625 CE) from the Muslim perspective. Hassan addresses Quraysh contemptuously as 'Sakhin' (the burnt/angry ones), praises the Ansar tribes of Aws and Khazraj/Najjar for their bravery, mourns the martyrdom of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and promises hellfire for the Qurayshi dead. The poem integrates theological conviction with tribal fakhr/hija, showing Hassan's mature Islamic poetic voice.

titleEn: Turn Away from Foul Words When You Hear Them

verses.sadr: والقوم إن نزروا فزد في نزرهم ،

translationEn: Turn away from foul speech when you hear it — sit as if you are heedless and do not hear. Leave off questioning and probing matters — for many a one who digs a pit is himself thrown into it. Keep to the company of the noble and their deeds, and when you follow someone, look carefully at whom you follow. Do not follow a temptation for mere passion — for temptation gathers together every evil. If people reduce their speech, add to their reduction — do not sit among them listening in. Do not be addicted to drinking — take only its moderation and you will wake with a clear head, unsplit. Strive for yourself alone, do not burden another — for your soul will be rewarded for its own deeds and protected from others. Death comes in numbers equal to souls, and I see no flight from it that profits the fleeing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: foul, one-eyed speech — ugly, blameworthy words
one who digs a pit — proverbial for self-destruction
they spoke little, reduced their speech
to be addicted, to make something a constant habit
as many as the souls — death is equal to all living

context: A wisdom poem (hikmah) in the style of moral instruction, offering practical advice on social conduct, drinking, following good companions, and accepting death. This genre of reflective poetry sits alongside Hassan's more combative hija and madih. The poem's practical Islamic ethics — avoiding eavesdropping, moderation in drink, reliance on oneself — reflect the values of the Medinan Muslim community.

titleEn: Hell's Guards Around Their Tents
translationEn: Hell's guards surround their tents, yet they are cowards in the thick of battle.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hell's guards, the angels who drive people into hellfire
cowards, those who are weak and craven
the thick of battle, the heat and noise of combat
around, surrounding — describing the encirclement of the tents

context: A single-verse epigram (maqtu'a) combining Islamic imagery (hell's guards) with the Arab warrior ethos. By invoking the Zabbaniyya — the fearsome angels of hellfire mentioned in the Quran (96:18) — as hovering around his enemies' tents, Hassan uses Quranic allusion to amplify his satire, while simultaneously mocking them as battlefield cowards. A striking compression of religious invective.

titleEn: Ask Banu al-Ash'ar When You Visit Them
translationEn: Ask Banu al-Ash'ar when you visit them: what was the news of Banu Wasi'? When they abandoned him while he called to them by the farthest kinship and the bond that joins — while the lion was overpowering him with its fangs, as he rolled in a pool of dark blood. May the Merciful not raise their fallen, and may He not weaken the power of the one who wrestled him down.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Banu al-Ash'ar, an Arab tribe being addressed
Banu Wasi', the tribe of the victim being lamented
the farthest kinship — remote tribal connection
the bond that gathers — common ancestry
the lion — here used for an enemy warrior or actual lion
rolled in dust and blood, prostrate on the ground
dark, settled blood — blood that has pooled and dried

context: A condemnatory poem against Banu al-Ash'ar for abandoning a kinsman (from Banu Wasi') to his fate while he cried out for help. The vivid image of a lion tearing the man apart while his relatives stood by makes this both a hija against the cowardly tribe and a ritha for the victim. Hassan ends with a curse: may God not raise their fallen, invoking divine justice against the deserters.

titleEn: I Remind Banu al-Najjar of My Father's Deeds

verses.ajuz: إذا لم يجد عان له من يوارعه

verses.sadr: على النأي منهم ذا حفاظ يطالعه
ألسنا ننص العيس فيه على الوجى ،

verses.ajuz: إذا ما شتاء المحل هبت زعازعه
إذا ما وليد الحي لم يسق شربة ،
وما نالنا من صالح ، فهو واسعه

verses.sadr: إذا الكبش لم يوجد له من يقارعه

verses.ajuz: إذا الخصم لم يوجد له من يدافعه

verses.sadr: ولا ننتهي أو يخلص الحق ناصعه
لأثنوا به ، ما يأثر القول سامعه
verses.ajuz: كما لو فعلتم مثل ذاك إليهم ،

translationEn: I remind Banu al-Najjar of the deeds of my father — when a captive finds no one to support him, with none at a distance among them loyal enough to visit him — while guests crowd around him and you see no one from afar with faithfulness to seek him out. Every affair he desires is blocked for him, and his bonds are tightened until his fingers cramp. When the settled people mention their gatherings, seeing what he endures, his tears pour freely. Are we not the ones who drive white camels to his rescue through rough ground when his patron sleeps soundly in his bed? We do not cease until we ransom him with our wealth — and good deeds are praised by their maker. I remind you — for aggression destroys its people — when the drought-winter comes with howling gales and the tribe's child is given no morning drink, while his nurse withholds it from him, and the emaciated milch-camels go away hunch-backed to a grazing ground with dry pasture in the upland. Are we not the ones who slaughter the fat camels amid our luggage and tend the ally when his supporters are few? When an affair befalls him, our souls shield him, and whatever good we attain, he shares in it. I remind you — for aggression destroys its people — when the ram finds none to stand against him. Are we not the ones who match him with a host like a flood at night whose surging waves reach out? We outnumber you in it and endure its heat, and we march against its heroes and overcome them. I remind you — for aggression destroys its people — when the adversary finds none to defend him. Are we not the ones who square with him, straighten his deviation, and do not stop until the truth emerges pure? So do not disavow what we have done for you — give thanks for it, for ingratitude is ruinous to its possessor. Just as, had you done the like for them, they would have praised it — so long as a listener transmits the speech.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I remind, I adjure — solemn invocation of past deeds
a captive, a prisoner — one who needs rescue
to support, to protect, to stand by
lameness, rough ground that wounds feet of camels
fat, full-humped camels — a sign of wealth and generosity
howling, violent gusts — of winter storms
we fight him, we battle against him

context: A long fakhr poem in which Hassan addresses Banu al-Najjar — his own tribe — reminding them of his father's and his own tribe's deeds of generosity: ransoming captives, feeding the hungry in winter, defending the oppressed, and championing justice. The repeated refrain 'aggression destroys its people' gives the poem a structural rhythm. This poem exemplifies the tribal virtue poetry that defined the social function of Arab poetry in Hassan's era.

titleEn: By God Ma'is Does Not Know Mecca's Terrain
translationEn: By God, Ma'is does not know whether the floor of Mecca or its heights are easier — and every Muharib and Banu Nizar — the marks of suckling are plain on their lips. Neither Jumah, even if you mention them, nor Taym — that is your rabble. For baseness is clearly manifest in them when it comes to battle-encounters and sword fights. And Makhzum, they and 'Adiyy Ka'b — the vile of mankind, they have no defense.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Ma'is — a man or clan being addressed/satirized
elevated ground, heights — used in locational reference
lips, especially of animals; here used for human lips mockingly
suckling — marks of being nursed, implying servile origin
Jumah — a clan of Quraysh being satirized

context: A satire against several Qurayshi clans — Muharib, Nizar, Jumah, Taym, Makhzum, and 'Adiyy Ka'b — attacking their cowardice in battle and implying servile or non-Arab origins through the 'suckling marks' insult. Hassan composed many poems attacking Qurayshi clans on behalf of the Prophet, and this poem is typical of his systematic satirical campaign against the enemies of the early Muslim community.

titleAr: لقد أتى عن بني الجرباء قولهم ،
titleEn: The Words of Banu al-Jarba Have Reached Me

verses.sadr: لقد أتى عن بني الجرباء قولهم ،
قد علمت أسلم الأنذال أن لها

translationEn: The words of Banu al-Jarba' have reached me — the rocky plateau of Jamdan stands between us and them, so let it stay. Aslam — the vile ones — has come to know that their neighbor will be killed in his house by hunger. And that lineage will prevent them from what they intended — one who is cut off will never attain glory and the heights. They have renounced their sister for me, they claim — while my lineage is at the peaks and glory is raised high.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Banu al-Jarba — a clan, the 'mangy ones', a derogatory name
the rocky plateau/ridge of Jamdan, a geographical landmark
Aslam — the vile ones; نذل means vile, contemptible person
one who is cut off — from lineage, kinship, tribe
the peaks, the summits — metaphor for highest nobility

context: A short poem of tribal pride and contempt for the Banu al-Jarba' and tribe of Aslam who apparently refused a marriage offer from Hassan or mocked him. Hassan asserts his superior lineage and predicts their poverty and downfall. The poem encapsulates the honor dynamics of early Islamic tribal society, where marriage negotiations were deeply tied to tribal status.

titleEn: Time Has Come for a Famous Poem

verses.ajuz: بطن إذا ما جارهم لم يشبع

translationEn: The time has come to say a renowned poem — an ugly, cutting one I have prepared for a people who are sucklings. My chest boils with it and I weave it well — I think it will be recited if it is not suppressed. Quraysh has taken glory, while you walk with the gait of shameless harlots. Abandon obscenity and restrain your backsides — and walk on the open highway. You are the remnants of the people of Lot — know it — and your hermaphrodites are pointed at with fingers. And when Quraysh reckons its lineages, boast among the assembly of the clan of Shuja'. Holes — the helpless when battle is joined in earnest — mean-bellied when their neighbor goes hungry.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ugly, hideous, scandalous — describing the satire itself
sucklings, those who are still nursing — implying immaturity/baseness
hermaphrodites, the effeminate — used as a severe insult
those without weapons or strength, helpless in battle

context: One of Hassan's most aggressive satirical poems, targeting a Qurayshi sub-clan with accusations of sexual deviance, effeminacy, cowardice, and ignoble lineage. The reference to 'people of Lot' is a Quranic allusion (Lot's people were destroyed for homosexuality), making this a theologically charged insult. Hassan presents himself self-consciously as a poet unleashing a devastating composition, a meta-poetic opening that signals his confidence in his craft.

titleAr: بني القين هلا إذ فخرتم بربعكم
titleEn: O Banu al-Qayn Why Do You Boast of Your Quarter

verses.sadr: بني القين هلا إذ فخرتم بربعكم
verses.ajuz: فخرتم بكير عند باب ابن جندع

translationEn: O Banu al-Qayn, when you boast of your quarter, why do you not boast of the bellows at the door of Ibn Junda'? Your father built it — before building his own house — at Hirs, and they concealed the mention of a smith who was expelled. They threw the forge's ashes to be recognized among you at a gathering of yours — vile and shameful.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Banu al-Qayn — tribe whose name means 'the smiths'
the bellows of a forge — symbol of lowly smithcraft
a blacksmith — considered a lowly profession in Arab tribal hierarchy
expelled, driven away — one thrust aside
ashes, cinders — remnant of the forge
grievous, painful, shameful — describing the disgrace

context: A short satirical poem attacking Banu al-Qayn — a tribe whose name literally means 'the smiths.' In pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, smithcraft was considered a degrading hereditary profession associated with non-Arab or low-status origins. Hassan mocks their attempt to boast of their quarter by reminding them of their ancestor's forge and the ashes of the bellows. The poem is a sharp example of genealogical satire (hija al-ansab).

titleAr: وما سارق الدرعين ، إن كنت ذاكرا ،
titleEn: The Thief of Two Coats of Mail Is No Nobleman

verses.sadr: وما سارق الدرعين ، إن كنت ذاكرا ،
ظننتم بأن يخفى الذي قد صنعتم ،
فإن تذكروا كعبا إذا ما نسيتم ،
verses.ajuz: فهل من أديم ليس فيه أكارعه

verses.sadr: هم الرأس ، والأذناب في الناس أنتم ،
verses.ajuz: فلم تك إلا في الرؤوس مسامعه

translationEn: The thief of two coats of mail — if you must mention him — is no generous nobleman with good manners. The daughter of Sa'd has sheltered him, and she struggled with the skin of her seat as he struggled back. Why did you not come to Usayd, your neighbor, seeking him out willingly rather than intending to oppose and contest him? You thought what you did would be hidden — while among us is a Prophet with revelation set down by him. Were it not for men among you whose feelings would be hurt by my satire, it would already have fallen upon you in its rising signs. If you mention Ka'b when you have forgotten — is there any hide without its lower legs? They are the head, and you are the tails among people — and the ears were never found except in the heads.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the thief of two coats of mail — a specific shaming incident
his manners, his self-restraint, his gentle nature
to contest with him, to litigate against him
its rising signs — the full force of the satire's impact
a full hide/skin — used in a proverb about completeness
its lower legs, the extremities of the hide

context: A satire referencing a specific incident — the theft of two coats of mail — as evidence of a clan's dishonor. Hassan invokes the Prophet's presence in Medina as a guarantee that nothing can be hidden, and uses the classic Arab proverb about heads and tails to assert that Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf's tribe (or a related clan) is the 'head' while the satirized group are mere 'tails.' This poem blends personal invective with Islamic apologetic.

titleEn: How Excellent the Band That Met Them

verses.ajuz: يا ابن الحقيق ، وأنت يا ابن الأشرف

translationEn: How excellent — praise be to God for them — a band that met you, O Ibn al-Huqayq and you, O Ibn al-Ashraf! They advance with thin, gleaming blades toward you, swaggering like lions in a shaded lair. Until they came to you in the very dwelling of your lands and gave you to drink of death with gleaming swords that made them shudder. Seeking with clear vision the support of their Prophet's religion, considering every grave hardship as trivial.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a band of men, a group of warriors
Ibn al-Huqayq — Sallam ibn Abi al-Huqayq, a Jewish leader assassinated
Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, a Jewish poet who satirized the Prophet and was killed
thin, gleaming swords — the finest blades
shaded, abundant — describing a lion's lair with shade
making one shudder — describing a cold or terrifying death blow
clear-sighted, acting with full conviction and insight

context: A praise poem celebrating the Muslim assassins who killed Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf and Sallam ibn Abi al-Huqayq — Jewish leaders who had actively worked against the Muslim community in Medina (7th century CE). Hassan praises the small bands of warriors who carried out these politically motivated assassinations as acts of religious devotion. The poem reflects the partisan nature of early Islamic poetry and its role in communal memory and justification.

titleEn: Whose House and Effaced Traces Remain

verses.sadr: دار خود تشفي الضجيع بعذب ال

translationEn: Whose house are these, and the effaced traces, between Sla' and the striped rocks of al-Azaf? The house of a beautiful maiden who heals the sleeper with sweet-tasting, pleasantly sour, and cool drink like aged wine. When you see her in her state of abandon and dishevelment — she is like a pearl within oyster shells.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the effaced traces, the obliterated marks of an old campsite
the striped rocky terrain of al-Azaf — a Medinan landmark
a beautiful, youthful woman, a maiden of grace
pleasantly sour — describing a quality of taste
aged, first-pressed wine — the finest wine
pearl within oyster shells — beauty hidden within roughness

context: A brief and elegant nasib fragment evoking a deserted encampment between the mountains of Sla' and the rocky terrain of al-Azaf near Medina. The beloved is compared in her natural state to a pearl within its shell — a classic image of hidden beauty. The three-verse poem represents the concentrated lyric mode that appears alongside Hassan's longer qasidas, showing his versatility in scale.

titleEn: We Cut the Ears of Ka'b and Amir

verses.ajuz: بقتل ابن كعب ثم حزت أنوفها

verses.sadr: وحاز ابن عبد ، إذ هوى في رماحنا ،
أصيبت به فهر ، فلا انجبرت لها

translationEn: We have cut the ears of Ka'b and Amir by killing Ibn Ka'b and then cut off their noses. Their ram and their massed forces turned away defeated — standing firm in separate groups, with their ranks unblamed. Ibn Abd claimed his place when he fell upon our spears — such are fates, their moment and their doom. Fihr was struck by that — may her afflictions never heal, her heat and her grief having faded. And another at Badr where their hope was dashed — their arrows and swords availed them nothing. And yet another coming soon, from which there is no turning — its noise and its rushing sound deafen the herald.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: cut off ears — metaphor for humiliating defeat
butted, gore-wounded — of a ram struck repeatedly
separate groups, scattered bands — describing fleeing troops
grief's biting pain, the sharp ache of sorrow
a rushing, swishing sound — of an approaching army
deafening the herald — overwhelming noise of coming battle

context: A poem of tribal boast (fakhr) and prophetic warning against the clans of Ka'b and Amir, referencing their defeats at Badr (624 CE) and threatening further battles. Hassan employs the metaphor of 'cutting ears and noses' — a common Arabic idiom for humiliation — and alludes to a coming third defeat. The poem serves as both a battle celebration and a psychological intimidation against Quraysh and allied tribes.

titleAr: لو أن اللؤم ينسب كان عبدا
titleEn: If Baseness Had a Lineage It Would Be a Slave

verses.sadr: لو أن اللؤم ينسب كان عبدا

translationEn: If baseness had a lineage it would be a slave — ugly of face, one-eyed, from Thaqif. You abandoned religion and faith through ignorance the morning you met the woman with the half-veil. And you returned to youthful folly and recalled amusements — from the bowels, and the slender waist.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one-eyed — a mark of ugliness and ill-omen
the tribe of Thaqif — often satirized by Hassan
the half-veil, a woman's head covering revealing one side
youth, youthful folly, early life pleasures
the bowels, the inner organs — seat of emotion and desire
the slender waist — a standard beauty description

context: A short satirical poem combining an abstract opening (if baseness had a lineage) with an attack on a specific person from Thaqif. The second half describes the person's abandonment of religion after being captivated by a woman — perhaps a specific historical incident. The title verse became proverbial in Arabic literature as a maxim about personified baseness. Thaqif was a tribe of Ta'if, rivals of Medina.

titleEn: Did Banu Bakr Think the Prophet's Letter a Trifle
translationEn: Did Banu Bakr think the letter of Muhammad was like their own random shooting from Awfad and Rasaf? You are more fitting to carry and collect shameful deeds than to gather yourselves for chastity. They spoke against the Prophet's script and woke up bound in the sandals of hatred and filth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the letter/document of Muhammad — a prophetic treaty or letter
their random shooting, scattered archery — implying disorder
place names associated with Banu Bakr's territory
bound together, entangled — here in sin and disgrace

context: A poem attacking Banu Bakr for their disrespect or violation of a treaty or letter from the Prophet Muhammad. Hassan mocks their comparison of the Prophet's authoritative document to their own random, inconsequential affairs. The poem ends with an image of them 'bound in sandals of hatred and filth' — a striking metaphor for their entrapment in sin. This reflects the political role of poetry in early Islamic diplomacy and conflict.

titleEn: O Mal the Turbaned Chief May Be Led Astray

verses.sadr: يا مال والحق إن قنعت به
خالفت في الرأي كل ذي فجر ،

translationEn: O Mal, the turbaned chief may be led to arrogance by some of his opinion's excess. We are satisfied with what we have and you with what you have — yet the opinion differs. O Mal, if you are content with truth, then truth in this matter is half of our affair. You have opposed in opinion every person of discernment — for Bujayr is a client of your people.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: O Mal — addressing someone by name (Malik/Mal abbreviated)
the turbaned chief — a mark of leadership and dignity
it makes him arrogant, puffed up with pride
truth, right, justice — a central Islamic value
a person of discernment, intelligence, wisdom
Bujayr — a client-ally whose status is being debated

context: A poem of diplomatic counsel and arbitration addressed to a chief named Mal (Malik), discussing a disputed matter involving a client-ally named Bujayr. Hassan positions himself as a mediator who urges the chief to be content with truth rather than arrogance. The poem reflects the social role of the poet in early Islamic tribal society as a negotiator, adviser, and voice of communal wisdom beyond mere praise or satire.

titleEn: Umayya ibn Khalaf Satirized Hassan
translationEn: Is there not one who will convey from me to Hassan a creeping message that will reach Ukaz? Was not your father among us a smith — beside the singing-girls — neglecting his dignity? A Yemeni who spent his days tightening a bellows and endlessly blowing the blaze of fire.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a message that creeps and spreads secretly
the great pre-Islamic poetry fair and market of Arabia
a blacksmith — a lowly profession in Arab tribal hierarchy
he neglected, he let go — his sense of honor
the forge bellows — symbol of the smith's trade
blazing flame, the heat of fire

context: The satire of Umayya ibn Khalaf al-Khuza'i against Hassan ibn Thabit, preserved in the diwan as the poetic exchange that prompted Hassan's famous reply (poem hassan-133). Umayya attacks Hassan's father as a lowly Yemeni blacksmith who neglected his honor beside singing-girls. This sets up one of the celebrated poetic exchanges (munafara) of early Islamic literature, with Hassan's devastating counter-satire preserved in the previous poem. The poem illustrates how poets served as tribal champions in verbal warfare.

titleEn: Deliver to Banu Juhjaба and Their People
translationEn: Deliver to Banu Juhjaба and their people, to Khatma — I am behind them, proud. And that we, against what they would impose on them, are enemies who ward off every oppressive scheme. We cleave their skulls with the flat edge of the blade, and we have cloven their skulls with it — unjust as they were.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a sub-tribe of the Aws in Medina
proud, noble; the nose (symbol of pride)
what they would impose on them; to drive to hardship
the flat of a sword blade

context: A short verse-message directed at Banu Juhjaба and their allies from Khatma (a branch of the Aws), warning them that the Khazraj stand as protectors of the oppressed and will repel any scheme against them. Hassan's poem serves as a political warning combining tribal allegiance with the threat of military force, reflecting the ongoing tensions between Ansar clans in early Medina.

titleEn: Why Does Your Eye Restrain Its Tears

verses.ajuz: من ذكر خود شطت بها قذف

verses.sadr: دع ذا وعد القريض في نفر
أو ندع في الأوس دعوة هربا ،

translationEn: Why does your eye restrain its tears at the memory of a graceful girl who was carried far away into remoteness — she departed with her estrangement, heading for a land other than ours, her circumstance changed. I did not know how swift her parting would be until I saw the litters had already moved on. They left me, my soul overwhelmed by what had not yet healed it, while sorrows gathered in retreat. Leave that, and return to verse among a group who call upon my glory — and my praise is their honor. If you summon my people to glory, you find them people of deeds that shine when they are described. Convey from me to al-Nabit a verse that humiliates them — for they swore allegiance to us. By God, we solemnly swear we will kill you — a violent killing, while the cavalry breaks open. Or we call in the Aws a battle-cry of pursuit, when the battalion's half has already shown itself. You were our slaves whom we showed favor to — whoever came to us — and slaves are doubly weak. How dare you foolishly contend with our glory, when you are a lineage with only a palm? Your ancestor shamed you, while our ancestor honored us — an ancestor in deeds who is equitable. We make those whose glory is their origin to be like the slaves of the Aws whenever they are described. Why did you not get angry for the slaves who were killed on the day of Bu'ath — wretchedness loomed over them? We killed them, the swords taking them — a violent taking — while you were exposed. How many a chief of yours have we slain in a regiment that brings destruction upon itself! And how many a vile slave who allies himself with you, possessing no lineage and no honor! For Sumayr is a slave who transgressed in foolishness — aided by slaves filthy in origin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: howdahs, camel litters for women traveling
to gather, to confine itself; also: to perform i'tikaf
a sub-tribe of the Aws, the Ansar's rival clan
to seek, to hunt for; brings upon itself
the Battle of Bu'ath, a major pre-Islamic battle between Aws and Khazraj
a regiment, a large military division

context: A complex multi-part poem that opens with a nasib (amorous prelude) about a departing beloved, then sharply pivots to a boastful attack on the Aws and specifically the sub-tribe of al-Nabit. Hassan recalls the Battle of Bu'ath (618 CE), the last major battle between the Aws and Khazraj before the Prophet's arrival, and taunts the Aws with their defeat. The poem also attacks Sumayr, an adversary mentioned elsewhere in the diwan.

titleAr: ألم ترنا أولاد عمرو بن عامر ،
titleEn: Do You Not See Us, Sons of Amr ibn Amir

verses.sadr: ألم ترنا أولاد عمرو بن عامر ،
verses.ajuz: لنا شرف يعلو على كل مرتقي

verses.sadr: رسا في قرار الأرض ثم سمت له
إذا غاب منها كوكب لاح بعده
verses.ajuz: شهاب متى ما يبد للأرض تشرق

verses.sadr: أولئك لا الأوغاد في كل مأقط ،

verses.ajuz: وضرب يزيل الهام من كل مفرق
له الأرض ، يرميه بها كل موفق
كتائب إن لا تغد للروع تطرق

verses.sadr: فكنا له من سائر الناس معقلا

verses.ajuz: بها كل أظمى ذي غرارين ، أزرق

verses.sadr: نفى الذم عنا كل يوم كريهة ،

verses.ajuz: بما كان من إل علينا وموثق

verses.sadr: فنحن ولاة الناس في كل موطن ،
verses.ajuz: متى ما نقل في الناس قولا نصدق
إذا غيرهم ، في مثلها ، لم يوفق

translationEn: Do you not see us — sons of Amr ibn Amir — we have a glory that rises above every summit? It is rooted in the depths of the earth, then its branches soar to rival every orbiting star. Kings and sons of kings — we are like rising stars ascending in the east. When one star sets among them, another shooting star appears that makes the earth blaze when it shows. For every noble who produces a noble, enriched by them — a refined bloodline never burdened. Like Jafna and al-Qamqam Amr ibn Amir, and the sons of the water of clouds, and the two sons of Muhrriq. And Haritha al-Ghitrif, or like Ibn al-Mundhir, and the like of Abu Qabus, lord of al-Khawarnaq. Those — not the base ones — in every battlefield they turn back the reach of the flashing cloud-burst. With thrusts like the gushing of a she-camel in labor, its spattering, and blows that remove the helmets from every crown. The Messenger of God came to us when the land frowned at him, every righteous guide launching him toward us. Driven away by the clans of Qays and Khandaq — battalions that if not attacked in the morning, would raid at dusk. And we became for him, above all other people, a lofty, impregnable fortress of jutting, towering peaks. Crowned with swords of Musharraf and with spears — in it every dark-edged double-bladed blue sword. The Khazraj women defend their land with them — like the lions of Kara, or like an embroidered garden. Aided by the Awsi Malikiyya — thin and sharp swords, like streaks, keen. Every day of hardship removed blame from us — thrusting like the kindling of burning reeds. And our honoring of our guests, and our faithfulness to every compact and binding covenant made upon us. We are the custodians of the people in every situation — whenever a word is transmitted among the people, we affirm it. Our wise men succeed in their judgments when others, in similar matters, do not succeed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a summit, a high point; a place of ascent
a battlefield, a place of fierce combat
the scattered clans of the Qays tribes
swords made in Musharraf (South Arabia), renowned for quality
bright red streaks (of lightning or fine blades)

context: One of Hassan's grandest fakhr poems, tracing the Ansar's lineage back to Amr ibn Amir, the legendary founder of South Arabian tribes who settled Medina. He catalogues legendary kings of Arab history — the Ghassanids (Jafna), the Lakhmids (Ibn al-Mundhir, Abu Qabus), Haritha al-Ghitrif — as ancestral parallels. The poem then pivots to the Ansar's sheltering of the Prophet Muhammad and their martial role in Islam, making it a masterwork of Islamic tribal fakhr.

titleAr: ما بال عينك لا ترقا مدامعها ،
titleEn: Why Does Your Eye Not Stop Its Flowing Tears

verses.sadr: ما بال عينك لا ترقا مدامعها ،
verses.ajuz: سحا على الصدر ، مثل اللؤلؤ الفلق
لا فشل حين تلقاه ولا نزق
وجنة الخلد عند الحور في الرفق

verses.sadr: ماذا تقولون ، إن قال النبي لكم ،
فيما قتلتم شهيد الله في رجل
verses.ajuz: طاغ قد أوعث في البلدان والطرق

verses.sadr: لا تذكرن ، إذا ما كنت مفتخرا ،
verses.ajuz: أبا كثيبة ! قد أسرفت في الحمق

translationEn: Why does your eye not stop its flowing tears — pouring down the chest like split pearls — for Khubaib? His death was in the service of the Merciful: no cowardice when he met them, no rashness. Go forth, Khubaib — may God reward you with Tayyiba (Medina) and the eternal garden in the company of the houris in ease. What will you say if the Prophet says to you, when the righteous angels are on the horizon: 'For what cause did you slay God's martyr, because of a man who was a tyrant who had spread corruption across lands and roads?' O Abu Ihab — tell me your story: where is the gazelle adorned with pearls and silver? Do not boast — when you are bragging — of Abu Kathibah! You have gone too far in foolishness. Nor of Aziz — for treachery is a defect; Aziz is of mean soul and character.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Khubaib ibn Adi, a Companion martyred at al-Raji'

context: An elegy for Khubaib ibn Adi al-Ansari, a Companion of the Prophet who was captured and crucified by the Quraysh after the tragedy of al-Raji' (625 CE). Hassan mourns him with a vision of prophetic intercession on the Day of Judgment, asking how the killers will justify executing God's martyr. He then attacks specific individuals — Abu Ihab and Abu Kathibah — who participated in or enabled the treachery.

titleEn: When God Greets a Folk Through Their Deeds

verses.sadr: فأخزاك ربي ، يا عتيب بن مالك ،
لقد كان خزيا في الحياة لقومه ،
verses.ajuz: وفي البعث ، بعد الموت ، إحدى العوالق

translationEn: When God greets a folk through their deeds and the Merciful — Lord of the Easts — grants them victory: then may your Lord shame you, O Utayb ibn Malik — and may He strike you with a thunderbolt before death. You extended your right hand against the Prophet with a throw — may your mouth be deadened; you were cut apart by lightning. Why did you not fear God and the destination to which you will return after one of the great calamities? He was a disgrace in life to his people, and in the resurrection, after death, one of the worst burdens.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Easts, the places of sunrise; all the horizons

context: A curse-poem (hija') against Utayb ibn Malik, who attacked or attempted to harm the Prophet Muhammad. Hassan calls down divine punishment — a thunderbolt before death — and warns him of the disgrace that awaits him both in life and on the Day of Resurrection. The poem reflects the genre of imprecatory verse that Hassan wielded as a spiritual weapon in defense of the Prophet.

titleEn: Poetry Is the Mind of a Man Displayed

verses.ajuz: على المجالس إن كيسا وإن حمقا

translationEn: Poetry is but the mind of a man, displayed before gatherings — whether it reveals wit or folly. The most poetic verse you can utter is one that, when you recite it, is met with: 'True!'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: most poetic, most expressive in verse

context: A brief but striking aphorism on the nature of poetry from Hassan ibn Thabit. He defines poetry as the mirror of a man's intellect — revealing wisdom or exposing foolishness — and asserts that the highest verse is one whose truth resonates immediately with the audience. This reflects the early Islamic view of poetry as morally meaningful self-expression before the community.

titleEn: We Camped by the Exhausted Well for Many Nights

verses.sadr: إذا ارتحلوا من منزل خلت أنه
ذروا فلجات الشأم ، قد حال دونها
إذا سلكت للغور من رمل عالج ،

verses.ajuz: فرات بن حيان يكن وهن هالك

verses.sadr: وإن نلق قيس بن امرىء القيس بعده
verses.ajuz: نزد في سواد وجهه لون حالك

translationEn: We camped for nights at the exhausted well with a vast, heavy army of broad-chested mounts — on every chestnut horse, its mid-section half its form, lean and tall, with high haunches. You see the lush arfaj plants scattered at their roots by the hard soles of the plodding camels. When they moved from a camp, you would think it the trampled ground of a crowded pilgrimage festival. We march such that wild gazelles cannot escape from our midst, even at full fleeing speed. Abandon the lowlands of the Levant — blows like the jaws of restless she-camels stand between you and them. In the hands of men who emigrated toward their Lord — His true Helpers and the hands of the angels. If you take the lowlands through the sands of 'Alij, tell her: 'That is not the path.' If we meet Furat ibn Hayyan in our search, then woe — he shall be destroyed. If we then meet Qays ibn Imri al-Qays, we shall add yet another dark hue to the blackness of his face. Convey a message from me to Abu Sufyan: you are among the worst of the vagabond men.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: an exhausted or depleted well; dried-out water-source
a type of desert shrub common in Arabian pastures
vagabonds, paupers, the dispossessed and wretched
pregnant she-camels that are restless and difficult to manage

context: A poem of military boasting and political warning directed against enemies of the Muslims, including Abu Sufyan ibn Harb and tribal informants (Furat ibn Hayyan, Qays ibn Imri al-Qays) who were spying or collaborating against Islam. Hassan describes a vast Muslim army on campaign and threatens those who stand against them. The poem belongs to the genre of political fakhr composed in service of the Prophet.

titleAr: فإن تك عنا ، معشر الأسد ، سائلا ،
titleEn: If You Ask About Us, O Tribe of Asad

verses.sadr: فإن تك عنا ، معشر الأسد ، سائلا ،
verses.ajuz: فنحن بنو الغوث بن زيد بن مالك

verses.sadr: لزيد بن كهلان الذي نال عزه
وجدت لنا فضلا يقر لنا به ،
verses.ajuz: إذا ما فخرنا ، كل باق وهالك

translationEn: If you ask about us, O tribe of Asad — we are the sons of al-Ghawth ibn Zayd ibn Malik, of Zayd ibn Kahlan, whose glory long ago reached the bright, interlocking stars. When the people enumerate their glory, deeds, and memorable days at the gathering of pilgrimage rites, you find for us a superiority acknowledged — whenever we boast — by every living man and every dead one.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the tribe of Asad, a major Arab tribe
interlocking, intertwined (of stars in dense clusters)
pilgrimage rites; gathering places during Hajj
one who has perished; the dead

context: A short genealogical boast tracing the Ansar's lineage to al-Ghawth ibn Zayd and the ancient South Arabian ancestor Zayd ibn Kahlan. Hassan challenges the tribe of Asad to acknowledge the Ansar's supremacy in lineage, a superiority affirmed even at pilgrimage gatherings where all Arab tribes converge. A compact example of the nasab-fakhr subgenre.

titleEn: Let My Mother Be a Ransom for All of Awf
translationEn: May my mother be a ransom for all of Awf and the sons of al-Abyad on the day of retribution — they defended me from oppression with unerring blows beneath torn edges of their mail-coats, with fingers whose tips protrude and legs that spin like the wheel.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ransom, sacrifice — the highest expression of gratitude
the day of reckoning; lower depths of consequence
my oppression, the injustice done to me
accurate, unerring (of a blow or strike)
long shirts; mail-coats in battle context
torn, ripped (of garments under battle stress)

context: A brief poem of gratitude and praise directed at the tribe of Awf and the sons of al-Abyad who defended Hassan against an oppressor. The formulaic 'may my mother be a ransom' is among the highest expressions of gratitude in classical Arabic rhetoric. The poem is in the ramal meter, a lyrical form often used for praise and lament.

titleEn: Who Will Deliver a Message to Hassan from Me
translationEn: Who will deliver a message from me to Hassan: 'You have betrayed my father, but you have not betrayed your own father.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who delivers, a messenger, a conveyor
you betrayed, you broke faith with, you let down
to betray, to fail, to break a promise
your own father (with accusative case enclitic)

context: A single-verse lampoon preserved in the diwan as part of a verbal exchange (muhajahat). The speaker accuses Hassan of betraying a father-figure while defending his own lineage. Such single-verse tit-for-tat exchanges were common in early Arabic poetry and were transmitted as standalone epigrams. This verse is answered by the next poem in the diwan (hassan-166).

titleEn: My Father's Faithfulness Runs Deep
translationEn: My father's loyalty runs deep indeed — while your father is just like you, no different from you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his faithfulness, his trustworthiness; his succession
like you, resembling you in character
he is no different from you; he did not surpass you

context: Hassan's retort to the preceding single-verse lampoon (hassan-165), affirming the nobility and faithfulness of his own father while turning the accusation back on his opponent — implying the opponent's father shares the same defects. Together, poems 165 and 166 form a classic verbal duel (muhajahat), each a complete stand-alone poem.

titleAr: إذا تذكرت شجوا من أخي ثقة ،
titleEn: When You Remember Sorrow from a Trusted Brother

verses.sadr: إذا تذكرت شجوا من أخي ثقة ،
verses.ajuz: فاذكر أخاك أبا بكر بما فعلا

verses.sadr: والثاني اثنين في الغار المنيف ، وقد
verses.ajuz: طاف العدو به إذ صعد الجبلا

verses.sadr: وكان حب رسول الله قد علموا ،
verses.ajuz: من البرية لم يعدل به رجلا

translationEn: When you recall sorrow from a trusted brother, remember your brother Abu Bakr and what he did — the second praised companion, whose station is lauded, the first of all people to affirm the messengers. The second of two in the lofty Cave, while the enemy circled around when he climbed the mountain. He was the beloved of the Messenger of God — as they knew — from all creation; no man was held equal to him. The best of creation — its most enduring and most compassionate after the Prophet, the most faithful to what he carried. He lived in praise, following God's command, upon the guidance of his departed companion, and he never deviated.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sorrow, grief, longing for the lost
the second follower; the companion ranked second to the Prophet
the lofty cave — Cave of Thawr, refuge during the Hijra
the most compassionate, most tender-hearted of them
the most faithful and loyal of them

context: An encomium for Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first Caliph and closest companion of the Prophet. Hassan praises his unique distinction as the first believer, the companion in the Cave of Thawr during the Hijra, and the most faithful inheritor of the Prophet's guidance. The poem reflects the Ansar's deep veneration for Abu Bakr and likely dates from after his death (634 CE).

titleEn: O Raven of Separation, You Heard — Now Speak
translationEn: O raven of separation, you have heard — now speak! You only utter what has already come to pass. Good and evil each have their limit, and both have a face and a direction. Gifts are paltry among them, and the grave of the rich man and the poor man are alike. Every life and every comfort is passing, and the daughters of time play with every man. Deliver a sign from me to Hassan: the composition of poetry heals the one with burning resentment. How many skulls you see in the dry riverbed, and hands that have been severed, and legs! Fine mail-coats have been stripped from brave warriors who perished in the descent. How many a noble chief we slew — honored on both sides of his lineage, bold and heroic — a man of true courage, a surpassing leader, never distressed by the thrust of spears. Would that my elders had witnessed at Badr the Khazraj's grief from the falling of spears. Ask the stone basin: who inhabits it now, after the skulls and heads scattered like partridges?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the raven of separation — a classical omen of parting
burning thirst; burning resentment, inner bitterness
the stone water basin at the hill of Uhud
partridges — a symbol of scattered, panicked flight

context: A poem that opens with the classical nasib motif of the raven of separation, then shifts to philosophical reflection on mortality before becoming a boast about battle victories. The reference to al-Mihras (the stone basin at Uhud) and the Khazraj's grief at Badr links it to the major early Islamic battles. A third voice interrupts to ask Hassan to convey a verse, revealing the poem's dialogic structure.

titleEn: The Battle That Seized Ibn al-Zub'ari

verses.ajuz: كان منا الفضل فيها لو عدل
مثل ما جمع في الخصب الهمل
نحن في البأس إذا البأس نزل

translationEn: A battle carried off Ibn al-Zub'ari — in it the virtue was ours, if justice were done. You dealt us blows and we dealt you blows: such is war, at times it turns. When we pressed with a true charge, we drove you to the mountain's slope — fleeing on your heels through the pass, like messenger-doves. We placed the Khatt-spears in your backs wherever we wished, drinking blow after blow. We felled seventy of you in a single stand — not counting deserters — and captured their equal as prisoners; you retreated like scattered partridges. They escaped us for not even an hour — they only fled in ignorance and cowardice. The pass grew too narrow for us when we crossed it, and we filled the high ground and foothills with them. With men who are not your equals — supported by Gabriel, who descended with victory. We prevailed at Badr through piety, obedience to God, and affirmation of the Messengers — with bold squadrons like the valley's snakes; whoever meets them is undone. We left a scandal in Quraysh on the day of Badr, and tales become proverbs. We left their gathering like stray cattle in rich pasture. We slew every chieftain among them and every flowing-robed noble. How many a generous chief we slew — of honored lineage, bold and heroic — and a nobleman by blood of a noble, whom we cared nothing for at the clash of spears. We — not you, O sons of degradation — we are the valiant when valor descends.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: battle, a decisive clash of forces
we felled, we brought down flat (in battle)
partridges — symbol of panicked, scattered flight

context: One of Hassan's most aggressive fakhr poems, directed at the Qurayshi poet Ibn al-Zub'ari and his tribe after the Battle of Badr (624 CE). Hassan recounts the Muslim victory, the flight of the Quraysh, and the role of Gabriel in securing Muslim dominance. The unusually graphic language reflects the raw energy of early Islamic tribal invective combined with the new spiritual legitimacy of divine aid.

titleEn: Soft-Sandaled Men of Noble Virtue

verses.ajuz: ولا يحسبون الخير لا شر بعده ،
حبوت بها غسان إذ كنت لاحقا

translationEn: Men of soft sandals, virtuous in their innermost character — white-skinned maidens greet them in their midst. Cloaks of fine red wool hang on their garment-stands; they protect bodies long accustomed to comfort, in pure-fringed garments with green-shouldered robes. They do not assume good is followed by no evil, nor do they count evil as an immovable fate. I bestowed this ode upon Ghassan when I was gathering with my people and all paths had grown difficult for me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: men of soft, thin sandals — a mark of refined luxury
their innermost character; the area of the waist-cloth — virtue
young slave-girls, maidens of the household
a type of fine red or crimson cloth
an unavoidable, immovable blow — an inevitable fate
the Ghassanid dynasty, Arab Christian kings of Byzantine Syria

context: A panegyric for the Ghassanid kings of Syria, composed before Hassan's conversion to Islam. The Ghassanids were Arab Christian vassals of Byzantium and among the most celebrated patrons of pre-Islamic Arabic poetry. Hassan describes their luxury, wisdom, and magnanimity. The closing couplet is a rare autobiographical note: he composed this when cut off from his own people and had no other recourse.

titleAr: أسألت رسم الدار أم لم تسأل
titleEn: Did You Ask the Traces of the Dwelling, or Did You Not

verses.sadr: أسألت رسم الدار أم لم تسأل

verses.ajuz: في قصر دومة ، أو سواء الهيكل
من دون والده ، وإن لم يسأل

translationEn: Did you ask the traces of the dwelling, or did you not — between al-Jawabi, then al-Badi', then Hawmal, then the meadow of Marj al-Safrayn, then Jasim, then the dwellings of Salma, worn away, no longer inhabited? Traces that the winds have swept over and obliterated — and the rain-laden clouds from Simak al-A'zal. A home belonging to a people I once saw with glory atop all that is glorious — glory that has not been transferred. What a fine band those were, whom I once banqueted a day in Jillik in a former age! They walked in garments of doubled weave, walking as bull-camels walk toward she-camels. They struck the helmeted warrior — his helmet gleaming — blows that flung the fingers of the joint. They mingled their poor with their rich, and bestowed gifts upon the weak and destitute. Sons of Jafna around their father's grave — the grave of Ibn Mariya, the noble and generous — so visited that their dogs no longer bark; they do not ask who the arriving guest is. They gave to drink from the flowing Barada river — as it splashed with smooth, mellow wine. They poured the antidote of fine wine; their maidens were never called to split bitter colocynth. Fair of face, noble in lineage, high-nosed, of the first and finest order. I spent long ages among them, then I remembered — as if I had never done so. If you see my head has changed color — gray now, white as mountain-thyme. My trysts see me as if I were in the castle of Duma or the temple precinct. I have drunk wine in its tavern — yellowish, clear, with the taste of pepper — a dripping youth hastened toward me with its cup and gave me a double portion, even without my asking. She who offered it and I returned it: 'It has killed, it has killed! Bring it — it has not killed!' Both the pressed grape and the filtered — pass it in the vessel most relaxing to the joint. A vessel that danced with what was in its bottom, like a young she-camel under an impatient rider. My lineage is deep-rooted among the noble, and my fireplace brands the bellies of those who warm themselves by its south wind. We have taken charge of the tribe's affairs and lead on days of calamity. Our chief lords over great chiefs; our speaker strikes the exact mark. We handle the most important matters for them and untangle every knotted affair. Our riders' mounts visit the gates of kings, and when we judge among the people, we judge justly. A young man who loves praise gives his wealth before his very father, even unasked. I hastened to its pleasure without delay — with a vessel of the finest trailing vine.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: traces, ruins of an abandoned dwelling — the atlal
ruins and remnants of old encampments
garments of doubled, fine weave — expensive brocade
mountain-thyme plant bleached white by seasons — metaphor for gray hair
pure, fine wine; the choicest of wines
antidote; here: the 'cure-all' of fine wine
the exact center of the joint — precision in speech and judgment

context: One of Hassan's most celebrated pre-Islamic qasidas, praising the Ghassanid kings of Syria. It follows the classical tripartite structure: atlal nasib, description of the Ghassanid court at Jillik (Damascus region), and a fakhr section. The poem praises the Ghassanids' generosity, nobility, and martial valor. The wine-drinking section describes pre-Islamic court pleasures. Considered one of the masterpieces of the diwan and a model of the panegyric genre.

titleEn: Did the Traces of the Abodes Stir You in the Wilderness

verses.ajuz: نعم قد عفاها كل أسحم هاطل
فلم يبق منها غير أشعث ماثل

verses.sadr: ديار التي كادت ، ونحن على منى ،

verses.ajuz: تأر قليلا ، سل بنا في القبائل
إذا اختارهم في الأمن أو في الزلازل

verses.sadr: وفينا إذا ما شبت الحرب سادة
وفي أحد يوم لهم كان مخزيا ،

verses.ajuz: وكائن ترى من مشفق غير وائل

translationEn: Did the traces of the abodes stir you in the wilderness? Yes — every dark pouring cloud has obliterated them. The sweeping winds have trailed their skirts over them; nothing remains but a disheveled standing post. The dwelling of one whose coquetry captivated the heart, yet how hard it is that she be generous with her gifts. She has a kohl-darkened, ever-tearful eye — a nursing mother watching over ostriches grazing in the thickets. The dwelling of one who, while we were at Mina, nearly set her abode with us — were it not for the swiftness of the riding-camels. O you who strive to reach our glory — glory comes to you; rest yourself and ask around. Do two waters equate: a green, overflowing one and a stagnant brackish well? Whoever equates the tails with the peaks has compared good to falsehood. Try to reach Suhayl in the sky — you will reach us only if you grasp it with your fingertips. Are we not those who settle in our enemy's land? Ask about us among the tribes — you will find we preceded in deeds and generosity, and in wielding the spears at the earliest calamities. We preceded mankind in glory and leadership — ancient, growing, never-obscure. We have a mountain that rises above all; we stand at the top of its tallest branch. We are generous in our camps, and our youth are the most miserly of misers when it comes to obscenity. Among the best of tribes you know for a beggar and a captive bound in chains. Among the best of tribes you know as neighbors — in security or in calamity. Among us when war flares are masters — elders and tall-belted young men. We aided and sheltered the Prophet; our forebears affirmed truth from the first of speakers. Whenever the Prophet campaigned against a tribe, we flanked his two sides with spears and squadrons. On the day Quraysh came with their full force, we crushed the enemy with a deliberate tread. At Uhud — a day of shame for them — we thrust at them with long flexible spears. At the day of Thaqif, battalions marching around them with broad swords — they fled, and God strengthened His Prophet's pillar with every brave, truthful young man. They fled to their fortress and bolted; how many a frightened man was there with no refuge. They surrendered in humiliation — so woe to you, O drivers of baggage-camels! I am easy with the friend, yet I straighten the head of the arrogant man who sways. I place wealth as a shield before my honor, guarding it so it brings no pleasure to those who would consume it. What thing new does not meet decay? And what pleasure is there that shall not one day pass away?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the open, trackless wilderness; the desert
sweeping desert winds that cover and erase traces
a stagnant, brackish well of uncertain water-supply
long, flexible lances; Samhari spears renowned for quality
broad, flat swords; wide-bladed cutting weapons
baggage-camels, laden transport animals — metaphor for cowardly followers
the man who tilts his head in arrogance and sways with pride

context: One of Hassan's longest and most accomplished qasidas. Following the classical structure — nasib, comparison boast, genealogical fakhr, and military account — it covers the Ansar's service in the battles of Badr, Uhud, the Trench (Khandaq), and Hunayn (the 'day of Thaqif'). The poem is a comprehensive panegyric of the Ansar and a personal statement of Hassan's values around honor, generosity, and poetic integrity.

titleEn: Deliver My Message to Abu Makhzum
translationEn: Deliver a message from me to Abu Makhzum — for some words cannot be avoided. By your father's life, had you lingered a moment, the horsemen would have caught up with you in al-Jalil. But you wept, alone, far from home, far from any aid for the slain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: avoidance, a way out, an escape
you lingered, you stayed, you remained
a region in the Levant (Galilee); or high open ground
aid for the slain; vengeance for the murdered man

context: A short lampoon directed at Abu Makhzum, a Qurayshi figure who fled before the Muslim forces could reach him.
