Hassan taunts him for weeping helplessly, alone and far from any aid — a cowardly flight that left the slain without avengers. The poem reflects the genre of political invective that Hassan wielded in defense of the Muslim community.

titleAr: يا حار ! في سنة من نوم أولكم ،
titleEn: O Harith! Are You in the Slumber of Your Ancestors

verses.sadr: يا حار ! في سنة من نوم أولكم ،
أم كنت ، بابن زياد ، حين نقتله ،

translationEn: O Harith! Are you in the sleep that your ancestors slept — or were you, woe to you, deceived about Gabriel? Or were you, alongside Ibn Ziyad when we slew him, caught off guard in the open expanse of the earth? You said: 'We shall not be seen' — but God sees you, and among you is the one fortified with decisive verses and the Divine word — Muhammad — while God the Almighty informs him of what all speech conceals.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a light sleep, slumber; (also: a year)
in heedlessness; during an unguarded moment
the Divine word; also an ancient Arabian royal title
hidden things, secrets of the inner self

context: A polemical poem directed at a man named al-Harith, warning him against underestimating divine revelation and the Prophet's knowledge. Hassan invokes Gabriel's role in supporting the Muslims and asserts that God informs the Prophet of all hidden intentions. The poem reflects Hassan's role as propagandist-poet defending the divine mission of Islam against those who thought they could plot unseen.

titleEn: I Bear Witness That Muhammad Is God's Messenger

verses.ajuz: رسول الذي فوق السماوات من عل

verses.sadr: وأن التي بالجزع من بطن نخلة ،
verses.ajuz: ومن دانها فل من الخير معزل

verses.sadr: وأن الذي عادى اليهود ابن مريم ،
verses.ajuz: رسول أتى من عند ذي العرش مرسل

translationEn: I bear witness, by God's leave, that Muhammad is the Messenger of the One who is above the heavens on high. And that Abu Yahya and Yahya — both — have deeds accepted in His religion. And that she who stands by the idol at the bend of Nakhlah, and those near her, are cut off from all good. And that the one whom the Jews opposed — the son of Mary — was a messenger who came, sent from the Lord of the Throne. And that the brother of al-Ahqaf, when they blamed him, stood firm in the religion of God among them and remained just.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: from on high, from above the heavens
a bend in a valley; the low, inner curve of a wadi
cut off, isolated, separated from good
the Prophet Hud, sent to the 'Ad people of the Ahqaf sand dunes

context: A theological poem of shahada (testimony), in which Hassan affirms belief in Muhammad's prophethood, validates certain companions' deeds, condemns pagan idol-worship at Nakhlah, and acknowledges the prophethood of Jesus and Hud. The poem demonstrates Hassan's engagement with Islamic doctrine and his role as a witness to the faith rather than merely its poet-warrior.

titleEn: We Repelled the Wrong Done to Us Despite the Tribes

verses.sadr: ورد سراة الأوس ، إذ جاء جمعهم ،

translationEn: We repelled the wrong done to us, despite all the tribes, with a sharp blade — refined as salt, pure in its polish. We struck them until our swords violated their sanctuary, and they returned stricken with wounds from the killing. We turned back the chiefs of al-Aws when their full force came, with thrusts like the jaws of submissive, drooping she-camels. Sumayr was humbled by force — Malik's neighbor — against his will, after all his arrogance and ignorance. Ibn 'Ajlan came with a mutilated foreign slave, then turned back diminished in manhood and in mind. And Ibn 'Ajlan became an exile, as if he were a hired laborer trailing after scrawny stray she-camels.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: purely refined in its polish; freed of all impurity
violated the sanctuary; made forbidden things lawful
submissive, tamed she-camels with drooping, loose lips
by force, compulsorily, against the will
a hired laborer, a menial worker; a dependent exile

context: A fakhr poem celebrating the Khazraj's victory over the Aws and specific enemies before or during the early Islamic period. Hassan names Sumayr and Ibn 'Ajlan as adversaries and humiliates them in verse. The poem reflects the inter-tribal tensions of Medina and the martial pride of the Ansar. The simile of thrusts 'like the jaws of she-camels' is a vivid metonymy for the gaping wounds of battle.

titleEn: A Chaste, Dignified Woman Above All Suspicion

verses.sadr: عقيلة حي من لؤي بن غالب ،
فإن كنت قد قلت الذي قد زعمتم ،
وإن الذي قد قيل ليس بلائط
verses.ajuz: بها الدهر بل قول امرىء بي ماحل

verses.sadr: له رتب عال على الناس كلهم ،

translationEn: She is chaste, dignified, above all suspicion — she wakes hungry for the flesh of heedless women (she does not backbite). The wife of the best of people in religion and station — the Prophet of guidance, of noble and generous virtues. The most honored lady of a tribe descended from Lu'ayy ibn Ghalib, their endeavors noble, their glory imperishable. A refined woman whom God has made pure in her character, cleansed from every evil and falsehood. If I had said what you allege — may my hand never raise my whip to my fingers again. And what has been said shall never cling to her throughout time — rather it is the speech of a man who slanders me. How could it be otherwise, when my love while I live and my support belong to the family of the Prophet of God, the ornament of all gatherings? He has a station exalted above all people; the pretensions of the arrogant fall short of it. I see you — and may God forgive you — a free, chaste woman of the protected, free from all malice.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: chaste, virtuous, guarded (of a woman's honor)
hungry — metaphor: she hungers but does not consume others' honor
heedless women; those whose honor is prey for backbiters
the most honored, prized noble lady of her clan
a slanderer, one who plots treachery against another
chaste, protected women — a Qur'anic legal term

context: This poem defends Aisha, the Prophet's wife, against the slanders of the 'Affair of the Necklace' (hadith al-ifk, 626 CE). Hassan was initially implicated in spreading the rumor and was punished for it. This poem appears to be his declaration of innocence and his strong vindication of Aisha's honor. The phrase 'she wakes hungry for the flesh of heedless women' — meaning she never backbites — is one of the most celebrated single epithets in Arabic praise poetry. One of the most historically significant poems in the diwan.

titleEn: How Many Months and Changes Have the Abodes Known

verses.sadr: بالمستوي دون نعف القف من قطن
ما يقسم الله أقبل غير مبتئس

verses.ajuz: إذ لا يزال سفيه همه حالي

verses.sadr: والمال يغشى أناسا لا طباخ لهم ،

verses.ajuz: لا بارك الله بعد العرض في المال

verses.sadr: كم من أخي ثقة ، محض مضاربه ،
كالبدر كان على ثغر يسد به ،

translationEn: How many months and changes have the abodes known — as the covenant of the worn parchment has grown old. At the level ground below the high plateau of Qatar, then the draining channels lined with acacia and desert-thorn. They have become barren wastes where the winds race through — their pebbles set ablaze with a fierce ignition. Whatever God apportions, I accept without grief, and I sit nobly with an easy mind. What do these people seek with their deeds, when a fool's concern never leaves my throat? I have known that my nature will overcome me toward generosity — whether pauper or man of wealth. Wealth visits people without substance — as the flood visits the roots of the old dandan-tree. I protect my honor with my wealth, not letting it be soiled — may God not bless wealth after honor is gone. I contrive to gain wealth if it is lost — but for honor, if it is gone, I have no contrivance. Poverty degrades men of noble lineage, and base-born vile men are imitated. How many a trusted brother with a pure sword-edge have I parted from — neither spurned nor despising. He was like the full moon upon a frontier gap he filled — now the gap is empty of him. Then I consoled myself for him, not cringing before misfortunes, with kindness and composure.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the worn, old parchment — a papyrus document withered by age
to race, to flow freely (of winds or swift animals)
they have no substance or worth; no cooking-fire — the worthless
a type of old, dry desert shrub

context: A reflective qasida combining atlal (ruins), philosophical wisdom on fate and generosity, and a lament for a departed trusted companion. Hassan meditates on the relationship between wealth and honor — a central concern in classical Arabic ethics — concluding that honor must be protected at any cost. One of the most quotable poems of the diwan for its ethical aphorisms.

titleEn: We Were the Kings of the People Before Muhammad

verses.ajuz: فلما أتى الإسلام ، كان لنا الفضل
إله ، بأيام مضت ما لها شكل
وأكرمنا باسم مضى ما له مثل
فما عد من خير ، فقومي له أهل

verses.sadr: إذا اختبطوا لم يفحشوا في نديهم ،

verses.ajuz: تحمل ، لا غرم عليه ، ولا خذل
له ما ثوى فينا الكرامة والبذل

verses.sadr: إذا حاربوا ، أو سالموا لم يشبهوا ،

translationEn: We were the kings of the people before Muhammad — and when Islam came, the virtue became ours still. God honored us — and there is no God but He — with days that have passed without equal. Through God's victory for the Prophet and His religion, He honored us with a name gone by without comparison. Those are my people — the best of all people entire; the gate of their kindness is never locked. They build upon the generosity of those who came before — whatever good is counted, my people are worthy of it. When they mix freely they do not speak obscenely before their companions, and there is no miserliness when asked. Their one who bears blood-wit fulfills every blood-wit — no loss, and no failure. Their neighbor dwells in the heights of their home; he has, while among us, honor and generosity. Their speaker speaks truth from the first of speakers; their judgment is just and their word decisive. In war and in peace they are without peer — their war is a terror, their peace is ease. From us is the Trustee of the Muslims throughout his life — and the one whom the angels washed of his impurity.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kings of the people; rulers and tribal leaders
virtue, superiority, the better and more honored portion
a lock — metaphor for closed, restricted generosity
blood-wit payment, the collective tribal indemnity
decisive, cutting — a word that settles all disputes
the Trustee of the Muslims (Uthman ibn Affan, or another companion)
the angels washed him — reference to Hanzala, the 'one washed by angels'

context: A comprehensive madih for the Ansar, tracing their pre-Islamic greatness through to their unique honor in Islam as the Helpers of the Prophet. The closing verse references a companion washed by angels — Hanzala ibn Abi Amir, martyred in a state of ritual impurity at Uhud, washed by the angels, earning the epithet 'ghasil al-mala'ika.' A masterwork of collective tribal praise that places the Ansar's identity at the center of Islamic history.

titleEn: Do You Recognize the Dwelling Whose Traces Have Faded

verses.sadr: دع عنك دارا قد عفا رسمها ،

translationEn: Do you recognize the dwelling whose traces have faded after you — swept by the pouring, abundant rain between al-Saradih, then Udmanah, then the outflow of al-Rawha' in Ha'il? I questioned it about that, and it fell silent — it did not know what answer to give the questioner. Leave behind a dwelling whose traces have faded, and weep instead for Hamza — the giver of gifts — who filled the wooden bowls when a dusty, barren wind swept through. He who left his foe at his lion-mat, stumbling over the shaft of a quivering spear. He who mounted the cavalry when they flinched — like a lion in his grove, bold. The white one at the peak of Hashim, who never veered from truth into falsehood. What a martyr lies between your spears — may the hands of Wahshi, his killer, be paralyzed! A man left upon a sharpened, flexible spear — the earth darkened at his loss and the light of the pale moon turned black. May God's blessing be upon you in a lofty garden, one that honors whoever enters it. We used to see Hamza as our fortress against every calamity that descended upon us. He was in Islam a great defender — never wavering, never letting anyone down. Do not rejoice, O Hind! — shed tears, and let flow the tear of the bereaved. Weep for Utba instead, whom Hamza cut down with the sword beneath the swirling dust — when he fell amid your elders, every one of them an arrogant, ignorant heart. Hamza struck them down in a group walking under swinging ring-mail. That morning, Gabriel was his minister — and what a minister for the mounted warrior!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wooden bowls of black jujube wood used for communal feasts
the matted mane of a lion; a warrior's thick shield-mat
the shaft of a quivering, weakened spear
a great defender, one who deflects and repels threats
the weakling, the one who slacks and falls behind
the swirling, drifting dust of battle

context: One of Hassan's most celebrated elegies for Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle martyred at the Battle of Uhud (625 CE). The poem opens with the classical atlal prelude before pivoting sharply to elegy. Hassan praises Hamza as Islam's fortress, curses his killer Wahshi, and turns to taunt Hind bint Utba — who commissioned the killing and mutilated Hamza's body — reminding her that Hamza himself had killed her father Utba at Badr. The poem ends with Gabriel as Hamza's companion in battle. A masterwork of early Islamic ritha.

titleEn: Qurayza Met What Destroyed It
translationEn: Qurayza met what destroyed it — and utter humiliation descended upon its fortress. Sa'd had warned them sincerely that their God is a Lord Most Majestic. Yet they did not cease breaking the covenant until the Messenger campaigned against them in their very homes. Our ranks encircled their fortress — the heat of its blow ringing with the clash of swords. And the believers came to a home of eternity, where a deep, cool shade has been established for them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what destroyed it, what brought it to ruin and humiliation
Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, chief of the Aws, arbiter at Banu Qurayza
breaking the covenant, violating the treaty
the ringing, clanging sound of swords in battle
a deep, cool, dense shade — a Qur'anic phrase

context: A poem about the siege and defeat of Banu Qurayza, the Jewish tribe of Medina that broke their treaty during the Battle of the Trench (627 CE). Sa'd ibn Mu'adh served as arbiter and judged their punishment. Hassan briefly acknowledges Sa'd's prior counsel, describes the Muslim siege, and ends with a contrast between the believers' eternal reward and the tribe's humiliating fall.

titleEn: They Aided Their Prophet and Strengthened His Arm
translationEn: They aided their Prophet and strengthened his arm at Hunayn, on the day when the heroes let one another down.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they aided, they gave victory to
they strengthened his back; they fortified and supported him
the Battle of Hunayn (630 CE), after the conquest of Mecca
each relied on the other; let one another down

context: A single-verse encapsulation of the Ansar's steadfastness at the Battle of Hunayn (630 CE), where many newly converted Muslims fled the initial Hawazin ambush but the Ansar held firm around the Prophet. Such single-verse gems were preserved and memorized as stand-alone epigrams summarizing major events of the Sira.

titleEn: My Father Fears the Enemy's Heart
translationEn: My father strikes fear into the enemy's heart, and he knows that I am the true fortress. By your noble brother whom you boast of — you will not find me making excuses. I shall not be content this year to remain in their home, and I am not one who is misled or who retreats. By your father — I am not one whose heart is struck with panic on the day of battle, nor one who fights unarmed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: heart; those who strike fear; the inner seat of courage
the fortress, the refuge, the unbreakable defender
to make excuses, to be evasive
to be misled, to follow blindly
to turn back, to retreat in cowardice
one whose heart is hollowed with fear; a coward
one who fights unarmed, without weapons; defenseless

context: A short personal fakhr in which Hassan boasts of his valor and contrasts himself with an opponent who relies on his brother's noble reputation. He asserts he is the fortress in battle — unmovable, undistracted, and fully armed. The poem reflects the highly personal dimension of Arab fakhr poetry as individual self-assertion.

titleEn: I Accepted the Judgment of al-Marqal Qays

verses.sadr: ألا يا مال لا تزدد سفاها ،

translationEn: I accepted the judgment of al-Marqal Qays — I felt no bias when I appointed my maternal uncle as judge. He has a hand that flows with blood in battle and a hand whose generosity rivals the pouring of the north wind. We have been the arbiters in every matter from of old, building the nobility of exalted deeds. And among us there shall always be — as long as we live — a bright-faced man, white as the new crescent moon. O Mal — do not increase in folly! The decree of a noble man stands firm in his word.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who moves swiftly; epithet for a decisive mediator or warrior
judgment, arbitration, ruling in a dispute
the pouring of the north wind — symbol of abundant generosity
the arbiters, the judges, those who rule in disputes
bright-faced, radiant — a mark of noble character
firm, unwavering, consistent in his word

context: A poem defending Hassan's appointment of his maternal uncle as arbiter in a tribal dispute, praising the uncle's qualities of valor and generosity and asserting the Ansar's ancient tradition of just arbitration. The poem also rebukes a person named Mal who criticized the decision. The poem reflects the centrality of tribal arbitration (tahkim) in classical Arab social life.

titleEn: A Weighty Verse That Rang Through the Night

verses.sadr: يراها الذي لا ينطق الشعر عنده ،

translationEn: A weighty rhyme that rang through the night — I received it descending from the vault of the sky. Whoever lacks the gift of poetry sees it and is unable to produce its like. We are those who abandon the fraying tails of rights when they twist — we take the branches and harvest the roots. We speak only good, silent before all obscenity — noble men, giving the tribe whatever it desires.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a rhyme; the end-rhyme of a verse; by extension: a complete ode
rang out, echoed loudly, cried aloud
the atmosphere, the aerial vault of the sky
those who abandon; those who leave off the difficult
eloquent speakers; those who speak only good words
struck dumb, tongue-tied before obscenity and vulgarity

context: A closing poem of self-praise and poetic boasting in which Hassan presents his verse as divinely inspired — 'descended from the sky' — a classical Arab topos of poetic inspiration. He then transitions to collective fakhr, praising his tribe's skill in resolving disputes and their noble restraint from indecency. The four verses form a tightly constructed gem of self-referential poetry about poetry itself, a fitting close to this batch of the diwan.

titleEn: I Wept at the Death of Ja'far

verses.sadr: ولقد جزعت ، وقلت حين نعيت لي :

verses.ajuz: ع الخير بعد محمد ، لا شبهه

translationEn: I wept, for the death of Ja'far is heavy — the Prophet's beloved, above all of creation. I was stricken with grief and said when his death was announced to me: who now shall stand firm in battle at the Eagle's Pass in its shade, with bright swords drawn from their scabbards, on a day when spears rain down and clatter? After the son of the blessed Fatima — Ja'far — the best of all creation and its most majestic, the greatest loss, the noblest of all in lineage, the mightiest when wronged, and the most submissive to the truth when it arises, without wavering in falsehood. The most abundant in generosity, the least in obscenity, the most giving of bounty when asked — no human reckoned among the great of creation resembles him after Muhammad.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: was hard, was grievous, was difficult
I was grief-stricken, overcome with sorrow
was announced dead, the news of death was brought
is asked for bounty, is petitioned

context: An elegy (ritha') for Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's cousin who was killed at the Battle of Mu'ta (629 CE) while carrying the Muslim banner. Hassan eulogizes him as second only to Muhammad in nobility, combining personal grief with a catalogue of Ja'far's virtues in the tradition of classical Arabic elegy.

titleEn: He Upheld the Prophet's Covenant and Guidance

verses.ajuz: يصول ، إذا ما كان يوم محجل

verses.sadr: إذا كشفت عن ساقها الحرب حشها
له من رسول الله قربى قريبة ،
وليس يكون الدهر ما دام يذبل
verses.ajuz: فما مثله فيهم ، ولا كان قبله ،

translationEn: He upheld the covenant of the Prophet and his guidance — his intimate disciple — and word was made equal by deed. He stood firm on his path and his way, befriending the friend of truth, for truth is most just. He is the renowned knight and the hero who charges when a day of glory comes. When war lifts its skirt in earnest, he fills it with a white-bladed sword, swift to rush toward death. A man whose mother was Safiyya, of the house of Asad, is truly honored. He has close kinship with the Messenger of God, and from supporting Islam, an enduring glory. How many a crisis did al-Zubayr fend from the Chosen One with his sword — and God gives and gives abundantly. There is none like him among them, nor was there before him, nor shall there ever be as long as Mount Yathbul endures. Your praise surpasses the deeds of whole nations, and your actions, O son of the Hashimite woman, are finer still.
context: A panegyric (madih) for al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, one of the Prophet's closest companions and the son of Safiyya bint Abd al-Muttalib. Hassan praises his devotion to the Prophetic path, his valor in battle, and his kinship with the Prophet, ending with the superlative that his actions surpass the words of praise.

titleEn: Friends in Ease Are Many
translationEn: Friends in times of ease are many, but in times of trial they are few. Let not the friendship of those you take as brothers deceive you — for you will find no true companion when calamity strikes. Every brother says: I am loyal — yet he does not do what he says. Except the friend who has both lineage and faith: he is the one who truly acts on what he says.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: let it deceive you, mislead you
the one who truly acts, the doer

context: A short wisdom poem (hikma) on true friendship, contrasting fair-weather companions with the rare friend of noble character and sincere faith. The concluding verse on the man of lineage and religion acting on his word became a widely-cited proverb in Arabic literary tradition.

titleEn: I Knew You as a Chaste Believer

verses.sadr: وما قلت في مال تريدين أخذه ،

translationEn: I have known you — and God is the Reckoner — to be chaste, one of the believing women, free of malice. A woman of dignity and stability: her neighbor's guest is fed to fullness, while she herself goes hungry, abstaining from the flesh of the heedless. And you have never said, when wanting to take wealth: 'O son, hold on — I shall not do it,' feigning piety.
context: A short poem of praise (madih) directed at a believing woman, commending her chastity, generosity to guests, self-denial, and avoidance of backbiting. The reference to abstaining from 'the flesh of the heedless' echoes the Quranic metaphor in 49:12 for backbiting (ghibah).

titleEn: He Inherited Misguidance from His Father
translationEn: He has inherited misguidance from his father — a shameful day when the Messenger parted from him. You came to Muhammad with a rotted, crumbled bone, seeking to give him the lie — and you are ignorant of him. Banu al-Najjar have dealt their blow to Umayya — cry out for help, O Uqayl! And ruined are the two sons of Rabi'a when they obeyed Abu Jahl — may their mothers wail over them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: calls for help, cries for aid
a woman who loses her children, a bereft mother

context: A satirical poem (hija') attacking enemies of Islam who inherited their fathers' disbelief. The reference to 'a rotted bone' echoes the Quranic episode (36:78) where a polytheist brought a decayed bone to mock the Prophet's teaching of resurrection. Hassan names Abu Jahl and the sons of Rabi'a as examples of those ruined by following falsehood.

titleEn: When the Thaqafi Boasts Against You, Say:
translationEn: When the man of Thaqif boasts against you, say to him: come, recall the affair of Abu Righal. Your ancestor was the most shameful of all fathers of old, and you resemble him perfectly — as a model of baseness, well known to Ma'add. They are neither of pure Arab lineage nor of client status. Thaqif are the worst of all who ride beasts of burden, resembling weasels in battle. Even if the saddles of tamarisk wood could speak, they would say: Thaqif are the worst of those who sit upon them. The slave of al-Fazr bequeathed them to his sons and swore never to sell them for any price. They were not kept for honor's sake — rather, he intended for them the humiliation of endless nights.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: boasted against you, competed in pride
the most shameful, the most disgraced

context: A scathing satire (hija') against the tribe of Thaqif, citing their ancestor Abu Righal as a symbol of treachery — he guided Abraha's army toward Mecca and was buried in infamy. Hassan invokes this historical shame to demolish any Thaqifi pretense of nobility.

titleEn: Muzayna Came from Amq to Aid Them

verses.sadr: فكل شيء ، سوى أن تذكروا شرفا ،
verses.ajuz: أو تبلغوا حسبا من شأنكم جلل
جار ، وليس لهم في موطن بطل

translationEn: Muzayna came from Amq to aid their allies — shame on you, Muzayna, with the rope-braids in your backsides! Everything you do is a calamity, save that you might mention some honor or attain some lineage. A people of filthy habits — no neighbor walks in their precinct, and they have no hero in any settlement.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a valley or lowland region (place name)
woe to you! shame on you!

context: A short satirical poem (hija') against the tribe of Muzayna for joining the enemies of the Muslims. Hassan attacks their character, denying them any nobility, honor, or even the basic virtue of protecting a neighbor — the most valued social obligation in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia.

titleEn: Tell Ubayd That Boasting Is a Defect

verses.ajuz: في الصالحين ، فلا يذهب بك الجذل
عوفا وجمع بني النجار قد حفلوا
يفعل بكم أحد في الناس ما فعلوا

verses.sadr: إذ أنتم لا تجيبون المضاف ، وإذ

translationEn: Convey to Ubayd that boasting is a defect among righteous men — do not let exultation carry you away. When I saw the Banu Awf and their brothers — Awf and the assembled Banu al-Najjar — they had gathered in full force. A people who laid your strongholds open with their swords — no one among all people has done to you what they have done. While you refused to answer the call of one seeking protection, and a nubile maiden wandered exposed amid the houses.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gathered in force, assembled in numbers
laid open, made permissible (to attack), violated
a girl whose breasts are budding, a nubile maiden

context: A poem of satire and rebuke (hija') addressed to a man called Ubayd, warning against empty boasting. Hassan contrasts true martial valor — embodied by the Banu Awf and Banu al-Najjar — with the cowardice of those who failed to protect their guests and women.

titleEn: Banu Asad's Numbers Deserve No Fear
translationEn: Banu Asad have not multiplied to be feared for their numbers, nor is the lesser among them any good. A tribe that wavers and drifts within Ma'add — their noses are more abject than a beaten road. They wish to be affiliated with Quraysh — like a mule imagining itself resembles a stallion's neigh.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: their noses (metonymy for pride or honor)
the road, the path (walked upon by all)

context: A brief satirical poem (hija') against the tribe of Banu Asad, mocking their tribal instability, lack of pride, and pretension to Qurashi nobility. The closing image of the mule imitating a horse became a proverbial expression for those who claim a dignity they do not possess.

titleEn: His People Named Him Abu Hakam
translationEn: His clan named him Abu Hakam — Father of Wisdom — but God named him Abu Jahl — Father of Ignorance. He never comes on pilgrimage without the cauldron of his ignorance boiling over. It is as though what seethes within him makes him a proclaimer of obscenity and a flood of foolishness. He is goaded on by black-faced hounds of a biting woman, like wild beasts plunging into shallow water. His leadership has bequeathed to his people nothing but the wrath of God and the humiliation of their origins. If he wins a round his forehead bleeds; if he waits a moment he longs to load up and flee. The poets have come at me and turned back from me with a broken, bladeless arrow. The tongue-tied ones keep clear of me, as young camels are kept away from the stallion's covering. They fear Hassan's gift — like an evening storm that bursts with true and heavy rain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a broken arrow (with a notched or missing nock)
the tongue-tied ones, those silenced in debate

context: A satirical poem (hija') attacking Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, one of the most prominent enemies of the Prophet in Mecca. Hassan uses the contrast between his clan's flattering nickname (Abu Hakam, Father of Wisdom) and the Prophet's epithet for him (Abu Jahl, Father of Ignorance) as the poem's central irony. Hassan also asserts his own supremacy over rival poets.

titleEn: Thaqif Has Always Been Base

verses.ajuz: لئيما ، إذا ما نص للمجد معقل
على ما بكم من لؤمكم متعزل
ثقيف ، فإن القصد في ذاك أجمل

verses.sadr: فإنكم إن ترغبوا لا يكن لكم
verses.ajuz: عن أصلكم في جذم قيس معول

verses.sadr: وما لكم في خندف من ولادة ،
verses.ajuz: ولا في قديم الخير مجد مؤثل

translationEn: Thaqif — acknowledge it — has always been base when the stronghold of glory is measured. Lower your gaze, for glory and its people are aloof from you on account of your meanness. Leave Ma'add and any claim of kinship to them — they are truly distant and far removed from you. Set aside foolish talk and aim straight for your father Thaqif — for that is the fairer course. If you aspire otherwise, you will find no support in leaving your origins within the stock of Qays. You have no birth-right in Khindif and no deep-rooted glory in any ancient good.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: raised to the highest point, examined closely
a place to slide away to, a distance
origin, root, stock of a tribe

context: A sustained satire (hija') against Thaqif continuing from earlier poems. Hassan urges them to abandon their pretensions to noble Arab genealogy and accept their low origins. The poem denies them any share in the prestige of Ma'add, Khindif, or Qays — the great ancestral groupings of the Arabs.

titleEn: On the Day of Badr We Met You with Reinforcement
translationEn: On the day of Badr we met you, and we had reinforcement — so victory was lifted up by Mika'il and Jibril.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Badr (site of the first major Muslim battle, 624 CE)

context: A single-verse poem (bayt mufrad) commemorating the Battle of Badr (624 CE), where the Muslims' victory was attributed to divine support through the angels Gabriel and Michael. This verse succinctly encapsulates the theological meaning Hassan attached to Muslim military triumph.

titleEn: Baseness Surpasses All of Thaqif

verses.sadr: إن هم أقاموا حل فوق رقابهم ،
قوم إذا ما صيح في حجراتهم

translationEn: Baseness itself is nobler in lineage than all of Thaqif combined — and whatever a base person does, they do the same. The King has built over them a house of shame that stands upon them and shall not be moved. If they stay, it rests on their necks forever; and if they move, it moves with them. A people who, when a cry goes up in their quarters, are met only by wretched, stunted cowards.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: their necks (symbol of submission or burden)
a cry was raised, an alarm was sounded
the wretched, the contemptible, the lowest
short and stunted, diminutive in stature

context: Another satire (hija') against Thaqif, combining cosmic imagery — God building a permanent house of shame over them — with scorn for their cowardice in battle. The image of shame as an immovable dwelling that travels with its owners is one of Hassan's most memorable satirical conceits.

titleEn: How Badly Khaybar Fought to Defend Its Wealth
translationEn: How badly the people of Khaybar fought to defend what they had gathered of farms and date-palms! They hated death, so their protected sanctuary was violated — and they behaved as the vile and the abject behave. Do you fear death? For the death of wasting and humiliation is no beautiful end.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Khaybar (Jewish fortress north of Medina, conquered 628 CE)
their protected sanctuary, their inviolable zone
you fear, you are in dread of

context: A poem of boasting (fakhr) celebrating the Muslim conquest of Khaybar (628 CE). Hassan taunts the defenders for their failure to fight effectively, implying that a shameful survival is worse than an honorable death in battle — a classic topos of early Arabic war poetry.

titleEn: You Are Not of the Noblest Company

verses.ajuz: ن لا عبد شمس ولا نوفل

verses.sadr: فلو كنت من هاشم في الصمي

translationEn: You are not of the noblest company — neither Abd Shams nor Nawfal. Your father was not the water-provider of the Hajj pilgrims, so sit down upon the most ignoble of lineages. Rather, you are a bastard attached to them, like an iron ring fastened to a camel-litter. Your lineages boil up with baseness like the cartilage bubbling in a cauldron. Had you truly been of the core of Hashim, you would not have mocked us — while your watering-hole warms over a fire.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the water-provider for the pilgrims (a noble Meccan office)
of mixed or low birth, a bastard, one of impure lineage
the core, the pure heart, the innermost

context: A satire (hija') against a man of disputed or low lineage who claims affiliation with the noble Meccan clans of Abd Shams, Nawfal, or Hashim. Hassan denies him any such honor, using the image of bubbling cartilage in a cauldron as a metaphor for his base character seething to the surface.

titleAr: لك الخير غضي اللوم عني فإنني
titleEn: Spare Me Your Blame, for I Love the Finest Character

verses.sadr: لك الخير غضي اللوم عني فإنني
verses.ajuz: أحب من الأخلاق ما كان أجملا

verses.sadr: فإن كنت لا مني ، ولا من خليقتي ،
verses.ajuz: فمنك الذي أمسى عن الخير أعزلا

verses.sadr: ألم تعلمي أني أرى البخل سبة ،
إذا انصرفت نفسي عن الشيء مرة ،
وإني ، إذا ما الهم ضاف قريته

verses.ajuz: على السيف لم تعدل عن السيف معدلا

verses.sadr: إذا انبعثت من مبرك غادرت به

verses.ajuz: رأيت لها من روعة القلب أفكا
ولا عاجزا في الحرب جبسا مغفلا

verses.sadr: إذا ما انتدى أجنى الندى ، وابتنى العلا ،
verses.ajuz: وألفي ذا طول على من تطولا
وإن كان أندى من سوانا ، وأحولا

verses.sadr: نطيع فعال الشيخ منا ، إذا سما
verses.ajuz: لأمر ، ولا نعيا ، إذا الأمر أعضلا
وإن كان منا حازم الرأي حولا

verses.sadr: وما ذاك إلا أننا جعلت لنا
فنحن الذرى من نسل آدم والعرى ،
وإنك لن تلقى من الناس معشرا
وأكثر أن تلقى ، إذا ما أتيتهم ،

verses.ajuz: إذا ما دعا داع إلى الموت أرقلا
تفرغ في حوض من الصخر انجلا

verses.sadr: له غلل في ظل كل حديقة

verses.ajuz: له بالسيوف ميل من كان أميلا

verses.sadr: وإنك لن تلقى لنا من معنف ،
وإلا أمرأ قد ناله من سيوفنا
فمن يأتنا أو يلقنا عن جناية

translationEn: May all good be yours — restrain your blame of me, for I love from all of character what is most beautiful. Leave me to my knowledge of affairs and my nature, for my fortune has never brought ill omen upon you. If you are not of my making or my character, then what has turned away from good comes from you. Did you not know that I consider miserliness a disgrace, and that I hate the two-faced and the fickle? Once my soul has turned away from something, I shall never return to it for the rest of time. When worry descends upon my encampment — I send it away with a swift she-camel, pacing in the evenings, a massive beast. Rounded and rhythmically striding — were she loaded upon a sword she would not deviate from the sword's path. When she rises from her resting place she leaves behind offspring like dried raisins, shriveled. When she kneels she settles on her calluses, as if a sharp rocky ridge lay across her chest. Fierce by nature — were a locust to chirp behind her, you would see her heart startled in fright. We are a people who do not elevate the treacherous, nor the shirker who hides behind loads at the time of obligations, nor the one who withholds wealth from what befalls him, nor the coward in war who is dull and helpless. We elevate among us every accomplished grey-haired man, radiant of brow, crowned with dignity. When he attends a gathering he brings forth bounty and builds glory, and is found one of long-lasting generosity toward those who aspire. You will not find among us a youth being elevated — even if he is more generous and shrewder than others. We follow the deeds of the elder among us when he rises to a matter, and we do not falter when a matter becomes difficult. He has an advantage in his judgment and his deeds, even if another among us is equally resolute. That is only because we have placed our elders first in all that is good. We are the peaks of Adam's progeny and its high ground — glory has settled among us until it took root. He built a house and its pillars rested upon us, so that mankind was powerless to move it. You will never find among people a group mightier than the Ansar in might, or more excellent. When you come to them, you shall most often find among them a great and generous chief of vast bounty. And a white-haired elder of blessed character, whose intervention is sought for the highest stakes, and a hoped-for youth. And a beardless youth who sets off willingly — when you send him he carries what he is charged with, and prospers. And a skilled orator whose reply cannot be answered, and a man of discernment with refined poetry. And a noble warrior who rises eagerly to the sword when a caller calls to death, and sets off at a gallop. And a graceful young man walking with pride, trailing his robe — lavish in generosity, open-handed, not to be blamed. We have a noble land encircled by its mountains, in which glory has built its home and settled. In it are palms and strongholds through which streams run, rising at times over flat rock and stones. When a stream's water is cut off, we replenish it with a new stream fed by bucket-camels, over great wide-mouthed wells, deep-socketed, pouring into a stone basin hewn clear. It has water-flow in the shade of every orchard, keeping pace with a rushing torrent of flowing water. When you come to it you find in its quarters tall lean horses and abundant grazing flocks. We have made our swords and spears a cave and fortress for it against armies and Bedouin alike. When they gathered a host against us, we rose toward them with Indian swords fed with slow-acting poison. With them we aided the best of all creation as an imam, and we revered the revealed Book. We aided, we gave shelter, and we were a people who struck with swords to straighten whoever was crooked among his followers. You will never find among us one who is harsh or blameworthy — except some base, misguided wretch. Or a man upon whom our swords have landed a blow, who now leans to one side, disarmed. Whoever comes to us or meets us after a wrong — he finds with us a noble dwelling and a refuge. We give protection, so our neighbor fears no sudden harm, and he finds wealth in our homes and grows prosperous.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Helpers of Medina, Muhammad's hosts
camels used to draw water from wells
a refuge, a place of safety

context: One of Hassan's major qasidas of fakhr (tribal boasting), addressed to a woman who blamed him. The poem moves through personal ethics, camel description, tribal codes of leadership, and a vivid account of Medina's landscape and the Ansar's role in sheltering and defending the Prophet. It stands as one of the fullest expressions of Ansari pride in the diwan.

titleEn: Are You Unmoved by the Ruins of Dwellings?

verses.sadr: ديار زهاها الله لم يعتلج بها
وإني إذا ما قلت قولا فعلته ،
ومن مكرهي إن شئت أن لا أقوله ،

translationEn: Are you truly unmoved by the traces of the dwellings — a palace of kings above the land of the water-chains? The Pleiades rain down their bounty upon it, and it received a cold that strips the roots of the low ground. When the camping marks of the tribe yielded their produce — grapes hanging over a tall established vine-frame. Dwellings that God made flourish — no shepherds of sheep circled around them from behind the streams. Whatever may be said of me, I am no liar, and I am no betrayer of the trustworthy in whose company I sit. When I say a word I act upon it, and I turn away from what my heart does not intend. And who could compel me, if I wished not to speak? And betraying the trustworthy is a character unworthy of praise.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you are stirred, you are moved (emotionally)
traces of the dwellings, ruins of the campsite
camping marks, latrine areas of the tribe's encampment
made it flourish, made it glitter, beautified it
traitor, one who betrays a trust

context: A qasida that opens with the traditional ruins prelude (nasib) before pivoting to self-praise (fakhr), asserting Hassan's personal integrity: his honesty, his fidelity to trust, and his principle of speaking only what he intends to do. The Pleiades reference places the ruins in the rainy season, a standard elegiac setting.

titleEn: O Sons of Hamas, Is There None Noble Among You?

verses.sadr: فاللؤم حل على الحماس ، فما لهم

translationEn: O sons of Hamas! Is there not a single noble among you? For manliness in Hamas is scarce. Woe to your mothers and woe to your fathers — a woe that echoes and wails among you! You mocked Hassan at the height of his fire — long is the folly for those whom Hamas has borne. Satire is easy against you — so be on your guard, for the abject remain abject. Do not grieve at being attributed to your father, for baseness endures while mountains crumble. The men of Ziyad did not sire you with their stallions, and the stallion of Banu Sala'a is otherwise engaged. A hornless, scabby he-goat prowled among you — and there is no escape from your ignominy. Baseness has settled upon Hamas — they have no elder who leads, and no spirited youth among them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: easy matter, something handled with ease
be on your guard, brace yourselves

context: A fierce satire (hija') against the tribe of Hamas who dared to mock Hassan. Hassan responds with a full-scale attack on their lineage, manliness, and leadership, denying them any noble ancestor and ending with the devastating verdict that they have no worthy elder or youth. The poem is notable for its sustained use of genealogical insult.

titleAr: إذا قال لم يترك مقالا لقائل ،
titleEn: When He Speaks He Leaves No Room for Others

verses.sadr: إذا قال لم يترك مقالا لقائل ،
كفى وشفى ما في النفوس ، فلم يدع
verses.ajuz: لذي إربة ، في القول ، جدا ولا هزلا
فنلت ذراها لا دنيا ، ولا وغلا

translationEn: When he speaks, he leaves no word for any other speaker — with gathered pearls of speech between which you see no gap. He sufficed and healed what was in souls, leaving no scope for any man of purpose, in speech, whether serious or jest. You ascended to the heights without effort, and reached their summits — neither falling short nor pushing through by force.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a gap, a break, a division
by forcing one's way, pushing through (as an intruder)

context: A short poem of praise (madih) for an unnamed eloquent speaker who leaves no topic unaddressed and no soul unhealed by his words. The three verses form a compact portrait of ideal eloquence — comprehensive, effortless, and transcendent — qualities Hassan valued both in the Prophet and in great orators.

titleAr: لا تعد من رجلا أحلك بغضه
titleEn: Do Not Count One Whose Hatred Has Cast You Here

verses.sadr: لا تعد من رجلا أحلك بغضه

translationEn: Do not count yourself alongside a man whose hatred has cast you into Najran, in a mean and clipped existence. Your shaft was tested in battle and found a hollow pipe, full of splits and cracks. The wrath of God is upon al-Zuba'ri and his son, and a wretched punishment abides them in this life.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: has placed you, has cast you down
a hollow pipe or tube, something empty inside
Ibn al-Zuba'ri, a Meccan poet hostile to Islam

context: A satire (hija') directed at Ibn al-Zuba'ri, a poet of Quraysh who fiercely satirized the Muslims. Hassan accuses him of hollow bravado — his spear-shaft a cracked empty tube — and calls down divine punishment upon him and his son. Najran, a Christian region in southern Arabia, may refer to exile or displacement.

titleEn: A Maiden Enslaved Your Heart in a Dream

verses.sadr: بيدي أغر ، إذا انتمى لم يخزه

verses.ajuz: من ولد شجع غير جد كرام

translationEn: A maiden enslaved your heart in a dream, giving the sleeping man to drink from a cool, smiling mouth. Like musk blended with cloud-water — or an aged wine red as the blood of a slaughtered beast. Her bag full and rounded, her waist compact, simple and unhurried in her rhythms. Built upon a smooth spineless base, her ample haunches when she sits like a marble grinding-stone. She almost grows lazy about coming to her bed — so languid is her softness and beautiful her form. By day I never cease to think of her; at night my dreams divide me over her. I swore to forget her and abandon her memory — until my bones are hidden in the grave. Who is there for a blaming woman who blames in foolishness — I have indeed disobeyed my blamers in following passion. She came to me at dawn, after slumber, with the approach of life's changing events. She claimed that a man is distressed by his day when he is cut off from a group of tents. If you have lied in what you told me — then may you escape as al-Harith ibn Hisham escaped: he abandoned his loved ones instead of fighting in their defense, and fled with the head of a swift mare and her bridle. A lean, swift horse tearing through the dust like a wolf in a shadowy cloud. She outstrips tall noble steeds on the open plain, the ground worn smooth by hooves and scattered stones. He filled both flanks with her — and was exhausted — while his loved ones met the worst of fates. His father's sons and kinsmen were in the battle in which God gave victory to the people of Islam. Had it not been for God and her galloping, we would have left him as prey for the beasts and crushed him with hooves. They were ground down as God executes His command — a war whose blaze is stoked with fire. From every prisoner whose bonds are tightened — a hawk, when he meets the regiment, a defender. And one prostrated who answers no call until the tall peaks are moved. With shame and manifest humiliation when they saw the white blades driving every bold warrior. In the hands of a radiant man whose lineage, when he names it, brings no shame upon him — a chief, a bold striker. White blades that when they meet iron ring out like lightning beneath the shadows of every cloud. They are not like Ya'mar when spears interlace and horses bound beneath every dust-cloud. So you have fouled yourself — you are of treacherous company, those who defecate when battle comes — the base. Leave all nobility: your people are a clan from the seed of Shuja', not truly noble. From the loins of Khindif of noble roots, a complete and bright woman bore one with him. One in whom spears sway in ranks, like a well-calf kept from his paternal uncles.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: full and rounded in the buttocks

context: One of Hassan's longest and most structurally complex qasidas, beginning with a ghazal prelude of dream-imagery and erotic description before pivoting sharply into war poetry and satire. The transition via al-Harith ibn Hisham's cowardly flight at Badr links the love theme to the battlefield. The poem ends with fierce hija' against cowardly enemies, showcasing Hassan's mastery of the classical multi-register qasida form.

titleEn: By Your Life, Your Kinship with Quraysh Is False
translationEn: By your life, your kinship with Quraysh is like the kinship of a camel calf to the chick of an ostrich. If you trace yourself back to Quraysh, you are like a she-camel stuffed with a dummy fawn, wandering aimlessly. You are attached to them as a bastard, like leather straps fastened to anklets. Do not boast of a people you are not from, and do not behave like the ignoble among the sons of Hashim.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the chick of an ostrich (proverbially unrelated)
a she-camel whose calf died, stuffed with a dummy to trick her

context: A satirical poem (hija') attacking a man who falsely claims Qurashi ancestry. Hassan uses two devastating animal metaphors: the camel calf and the ostrich chick have no biological connection, just as this man has no real kinship with Quraysh; and the she-camel deceived by a stuffed dummy, suggesting his claim is a hollow trick.

titleEn: Would That Khubaib Had Not Been Betrayed
translationEn: Would that Khubaib's trust had not betrayed him, and would that Khubaib had known those people well. Zuhayr ibn al-Agharr and Jami' bought him — and they had long been accustomed to riding into forbidden deeds. You granted protection, then when you granted it you betrayed it — and you were devouring greedily on the outskirts of al-Raji'.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Khubaib ibn Adi, a Companion martyred by betrayal
they bought him, they purchased him
you betrayed, you broke the covenant

context: An elegy (ritha') lamenting Khubaib ibn Adi al-Ansari, a companion of the Prophet who was captured and sold to the Quraysh after a group betrayed their guarantee of safe conduct near the well of al-Raji' (625 CE). Khubaib was crucified in Mecca. Hassan mourns him and curses his betrayers.

titleEn: When You See Two Shepherds with Sheep

verses.ajuz: من بطن عمق ذي الجليل والسلم

translationEn: When you see two little shepherds among sheep — two contemptible cubs swearing by Nuhm — with scraps of divided meat between them from the belly of the valley of al-Jalil and the acacia.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: two small worthless cubs, contemptible little ones
Nuhm, a deity or place sworn by (used here derisively)

context: A brief satirical sketch (hija') using the image of two petty shepherds quarreling over scraps of meat as a metaphor for the low status and trivial nature of the poem's targets. The oath by Nuhm (a non-Islamic deity) marks them as pagans of the lowest social rank.

titleEn: Did You Not Ask the New Campsite to Speak?

verses.sadr: أبى رسم دار الحي أن يتكلما ،
verses.ajuz: وهل ينطق المعروف من كان أبكما

verses.sadr: بقاع نقيع الجزع من بطن يلبن ،
أقامت به بالصيف ، حتى بدا لها
verses.ajuz: نشاص ، إذا هبت له الريح أرزما

verses.sadr: وقد أل من أعضاده ، ودنا له

verses.ajuz: إذا استن ، في حافاته البرق ، أثجما

verses.sadr: سأهدي لها في كل عام قصيدة ،

verses.ajuz: لذي العرف ذا مال كثير ومعدما
ولم أك عضا في الندامى ملوما

verses.sadr: متى ما تزنا من معد بعصبة ،

verses.ajuz: فأكرم بنا خلال وأكرم بنا ابنما

verses.sadr: نسود ذا المال القليل ، إذا بدت
وإنا لنقري الضيف ، إن جاء طارقا ،
verses.ajuz: من الشحم ، ما أمسى صحيحا مسلما

verses.sadr: ألسنا نرد الكبش عن طية الهوى ،
وكائن ترى من سيد ذي مهانة
أبى فعلنا المعروف أن ننطق الخنا ،

translationEn: Did you not ask the new campsite to speak — at the watercourse of Ashdakh, then Buraqa the dark? The trace of the tribe's dwelling refused to speak — and does the dumb ever give voice to what is known? In the flat wet ground of al-Jaz' in the valley of Yalban, whose people moved off heading toward Tihama. Dwellings of the tender-hearted Sha'tha' and her companion, in the nights when they camped at al-Mirad and then moved on. When she with the dark-rimmed eyes grazed at the valley's outflow among well-ordered arak trees. She stayed there through the summer until a storm cloud appeared, growling when the wind blew upon it. Its sides had grown massive and its middle hung close to the earth, then it thundered. The she-camels with young lowed within it as lightning crackled at its flanks and fell heavily. It almost brought down by the banks of al-Aqiq — with its great mass — a rounded rocky corner. When it climbed Turban and its rain poured down, it collapsed and dropped its full weight in a crash. Every water-channel and gully by morning had its flood overturning thorny trees without ceasing. They called out by night, and their loaded camels arose, raising the embroidered patterned saddle-cloths. They arched their necks like gazelles, and the borders of the Qutri cloaks showed their finely patterned weave. How will you meet her when her people have settled in a southern valley — of Ghifar and Aslam? A meeting far off, a separation of destinies — you will only meet her when a season comes round. I shall dedicate to her a qasida every year, and sit well-provided-for in Yathrib, honored. Am I not the best of neighbors, keeping his house open to the man of standing — the wealthy and the destitute alike? And a true boon-companion whose hand pours freely, flowing with bounty in the evenings, generous. I have strengthened my pillar through him, and he matched my character — I was never a biting one among companions, to be blamed. The bitter experience of wars has left us swords, coats of mail, and a massive assembled host. When the sky's horizons darken with dust and drought, as if it wore a striped, arrow-marked garment. You would think the great cauldrons around our homes were dark pack-horses standing fasting in the camp. Uninvited guests stay by them as if they were coming to a sea from Sumayha, full to the brim. We have a teeming settled community and nomadic kin like the peaks of Radwa — in might and honor. Whenever we weigh against Ma'add with a band of warriors, and Ghassan, we defend our watering-place from being destroyed. With every youth of bare knuckles worn bare by the clash of warriors, oozing musk and blood. When the sun is behind us our backs gleam, as if the inner veins were dripping at that moment. We have sired the sons of al-Anqa' and the two sons of Muhariq — how noble are we in character, how noble the sons we bear! We elevate a man of little wealth when his manliness appears among us — even if he is destitute. We entertain the guest when he comes by night with fat, for as long as he remains sound and in peace. Do we not turn back the ram from following his desire, and break the shafts of spears? How many a lord of humble descent do you see — his father is as our father, and a sister's son, and a forbidden kin. Ours are the gleaming white bowls glittering in the morning sun, and our swords drip with blood from valor. Our practice of good has prevented us from uttering obscenity — our speaker speaks only with what is proper. Our standing with kings and our defending, and the filling of tamarisk bowls to the point of cracking, have prevented disgrace. Every branch of Ma'add we have repaid for their deeds — woe for woe, and bounty for bounty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the campsite, the dwelling (of a tribe)
growled, thundered, made a deep sound
uninvited guests, those who come without an invitation
the gleaming white bowls (symbol of generosity)
the Phoenix, a legendary bird; here an ancestor name

context: One of Hassan's grandest qasidas (54 verses), combining the traditional nasib (longing for a departed beloved) with an extended storm description, personal praise, tribal boasting (fakhr), and celebration of the Ansar's lineage. The poem's description of the storm cloud is one of the most elaborate in classical Arabic poetry, and the boast about the gleaming white bowls became a locus classicus of Ansari generosity.

titleEn: Those Are My People — If You Ask

verses.ajuz: ل ، والزحف من خلفهم قد دهم
م لم ينب عنها ، ولم ينثلم

verses.sadr: فما إن من الناس إلا لنا

translationEn: Those are my people — if you ask — generous when a guest arrives one day. Their cauldrons are great for their feast-sacrificers, and they load into them the fat-humped aged camel. They share with their clients in wealth and protect their neighbor when he is wronged. They were kings in their own land, dealing out death in fury at any oppressive command. Kings over the people — never on any day of their time did they rule like the dissolution of an oath. They drove out 'Ad and its followers, Thamud, and some remnants of Iram. In Yathrib they built fortresses among the date-palms and stabled livestock within them. The water-hauling camels that the Jews trained — come, draw near, and be told: and in what they desired of pressed harvest wine, and a soft life at others' expense. They marched out against them loaded with their gear, on every noble unbroken stallion. With fine horses at their flanks, covered in thick leather trappings. When they couched near the two hills of Sirar and tightened the saddles on worn girths — nothing alarmed them but the rushing of the horses, as the marching host swept down upon them from behind. They flew in scattered rout, terrified, and we flew at them like the lions of the thicket. Upon every tall mare in prime condition who does not tire of long effort. And every bay horse, swift of heart, sound of hoof-tips, like a divination arrow. Upon them riders who have repeatedly met the clash of warriors and the striking of the black-armored. Lions when they are angered in war — they do not hold back but press forward. We returned with their chiefs and their women by force, and their wealth was divided. We inherited their dwellings after them and were kings therein, never removed. Then when the King's Messenger came to us with light and truth after the darkness, we leaned toward him and did not disobey him — on the morning he came to us from the land of the Sacred Precinct. We said: you have spoken truth, O Messenger of the King — come to us and settle among us. We bear witness that you are, before the King, truly sent with a righteous faith. So proclaim what you used to keep hidden — proclaim it openly, do not conceal it. For we and our children are a shield — we protect you, and in our wealth, take command. We are your guardians when they have denied you — so call out, and feel no shame. The depraved ones flew with their followers toward him, thinking he would be cut off. We stood before him with our swords, fighting off the oppressors of nations on his behalf. With every polished blade of eager swiftness — thin of edge, deep-biting, keen. When it meets the hard dense bones it does not rebound and is not notched. That is what the eras have bequeathed to us — an ancient glory and a high-held might. When one generation passes its offspring suffices, inheriting when the former breaks. There is none among people but that we have over him — even if he falls short — the bounty of favor.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: great cauldrons (symbol of generosity and feeding guests)
those who play the maysir game; here feast-sacrificers

context: A long fakhr qasida (51 verses) in which Hassan celebrates the Ansar's pre-Islamic glory, their conquest of Yathrib, and then their pivotal role in welcoming and defending the Prophet. The poem traces a seamless arc from tribal pride to Islamic devotion, making it one of the most comprehensive accounts of Ansari identity in classical Arabic poetry.

titleEn: Sorrows Prevented Sleep at Evening

verses.sadr: يا لقوم هل يقتل المرء مثلي
لو يدب الحولي من ولد الذ
وأنا الصقر عند باب ابن سلمى ،

verses.ajuz: كل دار فيها أب لي عظيم

translationEn: Sorrows prevented sleep at evening — and a vision when the stars sink low. From a beloved from whom your heart received a sickness, hidden within. O people, can a man like me be killed — weak in power and in bones, wearied? Her concern is perfume and the bed, and upon her gleam silver and strung pearls. Were a year-old cricket from the young of the locusts to crawl upon her, its claws would leave wounds. The sun of day surpasses her in nothing — except that youth does not endure. My maternal uncle is the orator of the Jabiya assembly in al-Jawlan when he rises before al-Nu'man. My father, in Sumayha, was the decisive speaker on the day when opposing parties gathered against him. And I am the hawk at the gate of Ibn Salma on the day Nu'man sat in chains. My father and a delegating elder set me free, and then we left — their lock shattered. I pledged both hands on their behalf — every palm bearing a share of the cut. My lineage placed me at the very top of their ranks — in every house there is a great father of mine. How many a forbearance has been wasted by lack of wealth, and how many a foolishness covered over by comfort. I do not care whether a he-goat bleats in the rocky land or a base man abuses me in secret. Those are our deeds, while the deed of al-Zuba'ri is obscure among his friends and blameworthy. I claim the valor from among you, when you appeared — a clan from the very core of Banu Qusayy. Nine of them bore the standard and flew into a thicket of spears — from Makhzum. They did not turn back until they were all destroyed in one stand, and each of them is blamed. With rich blood — and it was out of fierce loyalty that they stood firm, for a noble man is noble. They stood until they were visited by their tribes' death — with spears shattering in their throats. And Quraysh sought shelter from us in refuge — they did not hold firm, and their forbearances grew light. Their young men could not bear to carry the standard — for it is only the illustrious who carry the standard.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wearied, one who grows tired and restless
out of fierce loyalty, out of protective honor
were visited by death (lit. 'visited by tribes')

context: A rich multi-part qasida opening with a ghazal prelude of sleepless love before pivoting to genealogical pride and war boasting. Hassan celebrates his uncle's eloquence at the court of al-Nu'man ibn al-Mundhir (the Lakhmid king), his own role in freeing prisoners, and the Ansar's valor contrasted with the Qurayshi standard-bearers who fell at Badr. The poem weaves together pre-Islamic aristocratic prestige with Islamic military glory.

titleEn: What Stirred Hassan: Ruins and a Vision

verses.ajuz: من بيت رأس عتقت في الخيام

verses.sadr: كأسا ، إذا ما الشيخ والى بها

translationEn: What stirred Hassan was the traces of the encampment — the tribe's departing, the erecting of tents, and the drainage ditch whose embankments had crumbled away with time, in a valley of Tihama. The tale-bearers have achieved what they aimed for — and the rope binding him to Sha'tha' is frayed and worn. A spirit-maiden whose phantom kept him sleepless — she departs at dawn yet appears in dreams. Is she anything but a nursing doe, dwelling among the lote-trees on the slopes of Buram? She drives along a fawn with languid glance, with short steps and a faint cry. Her mouth is like a cool pool in stones, beneath the shade of clouds. Mixed with a reddish wine of fierce potency, from Bayt Ra's, aged in tents. The wine-shop aged it for years upon years — we drink it pure and blended, then we sing in houses of marble. It creeps through the body creeping, as locusts creep through thin, powdery soil. A cup — when an old man drinks five of them in a row — dresses him in a young man's cloak. From the wine of Baysan, chosen by me — an antidote that leaves the bones limp. Served by a red-faced man in a hood, with a well-shaped neck, tightly belted. Alert, quick to answer the call — not turned back by occupation, light in rising. Leave her memory and devote yourself to a strong she-camel — massive, of inexhaustible energy. Flowing in gait, prancing, plunging its head down to one side in the slack of its reins. You would think her mad and unruly, when the mirage licks the hilltops. My people are Banu al-Najjar — when a grey-white storm came hurling dust-clouds at its people. We never abandon our neighbor, never surrender our client, never accept defeat in a dispute. Among us is one whose good deed is praised, and who relieves distress on the day of pressing need.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the drainage trench around a tent, a camp ditch
a small pool, a cool water-pool in rocks

context: A qasida that moves through the classic stations of the pre-Islamic ode: the ruins and the beloved's departure, the erotic ghazal, the wine-song, the camel journey, and the tribal boast. The poem is notable for its sensory richness — the cool pool mouth, the creeping wine, the mirage on the hills — and its ending in Ansari tribal pride, showing Hassan's mastery of the full qasida form.

titleAr: هل المجد إلا السودد العود والندى ،
titleEn: Is Glory Anything But Ancient Lordship and Generosity?

verses.sadr: هل المجد إلا السودد العود والندى ،

verses.ajuz: على أنف راض من معد وراغم
ولدنا نبي الخير من آل هاشم

verses.sadr: لنا الملك في الإشراك ، والسبق في الهدى
بني دارم لا تفخروا ، إن فخركم

verses.ajuz: لنا خول من بين ظئر وخادم

verses.sadr: وأفضل ما نلتم من المجد والعلى

translationEn: Is glory anything but ancient lordship, generosity, standing with kings, and bearing great burdens? We aided and sheltered the Prophet Muhammad — to the pride of the willing and the indignation of the reluctant from Ma'add. A fierce tribe whose origins and protection lie at Jabiyat al-Jawlan amid the non-Arabs. We defended him when he settled among our tents, with our swords, against every aggressor and wrongdoer. We placed our sons and daughters as his shield, and we were content to give up the spoils of war for his sake. We struck the people until they followed one after another upon his faith, with sharp cutting blades. We gave birth to the greatest of Quraysh — we bore the Prophet of good from the house of Hashim. Ours is kingship in the Age of Paganism and precedence in guidance, the aiding of the Prophet, and the building of noble deeds. O sons of Darim, do not boast — for your boasting turns to a burden when noble deeds are recalled. Bereft are your mothers! You boast against us while you are our servants — some as wet-nurses, some as slaves. If you came to spare your blood and your wealth from being divided in shares, then set no equal beside God and submit — and do not dress in the manner of non-Arabs. Otherwise we shall make you permitted spoil and drive your women away — with the gathering of spears and the hard-hooved chargers. The best of what you have attained of glory and eminence came from following us at the approach of the sacred seasons.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fierce, isolated, cut off from others
his protection, what he guards jealously

context: A major fakhr qasida celebrating the Ansar's pre-Islamic and Islamic glory, addressing first the general virtue of lordship and generosity, then specifically honoring their role in sheltering the Prophet. The poem pivots to sharp satire against Banu Darim (of Tamim) who dared to boast against the Ansar, threatening them with sword and submission if they persist.

titleEn: Weep — Your Eyes Have Wept and Then Rushed Forth
translationEn: Weep — your eyes have wept and then rushed forth with blood that soaks their corners in steady streams. Why did you weep over those who passed away one after another — why did you not recall the noble deeds of the people? Remember among us one who is glorious and full of resolve — noble of character, magnificent in courage. I mean the Prophet, the brother of generosity and bounty, and the most righteous of those who swear oaths. For one like him, and for what he calls to, praise was never blunted or dull.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: steady streams of tears, flowing continuously
one of high resolve and ambition
blunt, dull, ineffective (of a sword or praise)

context: A short elegy (ritha') turning into prophetic praise (madih nabawi). Hassan redirects grief over the fallen toward remembrance of the Prophet, presenting him as the supreme model of noble resolve, generosity, and righteousness. The closing image of praise being 'not blunted' likens Hassan's own verse to a sharp sword — his characteristic self-image.

titleAr: ما بال عينك ، يا حسان ، لم تنم ،
titleEn: Why Do Your Eyes Not Sleep

verses.sadr: ما بال عينك ، يا حسان ، لم تنم ،
verses.ajuz: ما إن تغمض ، إلا مؤثم القسم

verses.sadr: لم أحسب الشمس تبدو بالعشاء ، فقد
لقد حلفت ، ولم تحلف على كذب ،
verses.ajuz: يابن الفريعة ، ما كلفت من أمم

translationEn: What ails your eyes, O Hassan, that they do not sleep — they cannot close except while swearing a guilty oath? I never thought the sun would appear at evening, yet I have met a sun that illuminates the darkest night. She is the finest of women, and the finest of her people is her father — people of majesty and loyal to their pledges. You have indeed sworn — and it was no false oath — O son of al-Furai'a, what a burden you have taken upon you from the people.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who commits a sin, guilty
the finest, the summit, the branch

context: A short ghazal in which Hassan, addressing himself, marvels at a woman so radiant she is compared to a sun appearing at nightfall. The poem blends self-reproach with admiration for a woman of noble family.

titleAr: ألين ، إذا لان العشير ، فإن تكن
titleEn: I Am Gentle When Companions Are Gentle

verses.sadr: ألين ، إذا لان العشير ، فإن تكن
إذا مات منا سيد ساد مثله ،

translationEn: I am gentle when my companion is gentle, but if he has a madness, my madness came before his. He is close, his evil coming before his good — when they demand the blood-price of me, I pay it. When one of our lords dies, another like him rises to lead — broad-armed in the mastery of leadership, a seasoned chief. He answers the call in great calamities and attends every battle — a trusted man who grows in goodness and is honored.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the battle, the clamor of war

context: A brief fakhr poem extolling the poet's clan — their readiness to pay blood-price, their unbroken chain of leadership, and their valor in battle. Each verse encapsulates a distinct tribal virtue.

titleEn: Chosroes Struck Me with Misery

verses.ajuz: نعم ثم لم تنطق ، ولم تتكلم

translationEn: Chosroes reached me with his wretchedness, though between us lay the plateaus of al-Saman and al-Mutathallam. He bereaved me — may God not grant him success in his affair — of my father, a generous man of little frowning. Let the waters of the two Harithids be effaced — their waters have already been effaced by every great tribe. His watering-place at Hadhar grew desolate after its people departed, though it used to draw water into jars and clay pitchers. I addressed the spring at al-Juba: farewell! It replied yes, then fell silent and did not speak. Dwellings of kings — I saw them in prosperity when the pillar of kingship had not yet crumbled. By my life, a garden between rocky ground and sand-dunes, at a gentle slope where its streams rose above every ravine, — by every lofty edifice and a gathering of revelers with a cup of pure wine not cut short — is dearer to Hassan, if he could reach it, than the dancing-songs of Ghifar and Aslam.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bereaved me, struck me with grief

context: An elegy lamenting the poet's father, with the desolation of royal seats as a backdrop. Hassan blames the Persian king Chosroes obliquely for the bereavement, then turns to nostalgic images of the Ghassanid royal courts.

titleEn: God Honored Us with His Prophet's Victory
translationEn: God honored us with the victory of His Prophet — through us He established the pillars of Islam. Through us He gave might to His Prophet and His Book, and He gave us might through striking and boldness. In every battlefield our swords fly, splitting skulls from the crests of helmets. Gabriel visits us in our homes bringing the duties of Islam and its rulings. He recites to us the Light therein, firmly ordained — a vow, by your life, unlike any other vow. So we are the first to make lawful what He made lawful, and to forbid for God's sake all He forbade. We are the chosen of all creation, its order and the bridle of every bridle. We wade into the floods of every death and guarantee the chances of the days. We who bind together the strands of affairs by our resolve, and who unravel the twisted ropes of nations. Ask Abu Karib, ask Tuba'a about us, and the people of sacrifice and divining-arrows. Ask those of sound minds about their noblemen on the day of al-'Uhayn, then Hajir, then Rua'm. We protect those we wish to protect and bestow generosity upon those who visit us in need. Our swords turn back the healthy survivors of armies, and we protect the neck of the proudest mighty chieftain. The blows of our swords and our spears have not ceased on every day of struggle and combat — until we left the earth's rough ground strung with our cavalry in ordered ranks. A band fled who had gone too far; had they stood firm, they would not have returned then in safety. If I boast of them through the glory of their ancient deeds — it is the boast of the wise man over the nations.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the mighty chief, the great leader
bands of men, groups without a tribe

context: A major qasida of Islamic fakhr-madih in which Hassan extols the Ansar's role in establishing Islam, receiving Gabriel's revelation, and their unmatched valor in battle. The poem invokes pre-Islamic Yemeni kings (Tuba'a) as witnesses to the tribe's ancient glory.

titleAr: إن ابن جفنة من بقية معشر
titleEn: The Son of Jafna, Remnant of a Noble Kin

verses.sadr: إن ابن جفنة من بقية معشر
لم ينسني بالشام ، إذ هو ربها ،

translationEn: The son of Jafna is of the remnant of a people whose fathers never fed them on blame. He did not forget me in Syria when he was its lord — not at all, nor did he leave me to seek aid from the Romans. He gives abundantly and regards his gift as no more than the paltry gift of one who is criticized. I visited him one day and he drew me close to his seat and gave me the finest drink from the spring.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: remnant, remainder, the best that remains
the pure wine, the finest drink

context: A short panegyric in praise of a Ghassanid king, likely one of the Banu Jafna kings of Syria, whom Hassan visited and received generous hospitality from. The poem praises his generosity and his loyalty to the poet.

titleEn: Whose Is This Faded Dwelling?

verses.sadr: خلاء المبادي ما به غير ركد
وقد كان ذا أهل كبير وغبطة ،
verses.ajuz: إذا الحبل ، حبل الوصل ، لم يتصرم
وإذ مضى من عيشنا لم يصرم

verses.sadr: ضعيف العرى دان من الأرض بركه
فإن تك ليلى قد نأتك ديارها ،
فما حبلها بالرث عندي ، ولا الذي
لعمر أبيك الخير ما ضاع سركم
ولا ضقت ذرعا بالهوى إذ ضمنته ،
ولا كان مما كان مما تقولوا

verses.ajuz: ذوي العلم عنا كي تنبي فتعلمي

verses.sadr: نبيح حمى ذي العز حين نكيده ،
ونحن إذا لم يبرم الناس أمرهم ،
verses.ajuz: نكون على أمر من الحق مبرم

verses.sadr: ونحن إذا ما الحرب حل صرارها ،
ولم يرج إلا كل أروع ماجد ،
فنحن كذاك ، الدهر ، ما هبت الصبا
فلو فهموا ، أو وفقوا رشد أمرهم ،
إنا إذا ما الأفق أمسى كأنما

translationEn: Whose is this faded dwelling whose traces are like the embroidered patterns of fine Sabiri linen? Its campsites are empty, with nothing but three still pools like sitting doves, and a split-trunk tree standing as an ally of decay, and remains like finely-ground dust. Summer winds moisten its withered dry grass upon a standing hulk like a ruined watering-trough. Garments of ruin clothed it after its time, and a dark cloud passed by with torrential crashing rain. Once it held a great household and prosperity when the rope — the rope of union — had not yet been severed. When we were many neighbors there in joy, when a portion of our life had not yet been cut short. Every hastening cloud whose fringes burst open, when the fertilizing winds drive it, pours down. Weak at its fringes, its belly near the earth, gliding like a dark, silent mountain. If Layla's abode has moved far from you and she withheld from the needs of your enamored heart — and she resolved to sever the bond after its union and hearkened to the words of the jealous pretender — my bond with her is not worn away by distance, though she speaks no word. Her love, were she to entrust me with its union — even if friends severed ties — would not be cut off from me. By your father's goodness, your secret has not been wasted in my keeping, that you should repay me with distance and severance. I was not weighed down by love when I harbored it, nor did hidden speech crowd my breast. What they said of me and spread abroad was nothing but unfounded conjecture. If you believe what you hear, then ask the men of knowledge about us so you may be told and learn. Whenever you ask about us you will be told we are noble people of ancient glory. We are the foremost hawks, bold strikers, shouldering a lance whose spine was never marred. By your life, no seeker of generosity comes to our lands for us to turn him away empty and wronged. Nor is the mighty lord, when he plots against us, forbidden to face our spears. Our guest at the time of hospitality is not turned away, nor is our neighbor abandoned in calamities. We open the sanctuary of the mighty man when we plot against him, and we guard our own sanctuaries with straight lances. When people fail to settle their affairs, we are on a matter firmly resolved in truth. Were Mount Radwa weighed against the forbearance of our nobles, our forbearance and Mount Yalamlam would tip the scales. When war unties its rope and bestows upon the milch-camels death and blood — and only every bold, noble man is hoped for, strong of sinew, possessed of glory and dignity — we are the bridle of the two commanders leading to battle when the cowardly trembler does not advance. So we are thus through all time, as long as the east wind blows — we return upon the ignorant with forbearance. If they understood or were guided to the right course of their affairs, we would return to them after hardship with greater bounty. When the horizon at evening seems as if on its two sides the color of dragon's blood reddens — we feed in winter and thrust with spears when war returns like a raging fire. At our tents when we are called upon you find gatherings with every turbaned elder — exalted in the tent's pillar, shielding his honor from blame, of auspicious character, a seasoned man. Generous in all conditions with a wide courtyard — when asked for kindness he does not frown. One who strikes the arrow-butts when it is winter and rushes swiftly to the caller of battle, resolute. Proud-nosed, long-armed, a noble chief, practiced in the clash of armored forearms, bearing battle-scars.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: traces, ruins, remnants of a dwelling
the jealous enemy, one who hides enmity
dragon's blood resin, deep red color

context: One of Hassan's longest qasidas, opening with the traditional atlal (ruined dwelling) motif and ghazal for Layla, then pivoting into a lengthy fakhr extolling the tribe's generosity, forbearance, valor, and nobility. The poem spans 37 verses encompassing classical Arabic poetic themes.

titleAr: أعين ، ألا أبكي سيد الناس ، واسفحي
titleEn: O Eyes, Weep for the Master of Men
